topics.title: IoT Architecture - What Is IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Architecture - What Is IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and other technologies for the purpose of connecting and exchanging data with other devices and systems over the internet. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, IoT is formally described as an interconnected ecosystem where uniquely identifiable embedded computing devices are seamlessly integrated within the existing internet infrastructure to enable advanced services and automation.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (PCADT602 - Module 1):**
> *The Internet of Things (IoT) is the inter-networking of physical devices, vehicles (also referred to as "connected devices" and "smart devices"), buildings, and other items embedded with electronics, software, sensors, actuators, and network connectivity which enable these objects to collect and exchange data.*

### Key Terminology Decoded

- **"Things"**: Any physical object that can be assigned an IP address and provided with the ability to transfer data over a network. This ranges from household appliances to industrial machinery.
- **"Internet"**: The global system of interconnected computer networks that uses the standard Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP) to link devices worldwide.
- **"Network Connectivity"**: The medium (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, Cellular) that allows the "thing" to communicate.
- **"Sensors & Actuators"**: Sensors **sense** the environment (temperature, motion, pressure), while actuators **act** upon it (turning on a motor, opening a valve).

## Conceptual Analogy: IoT as the "Human Nervous System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "IoT is the Nervous System of a Smart City"**
> 
> Imagine a human body:
> - **Sensors in the skin** → IoT Sensors (detect temperature, pressure, touch)
> - **Nerves** → Network Connectivity (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G) carrying signals
> - **Spinal Cord / Brain** → Cloud / Data Processing Center (analyzes signals)
> - **Muscles (responding to brain)** → Actuators (perform physical actions like switching on AC)
> - **The body itself** → The physical "Thing" (car, fridge, building)
> 
> Just as your nervous system works **without conscious thought** (reflexes), IoT systems often operate **autonomously** — your smart AC turns on *before* you feel hot, just like your hand pulls away from fire *before* you think.

### Core Characteristics of IoT (KTU Board-Ready Bullet Points)

1. **Connectivity**: Devices connect to each other and to the internet (using **Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, LoRaWAN, Cellular IoT**).
2. **Intelligence & Identity**: Each device has a **unique identifier** (IP/MAC address) and embedded intelligence.
3. **Dynamic & Self-Adapting**: IoT systems adapt to changing context and environment (e.g., traffic lights adjusting based on congestion).
4. **Heterogeneity**: Different hardware and network platforms coexist in the same IoT ecosystem.
5. **Safety & Security**: Data privacy and device authentication are paramount — a KTU-favorite 14-mark topic.
6. **Sensing**: Real-world data is captured via sensors.
7. **Enormous Scale**: Billions of devices simultaneously generating data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — KTU 2024):**
> The exam frequently tests the difference between **IoT vs. M2M (Machine-to-Machine) communication** and the **evolution from RFID → IoT**. Remember: *M2M is a subset of IoT, but IoT extends M2M by adding internet, cloud, analytics, and human interaction layers.*

---

## Why IoT Matters: The Five Core Value Propositions

| Value Driver | Real-World Impact | KTU Exam Weight |
|--------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Convenience** | Smart homes (Alexa, Google Home) | Frequently asked (3 marks) |
| **Efficiency** | Industrial IoT reduces energy waste by **20–30%** | High-yield 14-mark topic |
| **Safety** | Wearables detecting falls in elderly patients | Conceptual question |
| **Data-Driven Decisions** | Precision agriculture using soil sensors | Application-level |
| **Automation** | Self-driving cars, smart grids | High-priority 14-mark |

### Real-World Example: The Smart Refrigerator

- **Sensor** detects you are low on milk.
- **Connectivity** (Wi-Fi) sends data to the cloud.
- **Intelligence** (cloud analytics) processes the data and identifies nearby stores.
- **Actuator / Interface** displays the shopping list on your phone or auto-orders via Amazon.
- This entire chain is **autonomous**, **connected**, and **intelligent** — the three pillars of IoT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Whenever an exam question asks *"Explain IoT with an example"*, always structure your answer using the **Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Act** framework. Examiners award full marks when this 4-stage flow is explicitly written.

---

## IoT vs. Traditional Internet: The Paradigm Shift

The traditional internet is **Human-to-Human (H2H)** communication. IoT shifts this to **Thing-to-Thing (T2T)**, **Thing-to-Human (T2H)**, and **Machine-to-Machine (M2M)** communication. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes this transition as a foundational concept for Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The 4-Stage IoT Operational Flow (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Act)

The complete IoT architecture can be deconstructed into **four logical stages**. Understanding these stages is critical because KTU Module 1 exam questions are frequently built around them.

### Stage 1: Sensing (Perception Layer)

- The physical "things" are equipped with **sensors** that capture analog/digital data from the surrounding environment.
- Common sensor types: temperature, humidity, proximity, accelerometer, gyroscope, gas, IR, ultrasonic.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)** transform continuous sensor signals into discrete digital values usable by microcontrollers.
- **Engineering insight**: Sensor accuracy is bounded by **precision**, **range**, and **sampling rate** — these directly affect the quality of IoT data.

### Stage 2: Transmit (Network / Connectivity Layer)

- The digitized data is routed through communication protocols.
- Choice of protocol depends on **range, bandwidth, and power** constraints.
- **Short-Range**: Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), ZigBee, NFC, RFID.
- **Long-Range**: LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M.
- **High-Bandwidth**: Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), 5G.

### Stage 3: Analyze (Processing / Cloud / Edge Layer)

- Raw data is aggregated at gateways and pushed to cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).
- **Edge computing** processes time-critical data locally to reduce latency.
- **Big Data analytics, Machine Learning, and AI** extract actionable insights.

### Stage 4: Act (Application / Actuator Layer)

- The processed insight triggers an **actuator** (motor, relay, LED, valve) or a **user notification** (SMS, app alert).
- This closes the **control loop**, making the system autonomous and self-correcting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often test whether students can correctly map a real-world application (e.g., *smart agriculture*) to these four stages. A complete, well-labeled answer fetches the full 14 marks.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet (Module 1)

| **Concept / Metric** | **Definition / Formula** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **KTU Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Rate (Throughput)** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Payload Data}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | bits per second (bps) | Calculating bandwidth for IoT transmission |
| **Latency** | $L = t_{\text{receive}} - t_{\text{transmit}}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Edge vs Cloud tradeoff |
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Sensor data acquisition |
| **Nyquist Rate** | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing in IoT sensors |
| **Power Consumption (Tx)** | $P_{\text{Tx}} = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) | Battery-life estimation |
| **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | Wireless link quality |
| **Number of Connected Devices** | $N_{\text{IoT}} = 10^{10}$ to $10^{12}$ (projected) | devices | 5G massive IoT context |
| **Payload Size** | $L_{\text{payload}} = n_{\text{fields}} \times b_{\text{field}}$ | bytes | MQTT / CoAP packet design |
| **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{\text{Tx}}}{R_b}$ | Joules per bit (J/bit) | Energy-efficient IoT protocol design |
| **Coverage Range** | $R \propto \sqrt{P_{\text{Tx}} \cdot G}$ | meters (m) | LoRa vs Wi-Fi coverage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Formula Sheet:** The above formulas represent the **physical-layer and link-layer foundations** that IoT inherits from digital communication systems. While Module 1 is conceptual, KTU examiners often embed one numerical problem (e.g., calculate latency or throughput) within a 14-mark question to test applied understanding.

---

## Engineering Utility: Where This Knowledge Is Applied

| Industry | IoT Application | Core Architecture Stage Used |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| **Healthcare** | Remote patient monitoring, smart pills | Sensing + Transmit |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic management, waste management | Transmit + Analyze |
| **Agriculture** | Precision farming, smart irrigation | All 4 stages |
| **Manufacturing (IIoT)** | Predictive maintenance, robotics | All 4 stages |
| **Retail** | Smart shelves, inventory tracking | Sensing + Analyze |
| **Energy** | Smart grid, smart meters | All 4 stages |

---

## The IoT Definition — A Layered View

IoT can also be conceptualized as a **5-layer reference architecture** (per the IoT World Forum model, which is KTU-recommended reading):

1. **Perception Layer** → Sensors, actuators, RFID
2. **Network Layer** → Wi-Fi, 5G, Ethernet, gateways
3. **Processing Layer** → Cloud, edge, data centers
4. **Application Layer** → Smart home, smart health, smart industry
5. **Business Layer** → ROI, strategy, regulatory compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (Module 1):** A 14-mark question frequently asks: *"Explain the 5-layer IoT architecture with a neat diagram."* Always draw the diagram **with all 5 layers labeled**, give **2 examples** of components per layer, and conclude with a **real-world use case** spanning all layers.

---

## Key Differences: IoT vs. M2M vs. CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems)

| Parameter | **IoT** | **M2M** | **CPS** |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Communication** | Internet-based | Point-to-point / Cellular | Bidirectional physical-digital |
| **Intelligence** | Cloud + Edge analytics | Limited / Rule-based | Deep control algorithms |
| **Scope** | Global, heterogeneous | Local, homogeneous | Embedded, real-time |
| **Data Volume** | Big data, analytics | Small, telemetry | Real-time control signals |
| **Example** | Smart city dashboard | A vending machine reporting stock | Autonomous vehicle ECU |
| **H2H Support** | Yes (apps, dashboards) | No | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often answer "IoT = M2M". This is **incomplete**. The correct KTU-grade answer: *M2M is a predecessor of IoT, and IoT extends M2M by adding IP-based internet connectivity, cloud analytics, and human interaction layers.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Conceptual Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodological Note for Students:** Since Module 1 of PCADT602 is **conceptual and architectural** (not heavily numerical), this section will provide **rigorous, step-by-step logical analysis** of IoT definitions, evolution, and architectural transitions. Each conceptual step is decomposed at the same depth as a mathematical derivation, ensuring KTU-level analytical completeness.

---

## Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivation: From "Internet" to "Internet of Things"

### Step 1 — Defining the Baseline: Classical Internet

The classical internet is a **network of networks** that interconnects computing devices using the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. Communication is fundamentally **Human-to-Human (H2H)** — humans generate, consume, and exchange data.

$$
\text{Internet} = \{\text{Devices}\} \cup \{\text{TCP/IP}\} \cup \{\text{H2H Communication}\}
$$

### Step 2 — Introduction of Embedded Devices

With the rise of **microcontrollers (MCUs)** and **System-on-Chips (SoCs)** in the late 1990s and early 2000s, physical devices gained the ability to compute. This introduced the **"Smart Object"** — a thing with embedded intelligence.

$$
\text{Smart Object} = \text{Physical Object} + \text{Sensor/Actuator} + \text{MCU} + \text{Connectivity}
$$

### Step 3 — Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Communication

Smart objects began communicating with each other **without human intervention**. This was the birth of **M2M** — point-to-point, often cellular-based, telemetry.

$$
\text{M2M} = \{\text{Smart Object}_1\} \leftrightarrow \{\text{Smart Object}_2\}
$$

**Limitation of M2M:** Isolated, low-bandwidth, rule-based, and lacking global IP addressing.

### Step 4 — Convergence with the Internet

The convergence of M2M with **IPv6** (which provides $2^{128}$ unique addresses — enough to assign one to every atom on Earth's surface) enabled **global addressing** of devices.

$$
N_{\text{IPv6 addresses}} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ addresses}
$$

### Step 5 — Birth of the "Internet of Things"

IoT = M2M + Internet Protocol + Cloud + Analytics + Human Interaction

$$
\text{IoT} = \text{M2M} \cup \{\text{IPv6 Addressing}\} \cup \{\text{Cloud Computing}\} \cup \{\text{Data Analytics}\}
$$

### Step 6 — The Modern IoT Ecosystem

Today's IoT incorporates:
- **Edge AI** (on-device intelligence)
- **Digital Twins** (virtual replicas of physical things)
- **Federated Learning** (privacy-preserving distributed ML)
- **5G/6G URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)
- **Blockchain** (decentralized trust)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** IoT is not a single technology but a **convergence paradigm**. It is the synthesis of sensing, communication, computation, and intelligence. This is the **single most important conceptual framework** KTU examiners test in Module 1.

---

## Analytical Matrix: IoT Architectural Components

The following matrix maps **each component** to its **layer**, **function**, and **engineering example**. This is the gold-standard reference for KTU 14-mark answers.

| **Component** | **Layer** | **Primary Function** | **Engineering Example** | **Communication Protocol** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature Sensor (DHT22)** | Perception | Sense ambient temperature | Cold-chain monitoring | Analog / I²C |
| **Accelerometer (MPU6050)** | Perception | Detect motion / tilt | Wearable fitness tracker | I²C / SPI |
| **RFID Tag** | Perception | Identify objects uniquely | Supply chain inventory | ISO 14443 |
| **Microcontroller (ESP32)** | Edge / Network | Process & transmit data | Smart agriculture node | Wi-Fi / BLE |
| **Gateway (Raspberry Pi)** | Network | Aggregate & forward data | Industrial IoT gateway | MQTT broker |
| **Cloud Platform (AWS IoT)** | Processing | Store, analyze, visualize | Smart energy dashboard | HTTPS / MQTT |
| **Actuator (Relay)** | Application | Switch electrical loads | Smart lighting | GPIO / ZigBee |
| **Dashboard (Mobile App)** | Application | User interface & alerts | Patient health app | REST API |
| **AI/ML Model** | Processing | Predictive analytics | Anomaly detection | gRPC / Kafka |

---

## Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Mapping "Smart Home" to IoT Architecture

Let us apply the **5-layer IoT architecture** to a real-world **Smart Home Lighting System** — this is the exact type of example KTU examiners use to test conceptual understanding.

**Step 1 (Perception Layer):** A **PIR motion sensor** detects human movement in the room.

**Step 2 (Perception Layer):** A **light-dependent resistor (LDR)** measures ambient brightness.

**Step 3 (Edge Processing):** The **ESP32 microcontroller** reads both sensor values via GPIO/ADC.

**Step 4 (Decision Logic):** The ESP32 applies a rule:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Turn ON Light} & \text{if } (\text{Motion} = 1) \land (\text{LDR} < \text{threshold}) \\ \text{Turn OFF Light} & \text{if } (\text{Motion} = 0) \text{ for } t > t_{\text{timeout}} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 (Network Layer):** The ESP32 publishes the event to an **MQTT broker** hosted on AWS IoT Core.

**Step 6 (Processing Layer):** AWS Lambda function is triggered, updates the **DynamoDB** database, and runs a **machine learning model** to learn user preferences.

**Step 7 (Application Layer):** The **Blynk mobile app** receives a notification: *"Living room light turned ON at 19:42 — 25% of daily budget used."*

**Step 8 (Business Layer):** The energy company analyzes aggregated data to optimize **time-of-use pricing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** This 8-step mapping is worth **7 of the 14 marks**. The remaining 7 marks come from the **diagram + 2-layer component description + conclusion**. Always structure 14-mark answers in this **8 + 6** format.

---

## Analytical Comparison: RFID vs. IoT

| Parameter | **RFID (Pre-IoT)** | **IoT (Modern)** |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Data Storage** | Small tag ID (96–128 bits) | Large payload (KB–MB) |
| **Read Range** | Up to **10 m** (passive) | Global (via internet) |
| **Power Source** | Passive (energy harvesting) | Active (battery/grid) |
| **Communication** | One-way (tag → reader) | Bidirectional (thing ↔ cloud) |
| **Data Processing** | At reader only | At edge + cloud |
| **Decision Making** | Manual (human reads tag) | Autonomous (ML-driven) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** RFID is the **technological ancestor** of IoT. KTU Module 1 explicitly traces the evolution: *Barcode (1970s) → RFID (1980s) → M2M (2000s) → IoT (2010s) → AIoT (2020s)*. Memorize this timeline for a guaranteed 3-mark question.

---

## Why the IoT Concept is Revolutionary: The "Three Pillars" Derivation

IoT's revolutionary nature can be derived from three converging pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Pervasive Connectivity (Network)**
$$
\text{Connectivity} = \text{Wi-Fi} \cup \text{5G} \cup \text{LoRa} \cup \text{BLE} \cup \dots
$$

**Pillar 2 — Embedded Intelligence (Compute)**
$$
\text{Intelligence} = \text{MCU} + \text{Cloud} + \text{AI/ML}
$$

**Pillar 3 — Real-Time Sensing & Actuation (Physical)**
$$
\text{Physicality} = \text{Sensors} + \text{Actuators} + \text{Energy}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{IoT} = \text{Pillar 1} \cap \text{Pillar 2} \cap \text{Pillar 3}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If any one pillar is absent, the system is NOT IoT.** A webcam streaming video to a PC is **surveillance**, not IoT (no sensors). A temperature sensor with no internet is **data logging**, not IoT (no connectivity). This **three-pillar intersection** is a KTU-favorite 14-mark question.

---

## End-of-Section Synthesis

The deep analysis confirms that IoT is best understood as a **convergence paradigm** built on **sensing, connectivity, computation, and intelligence**. The architectural layers (perception, network, processing, application, business) provide a **universal analytical framework** applicable to **every IoT question** in the KTU Module 1 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid diagrams are used to render the **block-level functional architecture** of IoT systems. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric naming rule (e.g., `node1`, `layerA`) to avoid Mermaid compilation errors.

---

## Diagram 1: 5-Layer IoT Reference Architecture

This diagram maps the **IoT World Forum 5-layer reference model** — the most frequently asked 14-mark diagram in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layerA["LAYER 5: BUSINESS LAYER<br/>Strategy, ROI, Regulation"]
    layerB["LAYER 4: APPLICATION LAYER<br/>Smart Home, Smart Health, Smart City"]
    layerC["LAYER 3: PROCESSING LAYER<br/>Cloud, Edge, AI/ML, Big Data"]
    layerD["LAYER 2: NETWORK LAYER<br/>Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, MQTT, Gateway"]
    layerE["LAYER 1: PERCEPTION LAYER<br/>Sensors, Actuators, RFID Tags"]

    layerA --> layerB
    layerB --> layerC
    layerC --> layerD
    layerD --> layerE

    layerE -.SENSE.-> layerD
    layerD -.TRANSMIT.-> layerC
    layerC -.ANALYZE.-> layerB
    layerB -.ACT.-> layerA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to draw this in the exam:**
> 1. Draw 5 horizontal boxes stacked vertically.
> 2. Label each box clearly with layer name + 2 component examples.
> 3. Draw **upward arrows** (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Act → Business).
> 4. Add a one-line example per layer in brackets.

---

## Diagram 2: The 4-Stage IoT Operational Flow (Sense → Transmit → Analyze → Act)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PHYSICAL WORLD<br/>Temperature, Motion, Humidity"]
    node2["SENSOR LAYER<br/>DHT22, PIR, LDR, MPU6050"]
    node3["EDGE DEVICE<br/>ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi"]
    node4["NETWORK<br/>Wi-Fi, 5G, MQTT, LoRa"]
    node5["CLOUD PLATFORM<br/>AWS IoT, Azure, Google Cloud"]
    node6["ANALYTICS ENGINE<br/>AI/ML, Big Data Processing"]
    node7["ACTUATOR / DASHBOARD<br/>Relay, Motor, Mobile App, SMS"]
    node8["USER / ENVIRONMENT<br/>Real-World Action"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 -.Feedback.-> node1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** The **feedback loop** (from `node8` back to `node1`) is what makes IoT **closed-loop** and **autonomous**. Examiners often award 2 extra marks for explicitly drawing this feedback arrow.

---

## Diagram 3: IoT Component Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subgraph1["PERCEPTION & EDGE"]
        a1["Sensors"]
        a2["Actuators"]
        a3["Microcontroller Unit"]
        a1 --> a3
        a3 --> a2
    end

    subgraph subgraph2["CONNECTIVITY"]
        b1["Gateway / Router"]
        b2["Wireless Protocols"]
        b3["Wired Protocols"]
        b2 --> b1
        b3 --> b1
    end

    subgraph subgraph3["CLOUD & ANALYTICS"]
        c1["MQTT Broker"]
        c2["Data Lake"]
        c3["ML/AI Engine"]
        c1 --> c2
        c2 --> c3
    end

    subgraph subgraph4["APPLICATION"]
        d1["Web Dashboard"]
        d2["Mobile App"]
        d3["Alert System"]
    end

    subgraph subgraph5["BUSINESS"]
        e1["Analytics & Reporting"]
        e2["Decision Support System"]
    end

    subgraph1 --> subgraph2
    subgraph2 --> subgraph3
    subgraph3 --> subgraph4
    subgraph4 --> subgraph5
```

---

## Diagram 4: Evolution from Internet → IoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["PHASE 1<br/>Classical Internet<br/>H2H Communication<br/>Email, Web"]
    phase2["PHASE 2<br/>M2M Era<br/>Telemetry, SCADA<br/>Point-to-Point"]
    phase3["PHASE 3<br/>IoT Emergence<br/>IPv6 + Cloud + Sensors<br/>2010 Onwards"]
    phase4["PHASE 4<br/>AIoT and Edge AI<br/>Autonomous Systems<br/>2020 Onwards"]

    phase1 -->|"+ Smart Objects"| phase2
    phase2 -->|"+ Internet + Cloud"| phase3
    phase3 -->|"+ AI/ML + Edge"| phase4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** This evolution diagram frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question. Always state **which technology was added in each transition** — this single detail fetches 2 extra marks.

---

## Diagram 5: Smart Home IoT Architecture (Applied Case Study)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    home1["Smart Bulb"]
    home2["Motion Sensor"]
    home3["Door Lock"]
    home4["Thermostat"]

    hub["Smart Home Hub<br/>Google Home / Alexa"]

    cloud1["Cloud AI Engine<br/>Voice Recognition + Automation"]

    user1["User Smartphone App"]

    home1 --> hub
    home2 --> hub
    home3 --> hub
    home4 --> hub

    hub -->|"Wi-Fi/BLE"| cloud1
    cloud1 -->|"Internet"| user1
    user1 -.Voice/App Control.-> cloud1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this diagram** when answering: *"Explain IoT with a smart home example"* (a 14-mark KTU question). The 5 nodes cover perception, network, processing, application, and business layers in one integrated view.

---

## Visual Summary Table: All 5 Diagrams at a Glance
