- ⭐ **Analytics hierarchy** — Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (ascending intelligence).
- ⭐ Key formulas: $B = N \cdot S \cdot f$, $L_{total} = L_{edge} + L_{fog} + L_{cloud}$, $V = R \cdot t$.
- ⭐ Always **convert bytes to bits** (multiply by 8) when computing bandwidth in bps/Mbps.
- ⭐ **M/M/1 queue stability**: $\mu > \lambda$ (service rate must exceed arrival rate).
- ⭐ Edge data reduction (90% filtering) can yield **10× bandwidth savings** — a frequently tested numerical.
- ⭐ **Polyglot persistence** = using multiple database types for different data characteristics.
- ⭐ The cloud is **NOT** always the answer for IoT — placing every workload there violates latency and bandwidth constraints.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — IoT Data Management and Compute Stack**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The 'Things' in IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** The 'Things' in IoT # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, the term **"Things"** refers to any physical object, device, or entity in the real world that is uniquely identifiable, capable of being connected to a network (typically the Internet), and possesses the ability to **sense, collect, process, transmit, and/or actuate** data without requiring explicit human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCADT602)** and the **ITU-T Y.2060 Recommendation**, a "Thing" in IoT is defined as: > *"An object of the physical world (physical things) or the information world (virtual things) which can be identified and integrated into communication networks."* These "Things" extend connectivity beyond traditional computing devices (laptops, smartphones) to include everyday objects, industrial equipment, vehicles, wearable gadgets, and even living organisms equipped with embedded sensors and actuators. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A "Thing" is *not* just a sensor. It is the **complete physical or virtual entity** that becomes part of the IoT ecosystem. Every Thing has at least three core capabilities: **Identity**, **Connectivity**, and **Data Generation/Actuation**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **modern hospital patient monitoring system** as an analogy. In a traditional hospital, a nurse manually checks each patient's vitals (pulse, temperature, blood pressure) every few hours, writing them down on a chart. This is slow, error-prone, and limited. Now imagine attaching a **small wearable device** to each patient's wrist. This device: - **Identifies** the patient (unique ID). - **Senses** heart rate, body temperature, and oxygen saturation. - **Sends** this data wirelessly to a central monitor every second. - **Triggers an alarm** (actuation) if readings cross a dangerous threshold. The wearable device is a **"Thing"** in IoT. The patient doesn't need to manually push data; the Thing does it autonomously. Now scale this up: imagine **billions** of such devices — on cars, in farms, in homes, on bridges, inside factories — all talking to each other and to cloud servers. **That collective network of smart objects is the Internet of Things.** In simple words: **If it has a sensor or actuator, a unique identity, and an internet connection — it can be a "Thing" in IoT.** ## 1.3 Key Physical Identifiers and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics and identifiers are universally applied to "Things" in IoT: - **Unique Identity**: **IPv6 address** or **UID** (typically a **128-bit** identifier). - **Power Consumption**: As low as **micro-watts (µW)** for energy-harvesting devices. - **Data Rate**: From **100 bps** (passive RFID) to **several Gbps** (IoT gateways). - **Operating Frequency Bands**: **Sub-1 GHz** (LoRa, Zigbee), **2.4 GHz** (BLE, Wi-Fi), **5 GHz** (Wi-Fi). - **Communication Range**: From **< 0.1 m** (NFC) to **> 10 km** (LoRaWAN, Sigfox). > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to define "Things" in IoT, always mention the **three essential properties**: (1) Unique Identification, (2) Sensing/Actuation Capability, and (3) Network Connectivity. Examiners allocate marks for these three keywords. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Characteristics of IoT Things ## 2.1 Fundamental Characteristics of "Things" in IoT The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes the characteristics of IoT Things under the following structured framework: ### 2.1.1 Identity and Naming Every Thing must be **uniquely identifiable** within the IoT network. This is achieved through: - **IP Addressing**: Each Thing is assigned a unique **IPv6 address** (128-bit), enabling a theoretical addressing capacity of **3.4 × 10³⁸** unique devices. - **Electronic Product Code (EPC)**: Used in RFID-based IoT for object identification. - **URI / UUID**: Uniform Resource Identifiers or Universally Unique Identifiers for non-IP devices. ### 2.1.2 Sensing and Actuation Capabilities A Thing in IoT must interact with the physical world through: - **Sensors**: Devices that **detect and measure** physical phenomena and convert them into electrical signals. - Examples: Temperature sensor (LM35, DHT22), Accelerometer (ADXL345), Light sensor (BH1750). - **Actuators**: Devices that **convert electrical signals into physical action**. - Examples: Servo motors, Solenoid valves, Relays, LED arrays. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinction for KTU Exam:** A **sensor** *observes* the environment; an **actuator** *changes* the environment. Many exam questions test this distinction. ### 2.1.3 Communication and Connectivity Things communicate using a variety of wired and wireless protocols: - **Short Range**: NFC, RFID, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Zigbee, Z-Wave. - **Medium Range**: Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac), BLE Mesh, Thread. - **Long Range**: LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M, Satellite IoT. ### 2.1.4 Processing and Intelligence Modern IoT Things contain **microcontrollers (MCUs)** or **microprocessors (MPUs)** that perform local data processing: - **MCUs**: Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 8-bit, 16 MHz), ESP8266, ESP32, STM32. - **Edge Intelligence**: On-device machine learning inference (e.g., TensorFlow Lite Micro). ### 2.1.5 Power and Energy Autonomy Things are often deployed in remote or inaccessible locations, requiring efficient power management: - **Battery Powered**: Li-ion, Li-Po (energy density ~250 Wh/kg). - **Energy Harvesting**: Solar (PV cells), piezoelectric, RF energy harvesting, thermal gradients. - **Low-Power Protocols**: CoAP, MQTT-SN, designed for **duty-cycled** operation (< 1% active time). ### 2.1.6 Security and Privacy Each Thing must implement at least baseline security: - **Hardware Root of Trust**: TPM 2.0 chips, secure boot. - **Encryption**: AES-128/256 for data, TLS/DTLS for transport. - **Authentication**: X.509 certificates, device provisioning via DPS (Device Provisioning Service). ## 2.2 Classification / Categories of "Things" The KTU syllabus recognizes a formal classification of Things based on their function and form: | Category | Description | Examples | Power Profile | |----------|-------------|----------|---------------| | **Sensors** | Sense physical parameters | DHT11, MQ-135, PIR | Ultra-low power | | **Actuators** | Perform physical actions | Servo, Relay, Pump | Medium power | | **Smart Objects** | Everyday objects with embedded intelligence | Smart bulb, Smart lock | Low power | | **Wearables** | Body-worn Things | Smartwatch, Fitness band | Battery powered | | **Vehicular Things** | Vehicles as Things | Connected cars, Drones | Vehicle battery | | **Industrial Things** | IIoT machinery | CNC machines, PLCs | Mains powered | | **Virtual Things** | Software-defined entities | Digital twins, Dashboards | N/A | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** The "Things" in IoT are **not limited to physical objects**. Virtual things (like a digital twin of a turbine in a cloud platform) are equally valid Things in the IoT ecosystem. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The concept of "Things" is foundational in: - **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture sensors, automated irrigation valves. - **Smart Healthcare**: Continuous glucose monitors, smart inhalers. - **Smart Cities**: Air quality monitors, smart streetlights, traffic cameras. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Predictive maintenance sensors on rotating machinery. - **Smart Homes**: Smart thermostats (Nest), voice assistants (Alexa, Google Home). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes | |-----------|-----------------|------|-------| | IPv6 Address Space | $2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Addresses | Sufficient for every atom on Earth (~10²²) | | Energy of a Wireless Bit | $E_b = P_t \cdot T_b$ | Joules | $P_t$ = transmit power, $T_b$ = bit duration | | Battery Life Estimate | $L = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V}{I_{avg}}$ | Hours | $C_{bat}$ = mAh, $I_{avg}$ = average current | | Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | | Data Throughput (Time) | $T = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{bps}}$ | Seconds | $N_{bits}$ = data size, $R_{bps}$ = bitrate | | Path Loss (Friis) | $PL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ | dB | Free-space path loss | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of IoT Thing Components ## 3.1 Generic Architecture of an IoT "Thing" Every IoT Thing, regardless of complexity, can be decomposed into **five functional building blocks**. Let us derive the internal structure systematically. ### Step 1: Identify the Five Subsystems A complete IoT Thing consists of: 1. **Sensing Subsystem** (Sensors + ADC) 2. **Processing Subsystem** (MCU / MPU) 3. **Communication Subsystem** (Radio / Network interface) 4. **Power Subsystem** (Battery / Energy harvester) 5. **Actuation Subsystem** (Motors, Relays — optional) ### Step 2: Define the Data Flow The data flows as a linear pipeline: $$\text{Physical Phenomenon} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Radio} \rightarrow \text{Network}$$ If actuation is required, a reverse channel exists: $$\text{Network} \rightarrow \text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{DAC / PWM} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Physical Action}$$ ### Step 3: Detailed Functional Breakdown #### (a) Sensing Subsystem - **Transducer**: Converts physical energy into electrical signal. - **Signal Conditioning**: Amplification, filtering. - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**: Converts analog signal to digital value. The relationship between sensor output voltage $V_s$ and the physical quantity $Q$ (e.g., temperature) is: $$ \begin{aligned} V_s &= S \cdot Q + V_{offset} \\ \text{where } S &= \text{Sensitivity (V per unit)} \\ V_{offset} &= \text{Zero-point offset voltage} \end{aligned} $$ For example, the **LM35** temperature sensor has $S = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$ and $V_{offset} = 0 \text{ V}$. #### (b) Processing Subsystem The MCU samples the ADC at a rate $f_s$ and may perform local processing (e.g., averaging, thresholding, edge AI inference). The minimum sampling rate obeys the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**: $$ \begin{aligned} f_s &\geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \\ \text{where } f_{max} &= \text{highest frequency component in the signal} \end{aligned} $$ If the temperature changes at most once per second, $f_{max} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$, so $f_s \geq 1 \text{ Hz}$. In practice, a safety factor of $4\times$ is used: $f_s = 2 \text{ Hz}$. #### (c) Communication Subsystem The MCU packages the data into a protocol frame (e.g., MQTT publish message) and transmits via radio. The **time to transmit** a payload of $N$ bits at a bitrate $R$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tx} &= \frac{N_{header} + N_{payload}}{R_{bps}} \\ \text{where } N_{header} &= \text{protocol overhead bits} \\ N_{payload} &= \text{application data bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Example**: For a 50-byte payload (400 bits) with 20 bytes header (160 bits) at BLE 1 Mbps: $$T_{tx} = \frac{160 + 400}{1 \times 10^6} = 560 \text{ µs}$$ #### (d) Power Subsystem The Thing's battery life $L$ is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{I_{avg} \cdot V_{bat}} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} \text{ (hours)} \\ \text{where } C_{bat} &= \text{Battery capacity in mAh} \\ I_{avg} &= \text{Average current draw in mA} \end{aligned} $$ **Example**: A 1000 mAh battery with an average current of 1 mA yields: $$L = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 1000 \text{ hours} \approx 41.7 \text{ days}$$ If the device is in deep sleep for 99% of the time drawing only 10 µA and active for 1% drawing 50 mA: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{avg} &= (0.99 \times 0.01 \text{ mA}) + (0.01 \times 50 \text{ mA}) \\ &= 0.0099 \text{ mA} + 0.5 \text{ mA} \\ &= 0.5099 \text{ mA} \\ L &= \frac{1000}{0.5099} \approx 1961 \text{ hours} \approx 81.7 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### (e) Actuation Subsystem The MCU outputs a control signal — either a digital GPIO pin, a PWM waveform, or a DAC voltage — to drive the actuator. For a PWM-driven DC motor, the **effective DC voltage** is: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{avg} &= D \cdot V_{supply} \\ \text{where } D &= \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \text{ (duty cycle, } 0 \leq D \leq 1\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ For a 12V supply and $D = 0.6$: $$V_{avg} = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2 \text{ V}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem**: An IoT temperature sensor Thing uses an LM35 sensor ($S = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$) connected to a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 3.3 V. Find the digital output value when the temperature is 25 °C. **Solution**: **Step 1**: Calculate the sensor output voltage: $$ V_s = S \cdot T = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times 25 \text{ °C} = 250 \text{ mV} = 0.25 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2**: Calculate the ADC resolution (LSB size): $$ \text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} \approx 3.226 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 3**: Compute the digital output: $$ D_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_s}{\text{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{250 \text{ mV}}{3.226 \text{ mV}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 77.5 \rfloor = 77 $$ **Step 4**: Verification — convert back: $$ V_{reconstructed} = 77 \times 3.226 \text{ mV} = 248.4 \text{ mV} \approx 0.25 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Always show the **unit consistency** in your derivations. Examiners specifically look for proper unit cancellation as evidence of clear understanding. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic IoT "Thing" Functional Block Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of a generic IoT Thing, showing the five subsystems and their interconnections. ```mermaid graph TD A[Physical World Phenomenon
Temperature, Pressure, Motion, Light] --> B[Sensing Subsystem
Sensor + Signal Conditioning + ADC] B --> C[Processing Subsystem
MCU or MPU with Firmware] C --> D{Decision Logic
Threshold / Edge AI} D -->|Threshold Exceeded| E[Communication Subsystem
Radio BLE WiFi LoRa] D -->|Local Action Required| F[Actuation Subsystem
Motor Relay Solenoid] E --> G[Network Gateway
Router Edge Server] F --> H[Physical Action
Buzzer Valve LED] G --> I[Cloud IoT Platform
AWS IoT Azure IoT Hub] I --> J[Application Layer
Dashboard Mobile Alert] I -->|Downlink Command| E P[Power Subsystem
Battery Solar Energy Harvester] -.->|Powers| B P -.->|Powers| C P -.->|Powers| E P -.->|Powers| F ``` ## 4.2 Classification of "Things" in IoT (Hierarchical View) ```mermaid graph TD Root[Things in IoT] --> PT[Physical Things] Root --> VT[Virtual Things] PT --> PASS[Passive Things] PT --> ACT[Active Things] PASS --> RFID[RFID Tags] PASS --> NFC[NFC Tags] PASS --> QR[QR Code Labels] ACT --> SEN[Sensor Devices] ACT --> ACT2[Actuator Devices] ACT --> SM[Smart Objects] ACT --> WR[Wearable Things] ACT --> VEH[Vehicular Things] ACT --> IIOT[Industrial IoT Things] SEN --> SEN1[Temperature Humidity Gas] ACT2 --> ACT3[Motor Relay Valve Buzzer] SM --> SM1[Smart Bulb Smart Lock Smart Meter] WR --> WR1[Smartwatch Fitness Band Smart Glasses] VEH --> VEH1[Connected Car Drone Fleet Tracker] IIOT --> IIOT1[PLC CNC Machine Robotic Arm] VT --> DT[Digital Twins] VT --> VW[Virtual World Entities] VT --> DA[Data Streams and Dashboards] ``` ## 4.3 Data Flow Sequence in a Sensing "Thing" ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Env as Physical Environment participant Sen as Sensor participant MCU as Microcontroller participant Rad as Radio Module participant GW as Gateway participant Cloud as Cloud Server Env->>Sen: Physical stimulus (T, P, L) Sen->>MCU: Analog voltage (mV) MCU->>MCU: ADC conversion + processing alt Threshold NOT exceeded MCU->>MCU: Store in buffer (no TX) else Threshold exceeded MCU->>Rad: Digital packet (bytes) Rad->>GW: Encrypted RF transmission GW->>Cloud: MQTT/HTTPS publish Cloud->>Cloud: Store + Analyze Cloud-->>GW: Acknowledgment GW-->>Rad: ACK frame end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a block diagram of an IoT Thing, always label **five blocks**: Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power, and Actuation. Even if a part is optional (like actuation), drawing it and marking it as "optional" still scores full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define "Things" in the context of IoT. List any four examples of IoT Things.** **Model Answer**: In IoT, "Things" refer to physical or virtual objects that are uniquely identifiable, capable of sensing, processing, and communicating data over a network without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction. They are the basic building blocks of the IoT ecosystem. **Four Examples of IoT Things**: 1. **Smart Temperature Sensor** (e.g., DHT22) – senses ambient temperature and humidity. 2. **Smart Wearable** (e.g., Fitbit) – monitors heart rate and steps. 3. **Connected Car** – reports vehicle diagnostics and location to the cloud. 4. **Smart Energy Meter** – transmits real-time electricity consumption data to the utility provider. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Word Count Tip:** A 3-mark answer in KTU requires **2 to 3 lines for definition** and **4 to 5 lines for examples**. Keep total length around 80–100 words. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator in IoT Things. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer**: | Aspect | Sensor | Actuator | |--------|--------|----------| | Function | Detects and measures physical quantities | Performs a physical action in response to a signal | | Direction of energy | Converts physical energy → electrical signal | Converts electrical signal → physical action | | Role in IoT | Sense the environment | Change the environment | | Example | LM35 (temperature sensor) | SG90 (servo motor) | A **sensor** observes (e.g., a PIR motion sensor detecting an intruder), while an **actuator** acts (e.g., a relay switching on a security alarm when motion is detected). ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **five major characteristics** of Things in IoT. How do these characteristics distinguish IoT Things from traditional computing devices? **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture** of a generic IoT Thing. Explain the function of each block. --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Seven Marks Detailed Answer The **five major characteristics** of Things in IoT are: **1. Identity and Sensing Capability** Every IoT Thing must be uniquely identifiable (via IPv6 address, EPC, or UUID) and must be able to sense its environment using sensors. For example, a soil moisture sensor Thing has a unique IP and measures soil water content. **2. Connectivity** IoT Things connect to the internet (or local network) via short-range (BLE, Zigbee), medium-range (Wi-Fi), or long-range (LoRa, NB-IoT) communication protocols. **3. Processing Intelligence** Each Thing contains an embedded MCU/MPU that performs local data processing, filtering, and decision-making. Modern Things even run lightweight ML models at the edge. **4. Power Autonomy** IoT Things are often battery-operated or energy-harvested (solar, piezoelectric). Low-power design is critical — many Things operate for **years** on a single coin cell. **5. Security and Privacy** Things implement secure boot, encrypted communication (TLS/DTLS), and authenticated provisioning to prevent unauthorized access. **Distinction from Traditional Computing Devices**: | Parameter | Traditional PC | IoT Thing | |-----------|----------------|-----------| | Purpose | General computation | Single-purpose sensing/actuation | | Power | Mains (100W+) | Battery / harvested (<1W) | | Connectivity | Wi-Fi/Ethernet | Multiple (BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT) | | User Interface | Screen, keyboard | No direct UI; cloud dashboard | | Lifecycle | 3–5 years | 10+ years (battery) | #### Part (b) — Seven Marks Detailed Answer **Block Diagram of a Generic IoT Thing**: ```mermaid graph LR subgraph POWER[Power Subsystem] PWR[Battery and Energy Harvester] end subgraph SENSE[Sensing Subsystem] SNS[Physical Sensors] ADC[ADC Module] end subgraph PROC[Processing Subsystem] MCU[Microcontroller and Firmware] end subgraph COMM[Communication Subsystem] RAD[Radio Module and Antenna] end subgraph ACT[Actuation Subsystem] ACT2[Actuators and Drivers] end SNS -->|Analog signal| ADC ADC -->|Digital value| MCU MCU -->|Control data| RAD MCU -->|PWM or GPIO| ACT2 PWR -.->|3.3V rail| SNS PWR -.->|3.3V rail| MCU PWR -.->|3.3V to 5V| RAD PWR -.->|Driver voltage| ACT2 RAD -->|Encrypted packet| EXT[Network Gateway and Cloud] EXT -->|Downlink command| RAD ``` **Functions of Each Block**: - **Sensing Subsystem**: Contains the physical sensor (LM35, DHT11, etc.) and an ADC to convert analog signals into digital values. Example: A TMP36 sensor outputs 750 mV at 25 °C, which is digitized to a 10-bit value. - **Processing Subsystem**: The MCU (e.g., ESP32) runs firmware that samples the ADC, applies filters, makes decisions, and prepares data packets. It controls duty cycling for power savings. - **Communication Subsystem**: The radio module (e.g., nRF24L01, SX1276) transmits packets over the air using protocols like BLE or LoRaWAN. It handles encryption and addressing. - **Actuation Subsystem**: Performs physical actions using motors, relays, or solenoids when commanded by the MCU. Optional — not all Things need it. - **Power Subsystem**: Provides regulated DC power to all other blocks. Includes battery management, voltage regulators (LDO/Buck), and optional energy harvesting circuits. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various types of "Things" in IoT with suitable examples for each category. Differentiate between **physical** and **virtual** Things. **(b) [7 Marks]** An IoT Thing uses a 3.3 V, 10-bit ADC. The Thing is powered by a 2000 mAh battery and draws an average current of 0.5 mA. Calculate: 1. The ADC resolution (LSB) in millivolts. 2. The theoretical battery life in days. 3. The quantization error in terms of LSB count. --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Seven Marks Detailed Answer **Classification of Things in IoT**: IoT Things can be broadly classified into **two main categories**: **(A) Physical Things** — Real-world objects with embedded electronics: | Sub-category | Description | Example | |--------------|-------------|---------| | **Sensors** | Detect physical parameters | DHT11 (temperature), MQ-2 (gas) | | **Actuators** | Perform physical actions | Servo motor, Relay | | **Smart Objects** | Everyday objects with intelligence | Smart bulb, Smart lock | | **Wearables** | Body-worn devices | Smartwatch, Smart ring | | **Vehicular Things** | Vehicles connected to the network | Connected car, Drone | | **Industrial Things** | Factory equipment | PLC, CNC machine, Robotic arm | **(B) Virtual Things** — Software-defined entities: | Sub-category | Description | Example | |--------------|-------------|---------| | **Digital Twins** | Virtual replicas of physical Things | Digital twin of a wind turbine | | **Data Streams** | Continuous data flows | Live stock ticker feed | | **Virtual Worlds** | 3D / VR environments | Smart city simulator in Unity | **Difference Between Physical and Virtual Things**: | Aspect | Physical Things | Virtual Things | |--------|-----------------|----------------| | Existence | Tangible, in the real world | Software-defined, in cyberspace | | Examples | Smartwatch, Temperature sensor | Digital twin, Virtual dashboard | | Energy | Requires electrical power | Runs on server power | | Sensing | Direct interaction with environment | Indirect via physical counterparts | #### Part (b) — Seven Marks Detailed Answer **Given Data**: - Supply voltage: $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ - ADC resolution: $n = 10 \text{ bits}$ - Battery capacity: $C_{bat} = 2000 \text{ mAh}$ - Average current: $I_{avg} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$ **Step 1: Calculate the ADC resolution (LSB size)** The size of one Least Significant Bit (LSB) of a unipolar ADC is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} \\ &= \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1024 - 1} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1023} \\ &\approx 0.003226 \text{ V} = 3.226 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the theoretical battery life** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} \text{ hours} \\ &= \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = 4000 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to days: $$ L_{days} = \frac{4000 \text{ hours}}{24 \text{ hours/day}} \approx 166.67 \text{ days} \approx 5.5 \text{ months} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the quantization error in LSB** The theoretical maximum quantization error of an ideal ADC is: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{q,max} &= \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB} = \pm 0.5 \text{ LSB} \\ \text{Or in voltage:} \quad E_{q,max} &= \pm \frac{\text{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{3.226 \text{ mV}}{2} \\ &= \pm 1.613 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Results**: - ADC Resolution: **3.226 mV / LSB** - Battery Life: **4000 hours ≈ 166.67 days ≈ 5.5 months** - Quantization Error: **± 0.5 LSB = ± 1.613 mV** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use the formula $\text{LSB} = V_{ref} / 2^n$ instead of $V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$. Although the difference is tiny (3.223 mV vs 3.226 mV), the textbook-correct formula is **$V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$** for a unipolar ADC. Using the wrong formula may cost **0.5 to 1 mark**. Also, students often forget to convert hours to days in the battery-life subpart — always include both forms or specify the unit explicitly. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: The 'Things' in IoT** - **Definition**: A "Thing" in IoT is any uniquely identifiable physical or virtual object capable of sensing, processing, and communicating data over a network. - **Three Pillars of a Thing**: (1) **Identity** (IPv6/UUID), (2) **Sensing/Actuation**, (3) **Connectivity**. - **Five Characteristics**: Identity, Sensing, Connectivity, Intelligence/Processing, Power Autonomy, Security. - **Five Subsystems** of a generic Thing: Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power, Actuation. - **Sensors vs Actuators**: Sensors *observe* (input to system); Actuators *act* (output from system). - **Categories**: Physical Things (sensors, actuators, smart objects, wearables, vehicular, IIoT) and Virtual Things (digital twins, data streams, VR worlds). - **IPv6 Address Space**: $2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ — virtually unlimited addressing. - **Key Formula — Battery Life**: $L = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$ (in hours); remember to convert to days when required. - **Key Formula — ADC Resolution**: $\text{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$ — not $V_{ref} / 2^n$. - **Key Formula — Nyquist Sampling**: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ for lossless signal reconstruction. - **Key Formula — PWM Average Voltage**: $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ (used in actuation blocks). - **Communication Protocols** by range: NFC (< 0.1 m), BLE (10 m), Wi-Fi (50 m), LoRa (10 km), Satellite (> 1000 km). - **Energy Harvesting Sources**: Solar (PV), piezoelectric (vibration), RF (ambient RF), thermoelectric (ΔT). - **Virtual Things are also Things**: Don't forget to mention digital twins when listing examples of Things. - **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Equating "Things" with only "sensors". A Thing is the *complete entity*, not just the sensor component.

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **sensor** is a physical device, module, machine, or subsystem whose purpose is to detect events or changes in its physical environment and convert these physical stimuli (such as heat, light, sound, pressure, motion, or chemical composition) into measurable electrical signals (analog or digital) that can be interpreted, recorded, displayed, or processed by an electronic system — typically a microcontroller, microprocessor, or IoT edge node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):** A sensor is an electronic component that converts a **physical parameter** (stimulus) into a corresponding **electrical output signal** that can be measured, analyzed, and acted upon by the IoT system.

In the strict KTU/IoT engineering vocabulary, the term **transducer** is often used synonymously with sensor, but with a subtle distinction:

- **Sensor:** Specifically the *sensing element* that responds to a physical stimulus.
- **Transducer:** The *complete device* that converts one form of energy to another (sensors + signal conditioning + output stage).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smoke alarm in your kitchen**. It cannot "see" or "smell" smoke the way you do. Instead, it contains a tiny **sensor** (typically a photoelectric or ionization chamber) that physically reacts when smoke particles enter it. This reaction is translated into an electrical signal. If the signal crosses a threshold, the buzzer (actuator) beeps.

In IoT terms:
- Your **eyes, nose, and skin** = sensors (input).
- Your **brain** = microcontroller/processor (decision).
- Your **hands and mouth** = actuators (output).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy for IoT:** Sensors are the **"Five Senses of an IoT System"**. Without sensors, an IoT system is deaf, dumb, and blind to the physical world. They are the *data-acquisition frontline* of any IoT architecture.

## 1.3 The KTU-IoT Sensor Data Flow Chain

In an IoT node, the sensor never works in isolation. It is part of a strict, well-defined signal chain:

1. **Physical Stimulus** → (heat, light, motion, pressure, etc.)
2. **Sensor Element** → converts stimulus → raw electrical signal
3. **Signal Conditioning** → amplification, filtering, linearization
4. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** → digital samples
5. **Microcontroller / Edge Processor** → interprets data
6. **Communication Module** → Wi-Fi / BLE / LoRa transmits to cloud

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module 2, the focus is on understanding the **working principles**, **classification**, **characteristics (specifications)**, and **interfacing** of common sensors used in IoT applications (temperature, humidity, motion, light, gas, etc.).

## 1.4 Why Sensors Are Central to IoT

The entire economic and engineering premise of IoT rests on the **TRIPLE-C Principle**: **Collect, Communicate, Compute**. Sensors are responsible for the **Collect** leg. Without high-quality, reliable sensors, even the most sophisticated cloud AI or analytics is meaningless — a classic case of **"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)"**.

In the global IoT market (2024), sensor manufacturing is the **largest hardware segment**, valued in tens of billions of dollars, with billions of units shipped annually for smart cities, industrial IoT (IIoT), wearables, and precision agriculture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Classification (High-Yield for KTU)

KTU examiners frequently test the **taxonomy** of sensors. Mastering this classification is non-negotiable.

### A) By Power Requirement
| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Active Sensor** | Requires external power (excitation voltage) to operate. Generates output in response to input. | Thermistor, Strain Gauge, LDR, Photodiode |
| **Passive Sensor** | Self-generates electrical output without external excitation. Acts like an *energy converter*. | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric sensor, Solar cell, RTD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** Passive sensors **P**assively produce their own voltage (like a thermocouple). Active sensors **A**sk for external power.

### B) By Output Signal Type
| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Analog Sensor** | Output is a continuous voltage/current proportional to the measured quantity. | LM35 temperature sensor, Potentiometer |
| **Digital Sensor** | Output is discrete pulses, bits, or a serial protocol (I2C/SPI). | DHT11, DS18B20, BMP280 |

### C) By Sensing Phenomenon
- **Thermal:** Thermistor, Thermocouple, RTD, LM35, DHT11
- **Mechanical / Position:** Strain gauge, Accelerometer, Gyroscope, Flex sensor
- **Optical:** LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor, IR sensor
- **Chemical:** Gas sensor (MQ-series), pH sensor, Alcohol sensor
- **Biological:** Heart rate (PPG), ECG, EMG, Glucose sensor
- **Magnetic:** Hall-effect sensor, Reed switch
- **Acoustic:** Ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04), Microphone

### D) By Application Domain (IoT-Focused)
- Environmental: Temperature, humidity, air quality, CO₂
- Industrial: Pressure, flow, level, vibration
- Biomedical: Heart rate, SpO₂, body temperature
- Smart Home: PIR motion, door/window reed, smoke

## 2.2 Fundamental Characteristics of Sensors

Every sensor datasheet contains these **parameters**, which the KTU examiner loves to ask in definitions and short questions:

| Parameter | Definition | Formula / Unit |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| **Sensitivity** | Ratio of change in output to change in input. | $S = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Output}}{\Delta \text{Input}}$ |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of measured value to the true value. | Expressed as $\pm$ % of Full Scale (FS) |
| **Precision** | Repeatability / consistency of measurement. | Standard deviation $\sigma$ |
| **Range** | Min and Max input the sensor can detect. | $[L_{\min}, L_{\max}]$ |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input. | $\Delta x_{\min}$ |
| **Linearity** | How close the output-input curve is to a straight line. | Non-linearity = $\dfrac{\text{Max deviation}}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Drift** | Change of output with time (even when input is constant). | Drift = $\dfrac{\text{Output change}}{\text{Time}}$ |
| **Response Time** | Time taken for sensor output to reach 90% (or 63.2%) of final value. | $\tau$ (seconds) |
| **Hysteresis** | Difference in output for same input during increasing vs decreasing cycle. | $H = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Selectivity / Specificity** | Sensor's ability to respond only to the target stimulus. | Cross-sensitivity factor |
| **Dynamic Range (DR)** | Ratio between largest and smallest detectable signal. | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ in **dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When defining *Sensitivity* vs *Accuracy* vs *Precision*, students often confuse precision with accuracy. **Accuracy = Correctness**, **Precision = Consistency**. A sensor can be precise but inaccurate (e.g., consistently reads 2°C high).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta X_{in}}$ | V/unit |
| 2 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | RTD (Platinum) | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = S_{\text{AB}} \cdot \Delta T$ | Volts |
| 5 | Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Piezoelectric | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Coulombs |
| 7 | LDR | $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 9 | Non-linearity | $NL = \dfrac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal\,line}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 10 | Time constant (1st order) | $V(t) = V_{final}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | seconds |

## 2.4 Working Principles — Core Sensors

### 2.4.1 Temperature Sensors (KTU Favourite)

**(a) Thermistor (NTC Type):**
A semiconductor whose resistance decreases as temperature increases (Negative Temperature Coefficient). The resistance-temperature relationship follows the **B-parameter equation** (also called the $\beta$ equation):

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where $R_T$ is the resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin), $R_0$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually **298.15 K**, i.e., **25 °C**), and $\beta$ is a material constant (typically 3000–5000 K).

**(b) Thermocouple:**
Based on the **Seebeck Effect** — when two dissimilar metals are joined, a small voltage (millivolts) is generated proportional to the temperature difference between the hot junction (measurement point) and the cold junction (reference). Common types: **K-type** (Chromel-Alumel), **J-type**, **T-type**.

$$V_{out} = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$

where $S_{AB}$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** (μV/°C).

**(c) LM35 (KTU's most-asked analog temperature IC):**
A precision centigrade temperature sensor from Texas Instruments that provides a **linear analog output of 10 mV/°C**. No calibration required; operates from **4 V to 30 V**.

$$V_{out}(\text{mV}) = 10 \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

**(d) DHT11 / DHT22 (Digital Temperature + Humidity):**
Single-wire digital interface; outputs calibrated digital signal; uses a thermistor + capacitive humidity sensor + internal MCU.

### 2.4.2 PIR Motion Sensor (Passive Infrared)

A **Pyroelectric Infrared Sensor** detects changes in infrared radiation emitted by warm bodies (humans, animals). It has two slots that detect IR — when a warm body crosses the field of view, the differential IR change triggers the output. **"Passive"** means it does not emit any IR itself (unlike an active IR sensor).

> **Real-World Use:** Security alarms, automatic doors, smart lighting.

### 2.4.3 Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04)

Measures distance by emitting a **40 kHz ultrasound pulse** and timing the echo. Uses the basic physics equation:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the speed of sound in air (≈ **343 m/s** at **20 °C**), $t$ is the round-trip time, and division by 2 accounts for the to-and-fro travel.

### 2.4.4 Gas Sensor (MQ-series)

Uses a **chemiresistor** — a tin dioxide (SnO₂) sensing layer whose resistance changes when target gas molecules adsorb on its surface. A heater coil (heater) maintains the sensing layer at the optimal operating temperature (~300–400 °C). Output is an analog voltage proportional to gas concentration (in **ppm**).

### 2.4.5 LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)

A photoconductive cell. Resistance **decreases** as light intensity **increases**. The relationship is non-linear and given by:

$$R = A \cdot L^{-\gamma}$$

where $L$ is illuminance (lux), $A$ is a constant, and $\gamma$ is the photoconductive exponent (typically **0.7–0.9** for CdS cells).

## 2.5 Smart Sensors vs Dumb Sensors

| Feature | Dumb Sensor | Smart Sensor |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Output | Raw analog voltage | Processed digital data |
| Signal conditioning | External | Built-in |
| ADC | External | Internal |
| Communication | None | I2C, SPI, UART |
| Calibration | Manual | Auto/self-calibrating |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Thermistor | DS18B20, BME680 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trend:** Modern IoT prefers **smart sensors** because they integrate with microcontrollers directly via I2C/SPI, reducing analog noise issues and PCB complexity.

## 2.6 Sensor Interfacing Fundamentals (IoT Perspective)

Sensors interface with edge devices (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi) through:
1. **Analog Input Pin (ADC):** For analog sensors (LM35, LDR via voltage divider).
2. **Digital GPIO:** For digital output sensors (PIR, Reed switch).
3. **Serial Protocols:** For smart sensors (I2C — BMP280, SPI — MAX31855 for thermocouple, 1-Wire — DS18B20).
4. **PWM/Interrupt Pins:** For sensors with pulse-width or event-based outputs.

> **Engineering Insight:** In industrial IoT (IIoT), the choice of protocol (I2C vs SPI vs UART) is dictated by **distance**, **speed**, **noise immunity**, and **number of sensors per bus**. I2C allows multiple devices on just 2 wires using addressing — a major advantage for sensor-dense IoT nodes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: NTC Thermistor Temperature Calculation (Steinhart-Hart Equation)

The simplified B-parameter equation (covered in Section 2) is an approximation. The **exact** form is the **Steinhart-Hart Equation**, which models the resistance-temperature curve of an NTC thermistor to within ±0.01 °C over wide ranges.

The general Steinhart-Hart form is:

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot (\ln(R))^3$$

**Step 1 — Identify given values (typical exam problem):**
A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has B-parameter values: $R_0 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$, $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$, $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$. The measured resistance at some unknown temperature is $R_T = 5{,}000\ \Omega$. Compute the temperature $T$.

**Step 2 — Start with the B-parameter equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{1}{3950} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5000}{10000}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the logarithm term:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{5000}{10000}\right) = \ln(0.5) = -0.693147$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first term:**

$$\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second term:**

$$\frac{-0.693147}{3950} = -1.755 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Sum the two terms:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} + (-1.755 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.178 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Invert to obtain T in Kelvin:**

$$T = \frac{1}{3.178 \times 10^{-3}} = 314.66\ \text{K}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to Celsius:**

$$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = 314.66 - 273.15 = 41.51\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

> **Final Result:** The thermistor reading of 5 kΩ corresponds to **≈ 41.5 °C**, a realistic value for an NTC thermistor (resistance halves roughly every 25–30 °C increase from 25 °C baseline).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Calculation (Time-of-Flight)

A **HC-SR04** ultrasonic sensor is interfaced to a microcontroller. The trigger pin receives a 10 μs pulse; the sensor emits 8 cycles of 40 kHz burst; the echo pin goes HIGH for a duration $t_{echo}$ proportional to the distance.

**Step 1 — Physics principle:**
Sound travels at velocity $v$ through the medium. The total time $t_{echo}$ corresponds to the round-trip (sensor → object → sensor).

$$d_{one\text{-}way} = v \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Account for round trip (the factor of 2):**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Given data (typical exam):** $t_{echo} = 5.83\ \text{ms}$ (measured by timer), temperature = 25 °C.

**Step 4 — Speed of sound correction (temperature-dependent):**
The speed of sound in air depends on temperature according to:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})\ \ \text{m/s}$$

At $T = 25\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$v = 331.4 + (0.6)(25) = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into distance formula:**

$$d = \frac{346.4 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator:**

$$346.4 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0195\ \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Divide by 2:**

$$d = \frac{2.0195}{2} = 1.0098\ \text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{d \approx 1.01\ \text{m}}$$

> **Verification:** The HC-SR04 has a max range of about 4 m, so 1 m is well within operational bounds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: LM35 Output Voltage

**Step 1 — Given:** LM35 sensor operating at room temperature $T = 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 2 — LM35 transfer function (from datasheet):**

$$V_{out} = 10\ \text{mV} / ^{\circ}\text{C} \times T(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$V_{out} = 10 \times 30 = 300\ \text{mV} = 0.3\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — For ADC reading (10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, ESP32):**
ADC step size:

$$\text{Step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} = 3.226\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 5 — ADC digital value:**

$$\text{ADC} = \frac{V_{out}}{\text{Step}} = \frac{300}{3.226} \approx 93$$

**Step 6 — Reverse conversion (to verify):**

$$T = \frac{\text{ADC} \times V_{ref}}{1023 \times 10\ \text{mV}} = \frac{93 \times 3.3}{1023 \times 0.01} = \frac{306.9}{10.23} = 30.0\ ^{\circ}\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

---

| **LDR (GL5528)** | Analog | Resistance | Voltage divider | 1–1000 lux | — | Light sensing |
| **MPU6050** | Digital | I2C | I2C (400 kHz) | ±2000°/s, ±16 g | ±0.1°/s, ±0.01 g | Drones, wearables |
| **BME680** | Digital | I2C/SPI | I2C/SPI | T, P, RH, VOC | ±1 °C, ±3% RH | Air quality |

---

## 3.5 Sensor Noise & Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

**Step 1 — Definition of SNR:**
The signal-to-noise ratio quantifies the quality of a sensor's output:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)^2$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Worked example:**
A temperature sensor produces an output voltage swing of **500 mV** for the full temperature range. The RMS noise voltage is measured as **50 μV**. Compute the SNR in dB.

$$\text{SNR} = \left(\frac{0.5}{50 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2 = (10{,}000)^2 = 10^8$$

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(10^8) = 10 \times 8 = 80\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** A dynamic range of **80 dB** is excellent for a precision temperature sensor — equivalent to about **13.3 effective bits** of resolution, because:

$$\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{80 - 1.76}{6.02} \approx 13.0\ \text{bits}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why high-end ADCs (like 24-bit sigma-delta ADCs) are paired with precision sensors — they preserve the sensor's intrinsic low-noise resolution.

## 4.4 Voltage Divider — LDR Light Sensor Interfacing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC = 3.3V or 5V"]:::vcc --> R1["R1: Fixed Resistor (10 kohm)"]:::r1
    R1 --> NODE["NODE: Analog Input Pin to MCU ADC"]:::node
    LDR["LDR: Light Dependent Resistor"]:::ldr --> NODE
    NODE --> GND["GND"]:::gnd

    NODE -.-> MCU["MCU ADC: Reads Voltage Vout"]:::mcu

    classDef vcc fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef r1 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ldr fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef gnd fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mcu fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Operating Principle:**
At NODE, the output voltage is given by the standard voltage divider formula:

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$

- **Bright light:** $R_{LDR}$ decreases → $V_{out}$ **decreases**.
- **Darkness:** $R_{LDR}$ increases (MΩ range) → $V_{out}$ **increases** (approaches $V_{CC}$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Define a sensor. Differentiate between an active sensor and a passive sensor with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, pressure, motion, or chemical concentration) and converts it into a measurable electrical signal that can be read, recorded, or processed by an electronic system.

**Active Sensor:**
- Requires an **external power supply (excitation voltage)** to function.
- Generates an output only when external energy is provided.
- **Example:** A **thermistor** requires a bias voltage/current to produce a measurable resistance change.

**Passive Sensor:**
- **Self-generating** — produces its own electrical output (voltage or current) without needing external excitation.
- Operates on principles like the Seebeck effect, piezoelectric effect, or photovoltaic effect.
- **Example:** A **thermocouple** generates a millivolt-level voltage directly proportional to the temperature difference between its hot and cold junctions.

| Feature | Active | Passive |
|---------|--------|---------|
| External Power | Required | Not required |
| Output | Resistance/Capacitance change | Voltage/Current |
| Example | Thermistor, LDR | Thermocouple, Piezo |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any six important characteristics/specifications used to evaluate a sensor's performance.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensitivity:** Ratio of output change to input change.
2. **Accuracy:** Closeness of measured value to the true value (expressed as ±% FS).
3. **Precision / Repeatability:** Ability to produce consistent readings for the same input.
4. **Range:** Minimum and maximum input values the sensor can measure.
5. **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in input.
6. **Linearity:** Degree to which the output is linearly proportional to input.
7. **Response Time:** Time to reach 90% (or 63.2%) of the final output.
8. **Drift:** Change in output over time even when input is constant.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the working principle, construction, characteristics, and IoT applications of the **LM35** temperature sensor. Also derive the transfer function and interface it with an ESP32 microcontroller.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **NTC thermistor, RTD, thermocouple, and LM35** based on principle, range, accuracy, output type, and application.

---

#### Solution to (a):

**1. Introduction [1 mark]:**
The LM35 is a precision **centigrade-calibrated analog temperature sensor** from Texas Instruments. It is one of the most widely used sensors in IoT projects for ambient temperature monitoring.

**2. Construction [1 mark]:**
The LM35 is built using a bandgap reference circuit on a silicon die. The internal transistor pair (one at nominal current, one at 10× current) produces a stable voltage proportional to absolute temperature, which is then scaled to provide a 10 mV/°C linear output. The IC is housed in a TO-92, TO-220, or SOIC package.

**3. Working Principle [2 marks]:**
The LM35 uses the **proportional-to-absolute-temperature (PTAT)** principle. Two bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) are operated at different collector current densities (typically 1:10 ratio). The difference in their base-emitter voltages ($\Delta V_{BE}$) is directly proportional to absolute temperature (Kelvin). This $\Delta V_{BE}$ is amplified by an internal op-amp and offset-corrected by a bandgap reference to yield a Celsius-scaled output.

**4. Transfer Function Derivation [1.5 marks]:**
For the LM35, the datasheet specifies:

$$V_{out} = 10\ \text{mV} / ^{\circ}\text{C} \times T(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

**Proof (simplified):**
The PTAT voltage is $V_{PTAT} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \cdot \ln(10) \approx 198\ \mu\text{V/K}$ at room temperature.
After internal amplification by ~15× and offset cancellation by a 1.218 V bandgap reference, the output becomes:

$$V_{out}(\text{mV}) = 10 \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

**5. Key Characteristics [0.5 mark]:**
- Range: -55 °C to +150 °C
- Accuracy: ±0.5 °C (typical, calibrated)
- Linearity: ±0.25 °C
- Quiescent current: 60 μA
- Supply voltage: 4 V to 30 V

**6. ESP32 Interfacing Diagram [1 mark]:**
- VCC → 3.3 V or 5 V
- GND → GND
- Vout → ESP32 GPIO pin (e.g., **VP = GPIO36**, an ADC1-capable pin)

**7. IoT Applications [0.5 mark]:**
- Smart weather stations, server room monitoring, cold-chain logistics, greenhouse climate control, biomedical wearables.

---

#### Solution to (b):

| Feature | NTC Thermistor | RTD (PT100) | Thermocouple | LM35 |
|---------|----------------|-------------|--------------|------|
| **Principle** | Resistance changes with T (semiconductor) | Resistance changes linearly with T (metal) | Seebeck Effect (voltage from ΔT of 2 metals) | PTAT (bandgap reference) |
| **Range** | -55 to +150 °C | -200 to +850 °C | -200 to +2300 °C | -55 to +150 °C |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 to ±1 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±1 to ±5 °C | ±0.5 °C |
| **Output Type** | Resistance (analog) | Resistance (analog) | mV voltage (passive) | Analog voltage |
| **Active/Passive** | Active | Active | Passive | Active (self-powered via IC) |
| **Linearity** | Non-linear (exponential) | Highly linear | Approximately linear | Highly linear |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Low | Low |
| **Application** | Consumer electronics, IoT | Industrial process control | Furnaces, engines, kilns | IoT, prototyping |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of the **HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the distance equation and compute the distance for an echo time of $4.65\ \text{ms}$ at $T = 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of an **MQ-2 gas sensor**. Discuss its interfacing with an Arduino UNO and list four IoT applications.

---

#### Solution to (a):

**1. Working Principle [2 marks]:**
The HC-SR04 is an **active ultrasonic ranging sensor**. It emits a 40 kHz ultrasound burst (8 cycles) when triggered by a 10 μs pulse on its TRIG pin. The burst propagates through air, hits an object, and reflects back as an **echo**. The ECHO pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time of the ultrasonic pulse.

**2. Block Diagram [1 mark]:**

```
[TRIG] -> [Microcontroller Stage] -> [40 kHz Piezo Transmitter] --> AIR --> [Object]
                                                                                |
[ECHO] <- [Receiver + Amplifier] <- [40 kHz Piezo Receiver]  <----------------- (Reflected wave)
```

**3. Distance Equation Derivation [2 marks]:**
Let $v$ = velocity of sound in air, $t_{echo}$ = measured round-trip time.

The wave travels a **round-trip distance** of $2d$ in time $t_{echo}$:

$$2d = v \cdot t_{echo}$$

Therefore, the **one-way distance** is:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

The factor of **2** appears because the wave travels from sensor → object → sensor.

**4. Computation [2 marks]:**
Given: $t_{echo} = 4.65\ \text{ms} = 4.65 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$, $T = 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1:** Compute velocity of sound at 30 °C:

$$v = 331.4 + (0.6)(30) = 331.4 + 18.0 = 349.4\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the distance formula:

$$d = \frac{349.4 \times 4.65 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$349.4 \times 4.65 \times 10^{-3} = 1.6247\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4:** Final division:

$$d = \frac{1.6247}{2} = 0.8124\ \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** $d \approx 0.812\ \text{m} = 81.2\ \text{cm}$.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**1. Construction [2 marks]:**
The MQ-2 gas sensor consists of:
- **SnO₂ (tin dioxide) sensing layer** deposited on a ceramic tube.
- **Two gold electrodes** in contact with the sensing layer.
- **Heater coil (Ni-Cr alloy)** below the ceramic tube — heats the sensing layer to ~250–300 °C.
- **6-pin package** with a stainless steel mesh cover for explosion protection and air flow.

**2. Working Principle [2 marks]:**
The MQ-2 operates on the **chemiresistor principle**. In clean air, oxygen is adsorbed on the SnO₂ surface, trapping electrons and creating a high baseline resistance. When a combustible gas (LPG, methane, propane, hydrogen, alcohol vapor) contacts the hot sensing layer, the gas molecules react with the adsorbed oxygen, releasing the trapped electrons. This **lowers the resistance**. The change in resistance is inversely proportional to gas concentration. The MQ-2 typically uses a voltage divider circuit (sensor + load resistor) to convert this resistance change into an analog voltage.

**3. Interfacing with Arduino UNO [1.5 marks]:**
- VCC → 5 V (powers the heater).
- GND → GND.
- AO (Analog Out) → A0 (analog input pin).
- DO (Digital Out, optional) → Digital pin D2 (threshold via on-board potentiometer).

A simple Arduino sketch reads `analogRead(A0)` and compares to a threshold. For IoT, this is extended with an ESP8266/ESP32 and a Blynk/ThingSpeak dashboard for remote LPG leak alerts.

**4. IoT Applications [1.5 marks]:**
1. **Smart LPG Leak Detector** in kitchens (industrial/household safety).
2. **Indoor Air Quality Monitoring** in smart buildings.
3. **Fire Alarm Systems** in warehouses.
4. **Mining Safety:** Detecting methane accumulation in coal mines.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deducting Mistakes in Sensor Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Sensitivity" with "Accuracy"** — Sensitivity is *output per unit input*; accuracy is *correctness with respect to true value*. Examiners regularly deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic distance** — The most common error in HC-SR04 numerical problems. The wave travels **there AND back**, so the time corresponds to **2d**, not $d$.
>
> 3. **Using $T$ in Celsius when the B-parameter equation requires Kelvin** — Always convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$. Failure to do so → wrong answer → full marks lost in numerical problems.
>
> 4. **Writing "PIR sensor emits IR"** — It is a **PASSIVE** Infrared sensor. It does *not* emit anything. Mark deduction: 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Writing "Thermocouple contains a heater"** — Heaters are in MQ-series gas sensors and some flow sensors, not in thermocouples. Mark loss: 0.5 mark.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Active vs Passive sensors** — A thermocouple is **passive** (self-generating); a thermistor is **active** (needs excitation). This is a high-frequency 3-mark question trap.
>
> 7. **In Block Diagrams, omitting the heater / conditioning stage** — In smart sensor diagrams, omitting power management, signal conditioning, or ADC blocks results in **2-mark deduction** for incompleteness.
>
> 8. **Writing vague IoT applications like "for sensing"** — Examiners expect specific scenarios: "smart agriculture soil moisture monitoring in Kerala paddy fields" — specificity earns marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **sensor** converts a physical stimulus into a measurable electrical signal; the entire IoT data pipeline begins here.
- **Active sensors** need external power (thermistor, LDR); **Passive sensors** self-generate (thermocouple, piezoelectric).
- **Analog sensors** produce continuous outputs (LM35, MQ-2); **Digital sensors** produce discrete/serial outputs (DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050).
- Key sensor characteristics: **Sensitivity, Accuracy, Precision, Range, Resolution, Linearity, Drift, Response Time, Hysteresis, Dynamic Range**.
- The **NTC thermistor equation** is $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$, requiring **T in Kelvin**.
- **Thermocouple voltage:** $V = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ (Seebeck effect).
- **LM35 transfer function:** $V_{out}(\text{mV}) = 10 \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})$.
- **Ultrasonic distance:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ — **always divide by 2** (round-trip).
- **Speed of sound in air:** $v = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})$ m/s.
- **LDR voltage divider** is the standard analog interfacing technique — $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{LDR} / (R_1 + R_{LDR})$.
- **PIR** is **passive** — does not emit IR; detects IR from warm bodies.
- **MQ-series gas sensors** use a SnO₂ sensing layer + heater coil; resistance drops when target gas is present.
- **Smart sensors** integrate sensing + ADC + digital interface (I2C/SPI) + memory for calibration.
- **SNR in dB:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{signal}/P_{noise})$; ENOB = $\frac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$.
- KTU favourites: **LM35, DHT11, HC-SR04, PIR, MQ-2, LDR, MPU6050** — memorize their principle, output, and one application each.
- **Differentiator table** (NTC vs RTD vs TC vs LM35) appears in nearly every KTU paper — learn it cold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Actuators

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actuator (KTU 2024 Definition):** An *actuator* is an electromechanical or mechanical output device that **converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a physical action or motion** in response to a command received from an IoT processing unit (typically a microcontroller, SoC, or edge processor). In the IoT reference architecture, the actuator forms the **final execution stage** of the *Sense–Compute–Actuate* loop.

In the formal KTU IoT architecture model, an actuator is the **physical counterpart of a sensor**. While a sensor *transduces* a physical quantity into an electrical signal, an actuator *transduces* an electrical command into a physical effect.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Human Nervous System:**
> - Your **eyes, ears, and skin** are *sensors* (they collect information).
> - Your **brain** is the *processor* (it decides what to do).
> - Your **hands, legs, and mouth** are *actuators* (they perform the action decided by the brain).
>
> In an IoT system, a temperature *sensor* measures a room at 35 °C, the *microcontroller* decides "turn on the fan," and the *actuator* (motor) actually spins the blades. **The actuator is the "muscle" of any IoT system.**

## 1.3 Role of Actuators in the IoT Stack

Actuators are positioned at the **perception–action boundary** of an IoT node. According to the KTU 2024 module classification:

| IoT Stack Layer | Component Type | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Perception (Hardware) | **Sensors** (Input) | DHT11, LDR, PIR |
| Perception (Hardware) | **Actuators** (Output) | DC Motor, Relay, Buzzer |
| Network Layer | Communication modules | ESP8266, NRF24L01 |
| Processing Layer | MCU / SoC | Arduino, Raspberry Pi, NodeMCU |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Recall Thresholds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for Actuators:**
> - **Stroke Length** – linear displacement range (typically measured in **mm** or **cm**)
> - **Torque** – rotational force (measured in **N·m** or **kg·cm**)
> - **Operating Voltage** – usually **3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V, or 24 V DC** for IoT nodes
> - **Response Time** – delay between command and motion (**ms**)
> - **Power Rating** – in **Watts (W)**
> - **Duty Cycle** – ratio of active to total time, expressed in **%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Actuators (Hierarchical Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Based on Energy Domain

1. **Electrical Actuators** – use electrical energy directly
   - DC Motors, Servo Motors, Stepper Motors, Solenoids, Relays
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** – use pressurized fluid
   - Hydraulic cylinders, hydraulic motors
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** – use compressed air/gas
   - Pneumatic cylinders, air motors
4. **Thermal Actuators** – use heat-induced expansion
   - Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), bimetallic strips, Peltier modules
5. **Mechanical Actuators** – use stored mechanical energy
   - Springs, rack-and-pinion systems, cams
6. **Magnetic Actuators** – use electromagnetic force
   - Voice coils, linear magnetic actuators

### 2.1.2 Based on Motion Type

- **Linear Actuators** → produce straight-line motion (e.g., solenoid, hydraulic cylinder)
- **Rotary Actuators** → produce rotational motion (e.g., DC motor, stepper motor)

### 2.1.3 Based on Control Type

- **Binary (ON/OFF) Actuators** → two stable states (e.g., relay, buzzer, LED)
- **Proportional Actuators** → continuous variable output (e.g., servo, PWM-driven motor)

## 2.2 Detailed Working Principles of Major IoT Actuators

### 2.2.1 DC Motor (Direct Current Motor)

- Operates on **Lorentz Force Law** — a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force.
- When the armature (rotor) receives DC voltage, the commutator reverses the current direction every half-turn, producing **continuous unidirectional rotation**.

### 2.2.2 Servo Motor

- A closed-loop actuator containing a **DC motor, position sensor (potentiometer), and control circuitry**.
- Receives **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signals** at **50 Hz** (period = **20 ms**).
- Pulse width between **1 ms (0°)** and **2 ms (180°)** sets the shaft angle.
- Internal feedback controller continuously corrects the position — hence the name *servomechanism*.

### 2.2.3 Stepper Motor

- Rotates in **discrete angular steps** (commonly **1.8° per step**, i.e., 200 steps/revolution).
- Driven by a sequence of energised coils — no feedback required (open-loop).
- Excellent for precision positioning (CNC, 3D printers, camera gimbals).

### 2.2.4 Relay (Electromechanical Switch)

- An **electromagnetic switch** where a small control current energises a coil that physically moves a contact.
- Allows a low-power MCU pin (e.g., 5 V, 20 mA) to switch a high-power load (e.g., 230 V AC, 10 A).

### 2.2.5 Solenoid

- A coil wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger.
- Energising the coil creates a magnetic field that **pulls the plunger inward** along the coil's axis.
- Used in door locks, valves, vending machines.

### 2.2.6 Buzzer / Piezo Speaker

- Converts electrical oscillations into audible sound waves using **piezoelectric effect** (or simple electromagnetic vibration).
- Frequency range: typically **2 kHz – 4 kHz** for IoT alert buzzers.

### 2.2.7 LED (Light Emitting Diode)

- A semiconductor diode that emits **photons** when forward-biased.
- Acts as a visual output actuator; can be PWM-modulated to vary brightness.

### 2.2.8 Peltier Module (Thermoelectric Actuator)

- Uses the **Peltier Effect** — passing current through a junction of dissimilar conductors causes one side to heat and the other to cool.
- Used in IoT-controlled portable coolers and temperature chambers.

## 2.3 Why These Actuators Matter in IoT — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility by Domain:**
> - **Smart Agriculture:** DC pump (irrigation), servo (valve control), relay (motor starter)
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Pneumatic cylinders (assembly line), stepper (conveyor)
> - **Smart Home:** Relay (appliance switching), servo (curtains/blinds), buzzer (alarm)
> - **Healthcare Wearables:** Peltier (thermoregulation), vibration motor (haptic feedback)
> - **Smart Vehicles:** Solenoid (locks), stepper (instrument cluster needles), DC motor (fans)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Description | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Force on current-carrying conductor** | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Lorentz force on motor armature | **N (Newton)** |
| 2 | **Torque of DC Motor** | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | Proportional to armature current | **N·m** |
| 3 | **Back-EMF** | $V_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Opposes applied voltage | **V (Volt)** |
| 4 | **Speed of DC Motor** | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I \cdot R_a}{K_e}$ | No-load to full-load speed | **rad/s** |
| 5 | **Servo Angle from PWM** | $\theta = \dfrac{(t_{pulse} - t_{min})}{t_{max} - t_{min}} \times \theta_{range}$ | Linear mapping of pulse width to angle | **Degrees** |
| 6 | **Stepper Step Resolution** | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_{step}}$ | Number of steps per revolution | **integer** |
| 7 | **Stepper Step Angle** | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$ | Angle per step (typical 1.8°) | **Degrees** |
| 8 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Controls average power delivered | **%** |
| 9 | **Solenoid Force (approx.)** | $F \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$ | Where $g$ is air gap length | **N** |
| 10 | **Power Dissipated in Actuator** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical input power | **W (Watt)** |
| 11 | **Hydraulic Cylinder Force** | $F = P \cdot A$ | Pressure × Piston area | **N** |
| 12 | **Pneumatic Cylinder Force** | $F = P \cdot A$ | Compressed air pressure × area | **N** |
| 13 | **Efficiency of Actuator** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Mechanical output / Electrical input | **%** |
| 14 | **Peltier Heat Pump Rate** | $Q_c = S \cdot T_c \cdot I - \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed at cold side | **W** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For the 14-mark questions, students who can map the *physical principle* (e.g., Lorentz force, Peltier effect) to a *real IoT use-case* (e.g., "Why is a DC motor used in a smart fan?") score higher than those who only list definitions.

## 2.5 Selection Criteria for IoT Actuators (Decision Matrix)

| Criterion | Question to Ask | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Voltage Compatibility** | Is it 3.3 V or 5 V logic-compatible? | MCU pin protection |
| **Current Draw** | Does the MCU pin source enough current? | Need for driver IC (e.g., L298N) |
| **Precision Required** | Open-loop (stepper) or closed-loop (servo)? | Application accuracy |
| **Response Time** | How fast must it act? | Real-time constraints |
| **Power Source** | Battery or mains? | Energy budget |
| **Duty Cycle** | Continuous or intermittent? | Heat dissipation |
| **Environmental** | Indoor, outdoor, IP rating? | Reliability |
| **Form Factor** | Size and weight limits? | Edge device constraints |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Servo Motor Angle from PWM Pulse Width

### Problem Statement
A standard hobby servo motor receives a PWM signal at 50 Hz (period $T = 20$ ms). The servo positions its shaft at 0° for a pulse width of 1 ms, and at 180° for a pulse width of 2 ms. Find the shaft angle $\theta$ for a pulse width of 1.5 ms.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**

$$
T_{period} = 20 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{min} = 1 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{max} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\theta_{range} = 180^{\circ} - 0^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the linear mapping formula**

$$
\theta = \frac{(t_{pulse} - t_{min})}{t_{max} - t_{min}} \times \theta_{range}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$
\theta = \frac{(1.5 - 1)}{(2 - 1)} \times 180^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the fraction**

$$
\theta = \frac{0.5}{1.0} \times 180^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation**

$$
\theta = 0.5 \times 180^{\circ} = 90^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 1.5 ms pulse positions the servo shaft at **90°**, which is the neutral mid-point. This property is widely used in robotic arms and pan-tilt camera mounts in IoT surveillance systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stepper Motor Resolution for a CNC Plotter

### Problem Statement
A 3D printer / CNC plotter uses a stepper motor with a step angle of $\theta_{step} = 1.8^{\circ}$. A lead screw with pitch $p = 8$ mm is attached to the motor. Calculate (a) the number of steps per revolution, and (b) the linear displacement per step.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Part (a): Steps per revolution**

$$
N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_{step}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}}
$$

$$
N_{steps} = 200 \text{ steps/revolution}
$$

**Step 2 — Part (b): Linear displacement per step**

The lead screw converts one full revolution into a linear travel equal to the pitch $p$.

$$
\Delta x_{per\,step} = \frac{p}{N_{steps}} = \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{200}
$$

$$
\Delta x_{per\,step} = 0.04 \text{ mm} = 40 \text{ \mu m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This 40 µm resolution explains why stepper motors dominate in 3D printers and CNC plotters used in IoT-based smart manufacturing. The IoT processor (e.g., ESP32) sends a precise number of step pulses to achieve exact XY positioning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relay Coil Current and Snubber Design

### Problem Statement
A 5 V relay coil has a resistance of $R_{coil} = 200\ \Omega$ and an inductance $L = 50$ mH. The MCU GPIO drives it directly. (a) Find the steady-state coil current. (b) Find the time constant and the current after $t = 75$ ms.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Part (a): Steady-state current**

At steady state, the inductor behaves as a short circuit, so:

$$
I_{ss} = \frac{V}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{200\ \Omega} = 0.025 \text{ A} = 25 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Part (b): Time constant**

$$
\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}}{200\ \Omega} = 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Current at $t = 75$ ms**

First, compute the number of time constants:

$$
n = \frac{t}{\tau} = \frac{75 \text{ ms}}{0.25 \text{ ms}} = 300
$$

Since $n = 300$ is very large, the current is essentially at steady state:

$$
I(t) = I_{ss} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 25 \text{ mA} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-300}\right)
$$

$$
I(t) \approx 25 \text{ mA} \cdot (1 - 0) \approx 25 \text{ mA}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common mistake is to think the inductor limits the steady-state current. Inductance only affects the *transient* response. Once $t \gg \tau$, current is governed purely by coil resistance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hydraulic Cylinder Force Calculation

### Problem Statement
A hydraulic actuator (IoT-controlled industrial press) has a piston diameter $D = 50$ mm and operates at a fluid pressure of $P = 2$ MPa. Calculate the linear force exerted.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the piston area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pressure–force relation**

$$
F = P \cdot A = (2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Final computation**

$$
F = 3927 \text{ N} \approx 3.93 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hydraulic actuators can multiply a small input force from a pump into a multi-kilonewton output, making them indispensable in heavy IoT-driven industrial robotics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Actuator Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Actuators in IoT] --> B1[By Energy Domain]
    A1 --> B2[By Motion Type]
    A1 --> B3[By Control Type]

    B1 --> C1[Electrical]
    B1 --> C2[Hydraulic]
    B1 --> C3[Pneumatic]
    B1 --> C4[Thermal]
    B1 --> C5[Magnetic]
    B1 --> C6[Mechanical]

    C1 --> D1[DC Motor]
    C1 --> D2[Servo Motor]
    C1 --> D3[Stepper Motor]
    C1 --> D4[Relay]
    C1 --> D5[Solenoid]
    C1 --> D6[LED and Buzzer]

    C4 --> D7[Peltier Module]
    C4 --> D8[Shape Memory Alloy]

    B2 --> E1[Linear Actuators]
    B2 --> E2[Rotary Actuators]
    E1 --> F1[Solenoid, Hydraulic Cylinder]
    E2 --> F2[DC, Servo, Stepper]

    B3 --> G1[Binary ON OFF]
    B3 --> G2[Proportional]
    G1 --> H1[Relay, Buzzer, LED]
    G2 --> H2[Servo, PWM Motor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: IoT Sense–Compute–Actuate Loop (with Actuator Position)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Physical World] --> S2[Sensor Input Layer]
    S2 --> S3[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    S3 --> S4[Microcontroller or Edge Processor]
    S4 --> S5{Decision Logic}
    S5 --> S6[Driver Circuit]
    S6 --> A1[Actuator Output Layer]
    A1 --> S1

    subgraph ACTUATOR_BLOCK
        A2[Relay Module] --> A3[High Power Load]
        A4[Motor Driver L298N] --> A5[DC Motor]
        A6[Direct PWM Pin] --> A7[Servo Motor]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Actuator Interface

```mermaid
graph TD
    MCU[Microcontroller GPIO Pin] --> DRV[Driver Stage]
    MCU --> PWM[PWM Generator]
    DRV --> ISO[Isolation and Protection]
    ISO --> ACT[Actuator Element]
    ACT --> LD[Mechanical Load]

    DRV --> R1[Transistor BJT or MOSFET]
    ISO --> R2[Optocoupler or Relay Coil]
    ACT --> R3[Motor or Solenoid or Heater]
    PWM --> R4[Servo or LED Dimming]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Selection Decision Tree for Choosing an IoT Actuator

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Need Motion?] --> Q2{Type of Motion}
    Q2 -->|Linear| L1[Solenoid or Linear Actuator]
    Q2 -->|Rotary| R1[DC Servo or Stepper]

    Q1 --> Q3{Need Precision?}
    R1 -->|Yes Open Loop| R2[Stepper Motor]
    R1 -->|Yes Closed Loop| R3[Servo Motor]
    R1 -->|No| R4[DC Motor]

    Q1 --> Q4{Switching High Power Load?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| H1[Electromagnetic Relay]
    Q4 -->|No| H2[Transistor Switch]

    Q1 --> Q5{Need Sound or Light Alert?}
    Q5 -->|Sound| AL1[Buzzer or Piezo Speaker]
    Q5 -->|Light| AL2[LED or Display]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define an actuator in the context of IoT. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
An actuator is an output device that converts a control signal from an IoT processing unit into a physical action. It is the execution component of the *Sense–Compute–Actuate* paradigm.

**Examples:**
1. **DC Motor** – used in smart fans and robotic vehicles to produce rotational motion.
2. **Electromagnetic Relay** – used in home automation to switch household appliances on/off.

*(Word limit guideline for 3-mark answer: 60–80 words, plus 1 diagram if relevant.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servo motor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Control type | Open-loop | Closed-loop (with feedback) |
| Motion | Discrete steps (1.8° typical) | Continuous within 0°–180° |
| Position sensor | Not required | Built-in potentiometer |
| Holding torque | High at standstill | Moderate |
| Typical use | 3D printers, CNC | Robotic arms, camera gimbals |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently *list* actuator types without explaining the **working principle**. Always mention the underlying physics (e.g., Lorentz force, electromagnetism, Peltier effect).
> - Avoid generic statements like "motor is used in robotics." Be specific: *"A DC motor with L298N driver is used in IoT-based line-follower bots to drive wheels."*
> - For 7-mark sub-parts, **diagrams are mandatory**. A missing block diagram can cost 1–2 marks.
