> - Always state **standard values** (e.g., servo PWM = 50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse) — examiners reward numerical specificity.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **DC motor** as an IoT actuator. Derive the relationship between applied voltage, back-EMF, and angular speed. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe how a **servo motor** is interfaced with an ESP32 microcontroller in an IoT-based automated door lock system. Show the PWM signal mapping. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — DC Motor Working Principle and Equation

**Step 1 — Physical Principle**
A DC motor operates on the **Lorentz Force Law**. When a current-carrying armature conductor is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, it experiences a force:

$$
F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

This tangential force, acting at the radius of the armature, produces a torque $\tau$ that rotates the shaft.

**Step 2 — Equivalent Circuit**
The DC motor is modelled as a series combination of armature resistance $R_a$, armature inductance $L_a$, and a back-EMF source $V_b$:

$$
V = I \cdot R_a + L_a \cdot \frac{dI}{dt} + V_b
$$

**Step 3 — Back-EMF Relation**
As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it itself cuts flux lines and generates a back-EMF:

$$
V_b = K_e \cdot \omega
$$

where $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 4 — Steady-State Speed Equation**
At steady state, $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$
V = I \cdot R_a + K_e \cdot \omega
$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$
\omega = \frac{V - I \cdot R_a}{K_e}
$$

**Step 5 — Torque Equation**
The electromagnetic torque developed is proportional to armature current:

$$
\tau = K_t \cdot I
$$

where $K_t$ is the torque constant (in SI units, $K_t = K_e$).

**Step 6 — IoT Context**
In an IoT system, the **MCU cannot directly drive a DC motor** due to current limitations. An **H-bridge driver IC like L298N** is used. The MCU sends a PWM signal on the enable pin to vary speed, and logic on input pins to vary direction. The motor is typically used to drive fans, pumps, or wheels in IoT nodes.

**Final Formula to Remember:**

$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{V - I \cdot R_a}{K_e}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\tau = K_t \cdot I}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Servo Motor Interfacing with ESP32

**Step 1 — System Overview**
The IoT-based automated door lock uses:
- **ESP32** as the Wi-Fi-enabled MCU
- **Servo Motor (SG90)** as the actuator to rotate the lock bolt
- **Cloud trigger** (e.g., MQTT message from mobile app) to open/close the door

**Step 2 — Block Diagram**

```
Mobile App --[Wi-Fi/MQTT]--> ESP32 --[PWM 50 Hz]--> Servo (SG90) --[Shaft]--> Lock Bolt
```

**Step 3 — PWM Signal Specification**
The ESP32's LEDC (LED Control) peripheral generates a 50 Hz PWM signal on a GPIO pin (e.g., GPIO 13). Pulse widths:
- **1.0 ms → 0°** (lock closed)
- **1.5 ms → 90°** (mid-position, unlock transition)
- **2.0 ms → 180°** (lock fully open)

**Step 4 — Signal Calculation**
Using the linear mapping formula:

$$
\theta = \frac{t_{pulse} - 1 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} \times 180^{\circ}
$$

For $t_{pulse} = 1.5$ ms:

$$
\theta = \frac{1.5 - 1}{1} \times 180^{\circ} = 90^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Considerations**
The SG90 servo draws up to **~200 mA** under load. The ESP32's 3.3 V pin can supply only ~40 mA, so an **external 5 V supply** is required, with the **GND tied to ESP32's GND** for a common reference.

**Step 6 — IoT Flow**
1. User taps "Unlock" on mobile app.
2. App publishes MQTT message to broker (e.g., `iot/home/door/cmd`).
3. ESP32 subscribes, receives `unlock`.
4. ESP32 sets servo PWM to 2.0 ms → shaft rotates 180° → door opens.
5. After 5 s timeout, servo returns to 1.0 ms → door locks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Classify IoT actuators based on energy domain. Explain the working of a **relay** and a **Peltier module** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of a **solenoid actuator**. Show how it is used in an IoT-based smart irrigation valve system. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — Classification, Relay, and Peltier

**Step 1 — Classification by Energy Domain**

| Class | Energy Source | IoT Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrical | Electric current | DC motor, relay, solenoid |
| Hydraulic | Pressurized fluid | Industrial press cylinders |
| Pneumatic | Compressed air | Pneumatic grippers |
| Thermal | Heat / temperature | Peltier, SMA wires |
| Magnetic | Electromagnetic field | Voice coil, linear mag. actuator |
| Mechanical | Stored mechanical energy | Spring, cam, rack-pinion |

**Step 2 — Relay Working**
A relay is an **electromagnetic switch**. It consists of:
- A **coil** (electromagnet)
- A **movable armature** with electrical contacts
- A **spring** to restore the contact when de-energised

When current flows through the coil, it produces a magnetic field that attracts the armature, closing (or opening) the contacts. This allows a low-power MCU signal to switch a high-power load.

**Electrical equivalent:**

$$
I_{coil} = \frac{V_{cc}}{R_{coil}}
$$

For a 5 V relay with $R = 200\ \Omega$:

$$
I_{coil} = \frac{5}{200} = 25 \text{ mA}
$$

**IoT use-case:** Smart plug controlling a 230 V lamp from an ESP32.

**Step 3 — Peltier Module Working**
A Peltier module (TEC) is a **solid-state thermoelectric device** consisting of p- and n-type semiconductor pellets connected electrically in series and thermally in parallel. When DC current passes through:

- One side absorbs heat → **cold side** ($T_c$)
- The other side releases heat → **hot side** ($T_h$)

The governing equation for the heat absorbed at the cold side:

$$
Q_c = S \cdot T_c \cdot I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 \cdot R - K \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $S$ = Seebeck coefficient
- $R$ = electrical resistance
- $K$ = thermal conductance
- $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$

**IoT use-case:** Portable IoT-controlled mini-refrigerator for vaccine transport in smart healthcare logistics.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Solenoid and Smart Irrigation

**Step 1 — Solenoid Definition**
A solenoid is a **cylindrical coil of wire** that produces a uniform magnetic field along its axis when energised. A movable ferromagnetic plunger placed inside the coil experiences an axial force and is pulled inward.

**Step 2 — Force Equation**

$$
F \approx \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of turns
- $I$ = coil current
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of plunger
- $g$ = air-gap length
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space $\approx 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

**Step 3 — Solenoid Valve Construction**
A **solenoid valve** combines a solenoid with a fluid passage. When energised, the plunger opens (or closes) the valve port, allowing or blocking water flow.

**Step 4 — Smart Irrigation System**

```
Soil Moisture Sensor --> ESP32 --> Relay Driver --> Solenoid Valve --> Water Pipe --> Crop Field
                                   (Wi-Fi Cloud)        (12V DC)         (ON/OFF)
```

**Step 5 — Operating Logic**
1. **Soil moisture sensor** reads volumetric water content (VWC) in %.
2. ESP32 reads the value and compares with a threshold (e.g., 30%).
3. If VWC < 30% → ESP32 sends HIGH to relay driver → **solenoid valve opens** → irrigation starts.
4. If VWC ≥ 30% → relay de-energises → **valve closes**.
5. Farmer can override remotely via a **Blynk / MQTT dashboard**.

**Step 6 — Selection Notes**
- Use a **latching solenoid valve** to save power (only consumes current during state change).
- Add a **flyback diode** across the solenoid coil to protect the MCU from inductive kickback.

**Final Formula Recap:**

$$
\boxed{F \approx \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Actuators**

- ✅ **Actuator = output transducer** — converts electrical/control signal into physical action; the "muscle" of an IoT system.
- ✅ **Three major classifications:** Energy domain (Electrical/Hydraulic/Pneumatic/Thermal/Magnetic/Mechanical), Motion (Linear/Rotary), Control (Binary/Proportional).
- ✅ **DC Motor:** Lorentz force principle. $\omega = \dfrac{V - I R_a}{K_e}$, $\tau = K_t I$. Needs **H-bridge driver (L298N)** for direction control.
- ✅ **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop, 50 Hz PWM, 1–2 ms pulse → 0°–180°. **PWM frequency = 50 Hz**; period = **20 ms**.
- ✅ **Stepper Motor:** Open-loop, $N_{steps} = 360°/\theta_{step}$. Typical $\theta_{step} = 1.8°$ → 200 steps/rev.
- ✅ **Relay:** Electromagnetic switch. MCU pin (5 V, 25 mA typical) controls high-voltage load. Always use a **flyback diode**.
- ✅ **Solenoid:** Coil + plunger; force $F \propto N^2 I^2 / g^2$. Used in valves, locks, vending machines.
- ✅ **Peltier Module:** Solid-state heat pump. Cold side absorbs $Q_c = S T_c I - \tfrac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Buzzer / LED / Display:** Simple ON/OFF or PWM-modulated visual/audio actuators.
- ✅ **PWM Duty Cycle** $D = t_{on}/T \times 100\%$ — controls average power to proportional actuators.
- ✅ **Power = $V \cdot I$** — verify MCU can source enough current; use **driver circuits** otherwise.
- ✅ **Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinders:** $F = P \cdot A$ — high-force industrial IoT applications.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Selection criteria:** voltage, current, precision, response time, duty cycle, environment, form factor.
- ✅ **Always add protection:** flyback diodes for inductive loads, optocouplers for isolation, fuses for over-current.
- ✅ **IoT context to mention in answers:** smart home, smart agriculture, IIoT, healthcare wearables, smart vehicles.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Actuators Study Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PCADT602 — Internet of Things*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Smart Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Smart Object** is an autonomous physical or virtual entity that is augmented with **sensing, processing, networking, and actuation capabilities**, enabling it to perceive its environment, make localized decisions, and participate as an active node within an **Internet of Things (IoT)** ecosystem.

According to the IoT reference architecture defined by **ITU-T Y.4000/Y.2060**, a smart object is the most fundamental atomic unit of an IoT system — the "thing" that bridges the gap between the physical world and the digital/cyber world.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Smart Object is a sensor-equipped, microprocessor-based device with communication ability, a unique identifier, and some level of embedded intelligence that allows it to sense, interpret, reason, and react to its local context and exchange data over a network."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **regular wristwatch** versus a **modern smartwatch**:

| Feature | Regular Watch (Dumb Object) | Smart Watch (Smart Object) |
|---|---|---|
| Tells time | ✅ | ✅ |
| Knows its identity (unique serial) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Sense heart rate / steps | ❌ | ✅ |
| Connects to Wi-Fi/Bluetooth | ❌ | ✅ |
| Runs small programs (apps) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Sends data to the cloud | ❌ | ✅ |
| Acts (vibrates, lights up) | Limited | ✅ Programmatically |

A **Smart Object** is like giving "senses" (sensors), a "brain" (microcontroller), a "voice" (communication module), and "muscles" (actuators) to an otherwise passive item — be it a coffee mug, a streetlight, a heart pacemaker, or a shipping container.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Realization for Students:** The IoT is not *about* the internet — it is *about* these **smart objects** that bring physical-world phenomena into the digital realm. The internet is merely the transport medium.

## 1.3 The Six Core Properties of a Smart Object (KTU High-Yield)

Every smart object must possess these six properties. KTU examiners frequently ask for this list:

1. **Identity** — A unique global identifier (UID) like an **IPv6 address**, **EPC (Electronic Product Code)**, or **MAC address**.
2. **Sensing Capability** — Ability to measure physical parameters using sensors (temperature, pressure, motion, light, etc.).
3. **Actuation Capability** — Ability to influence the physical world via actuators (motors, valves, LEDs, buzzers).
4. **Processing / Embedded Intelligence** — A microcontroller/MCU runs firmware that interprets sensor data and makes decisions.
5. **Communication Interface** — Wireless (BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, NFC) or wired (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN) connectivity.
6. **Power Source** — Battery, energy harvesting, or mains supply providing necessary energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "I-SAP-CP"**: **I**dentity, **S**ensing, **A**ctuation, **P**rocessing, **C**ommunication, **P**ower.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Unique Identifiers**: **IPv6 address (128-bit)** or **EPC-96 (96-bit)** tag for object addressing.
- **Wireless Communication Bands**: **2.4 GHz (ISM band)**, **868/915 MHz (Sub-GHz)**, **5 GHz (Wi-Fi)**.
- **Typical Power Budget**: **µW to mW** for battery-operated smart objects.
- **Sampling Theorem Constant**: **2 (Nyquist factor)** — sampling rate must be at least 2× the highest signal frequency.
- **ADC Resolution Range**: Typically **8-bit to 24-bit** depending on application precision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Smart Object

A smart object is typically organized into **four functional layers**:

1. **Hardware Layer (Physical Resources)**
   - Microcontroller Unit (MCU) — e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+, ESP32, ATmega328P
   - Memory — ROM (firmware), RAM (runtime data), EEPROM/Flash (persistent state)
   - **Sensors** — transducers converting physical phenomena to electrical signals
   - **Actuators** — transducers converting electrical signals to physical action
   - **Communication Module** — radio transceiver (e.g., nRF24L01, CC2530, ESP8266)
   - **Power Subsystem** — battery + DC-DC regulator + optional energy harvester

2. **Software / Firmware Layer**
   - Bare-metal firmware or RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, Contiki, RIOT OS)
   - Drivers for peripherals (I²C, SPI, ADC, GPIO)
   - Application logic (sense → process → decide → act → transmit)

3. **Communication Layer**
   - Implements one or more protocols (BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, MQTT, CoAP)
   - Handles packetization, addressing, security (AES-128, TLS)

4. **Application / Service Layer**
   - Defines the object's "purpose" (e.g., a smart thermostat maintains room temperature)
   - Often interfaces with cloud backends via RESTful APIs or message brokers.

## 2.2 Sensor Operating Principle — The Why & How

**Why sensors?** Without sensors, a smart object is blind to its environment. The IoT's foundational value — *bringing the physical world online* — depends entirely on sensors.

**How they work (operational pipeline):**
- **Step 1:** Physical phenomenon (temperature, light, vibration) acts on the sensing element.
- **Step 2:** The element produces an analog electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance change).
- **Step 3:** **Signal Conditioning** amplifies and filters the signal.
- **Step 4:** **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** converts to discrete digital code.
- **Step 5:** MCU processes digital value using calibration constants.
- **Step 6:** Decision logic decides whether to store, transmit, or actuate.

## 2.3 Actuator Operating Principle — Closing the Loop

**Why actuators?** They give the smart object the ability to *change* the physical world (the "effect" arm of the sense-think-act loop).

**Common actuators:** DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors, solenoids, piezoelectric transducers, LEDs, buzzers, relays, hydraulic/pneumatic valves.

**Drive Signal Types:**
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** — for variable-speed motors, dimmable LEDs.
- **PWM Frequency Range**: Typically **100 Hz to 25 kHz**.
- **Digital On/Off** — for relays and simple switching.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_m$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency; $f_m$ = max signal frequency | Hz |
| 2 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1}$ | $\Delta$ = LSB step size; $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage; $N$ = ADC bits | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | $\Delta Y$ = change in output; $\Delta X$ = change in input | V/unit or mV/unit |
| 4 | Battery Life Estimate | $L = \dfrac{C}{I_{avg}}$ | $L$ = life in hours; $C$ = capacity; $I_{avg}$ = average current draw | hours |
| 5 | Data Rate (Single Channel) | $R = N \cdot f_s$ | $R$ = data rate; $N$ = bits per sample; $f_s$ = sampling rate | bits/sec (bps) |
| 6 | Memory Requirement | $M = R \cdot T = N \cdot f_s \cdot T$ | $T$ = total recording time; $M$ = memory size | bits |
| 7 | Friis Path Loss | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P_r$ = received; $P_t$ = transmitted; $G_t$, $G_r$ = antenna gains; $\lambda$ = wavelength; $d$ = distance | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Link Budget | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} - L_{misc}$ | $L_{path}$ = path loss; $L_{misc}$ = connector/cable losses | dBm |
| 9 | Sense-Compute-Transmit Energy | $E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm}$ | Energy consumed in each subsystem | Joules (J) |
| 10 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | Ratio of largest to smallest detectable signal | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse ADC resolution $\Delta$ with sensor resolution. ADC resolution depends ONLY on bit-depth $N$ and $V_{ref}$. Sensor resolution depends on transducer physics AND the ADC. Both are independent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Smart Object Application | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Wearable ECG patch | Continuous cardiac monitoring with on-device arrhythmia detection |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture + pH sensor node | Precision irrigation, reducing water waste by up to **40%** |
| **Industrial IoT** | Vibration sensor on motor bearings | Predictive maintenance, prevents catastrophic failure |
| **Smart Cities** | Smart streetlight with PIR + lux sensor | Adaptive lighting reduces municipal energy consumption by **30–60%** |
| **Logistics** | RFID-tagged shipping container | Real-time cold-chain monitoring for pharmaceuticals |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Life of a Smart Object

**Problem:** A smart object has an average current consumption of **$I_{avg} = 15 \text{ mA}$** and is powered by a **$C = 2400 \text{ mAh}$** lithium battery. Estimate its operational lifetime in hours and days.

**Starting Equation:**
$$L = \frac{C}{I_{avg}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify Units**

The capacity $C$ is expressed in **mAh (milliamp-hours)**. The current $I_{avg}$ is in **mA (milliamps)**. Dividing mAh by mA yields time in **hours**, which is dimensionally consistent.

$$L = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{15 \text{ mA}}$$

**Step 2 — Perform Division**

$$L = 2400 \div 15 = 160 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Days**

$$L_{days} = \frac{L}{24} = \frac{160}{24} \approx 6.67 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Adjustment (Duty Cycle Correction)**

In reality, the smart object **sleeps** most of the time. If duty cycle $D = 1\%$ (active 1% of the time), then:

$$I_{eff} = I_{active} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

Assuming $I_{sleep} = 5 \text{ µA}$:

$$I_{eff} = 15 \text{ mA} \cdot 0.01 + 0.005 \text{ mA} \cdot 0.99 = 0.15 + 0.00495 \approx 0.155 \text{ mA}$$

$$L_{new} = \frac{2400}{0.155} \approx 15{,}484 \text{ hours} \approx 1.77 \text{ years}$$

This demonstrates why **duty cycling** is critical to smart object longevity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** If the question does NOT mention duty cycle, assume continuous operation unless stated otherwise. Marks are awarded for explicitly mentioning the assumption.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Resolution Calculation

**Problem:** An MCU has a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. Find the voltage represented by 1 LSB.

**Starting Equation:**
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Values**

$$N = 12 \implies 2^{12} = 4096 \implies 2^{12} - 1 = 4095$$

$$\Delta = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{4095}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Numerically**

$$\Delta = 8.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 805.8 \text{ µV per LSB}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

Each step of the ADC corresponds to approximately **806 µV**. Any analog voltage change smaller than this cannot be resolved — this is the **quantization limit** of the system.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensor Resolution with Sensitivity

**Problem:** A temperature sensor has a linear output: $V_{out} = 0.01 \cdot T + 0.5$ (V), where $T$ is in °C. If the ADC has 10-bit resolution with $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$, calculate the **temperature resolution** of the entire smart object (smallest detectable temperature change).

**Step 1 — Find ADC Voltage Resolution**

$$\Delta V_{ADC} = \frac{5}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5}{1023} \approx 4.887 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Find Sensor Sensitivity**

From $V_{out} = 0.01 \cdot T + 0.5$, the slope is:
$$S = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} = 0.01 \text{ V/°C} = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Overall Temperature Resolution**

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta V_{ADC}}{S} = \frac{4.887 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} \approx 0.489 \text{ °C per LSB}$$

**Step 4 — Verification**

A 10-bit ADC paired with a 10 mV/°C sensor can detect a temperature change of about **0.49 °C** — sufficient for room climate monitoring but inadequate for medical-grade fever detection (which needs **≤ 0.1 °C**).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sampling Rate and Data Rate

**Problem:** A vibration sensor monitors a motor bearing. The highest relevant frequency is $f_m = 500 \text{ Hz}$. The MCU samples with 16-bit resolution on a single channel. Find the minimum sampling rate and the resulting data rate.

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist Theorem**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_m = 2 \cdot 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

Choosing a safety factor of 2.5×:

$$f_s = 2500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Data Rate**

$$R = N \cdot f_s = 16 \cdot 2500 = 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Storage for 1 Hour of Monitoring**

$$M = R \cdot T = 40{,}000 \cdot 3600 = 1.44 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits} \approx 18 \text{ MB/hour}$$

This shows why edge-processing (on-sensor analytics) is preferred — transmitting raw 40 kbps continuously drains battery fast.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Smart Object Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Reduces When... | Increases When... | KTU-Relevant Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Consumption** | Sampling rate ↓, duty cycle ↓, radio duty cycle ↓ | Higher $f_s$, always-on radio | Battery life extension |
| **Latency** | Faster MCU, edge processing | Cloud-only decision making | Real-time control loops |
| **Cost** | Lower-bit ADC, simpler radio | High-precision sensors | Large-scale deployment feasibility |
| **Data Volume** | On-board compression, feature extraction | Raw stream transmission | Bandwidth / cloud-storage cost |
| **Security** | Symmetric crypto, hardware roots-of-trust | Plain-text transmission | Tampering / privacy risk |
| **Accuracy** | More bits, calibration, sensor fusion | Single noisy sensor | Application correctness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smart Object Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] -->|Stimulus| B[Sensor Array]
    B -->|Analog Signal| C[Signal Conditioning]
    C -->|Clean Analog| D[ADC]
    D -->|Digital Data| E[Microcontroller MCU]
    F[Firmware Storage ROM] --> E
    G[RAM] <--> E
    E -->|Decision| H[Actuator Driver]
    H -->|Drive Signal| I[Actuators]
    I -->|Physical Effect| A
    E -->|Packetized Data| J[Comm Module Radio]
    J -->|Wireless Waves| K[Network Cloud]
    L[Power Subsystem Battery Harvester] -.-> B
    L -.-> C
    L -.-> D
    L -.-> E
    L -.-> H
    L -.-> J
    style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style K fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style L fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style E fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#3f6212
```

## 4.2 The Sense-Think-Act Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1 SENSE] --> P2[Stage 2 ACQUIRE and CONVERT ADC]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 PROCESS on MCU]
    P3 --> P4{Stage 4 DECIDE Threshold Check}
    P4 -- Condition Met --> P5[Stage 5 ACT via Actuator]
    P4 -- Condition Not Met --> P6[Stage 6 TRANSMIT Data to Cloud]
    P5 --> P7[Stage 7 LOG / SLEEP]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style P3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style P5 fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
    style P6 fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3
```

## 4.3 Smart Object Connectivity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0 Physical World]
    L1[Layer 1 Smart Object Sensor MCU Actuator]
    L2[Layer 2 Edge Gateway Aggregator]
    L3[Layer 3 Network Internet Core]
    L4[Layer 4 Cloud Backend and Analytics]
    L5[Layer 5 Application Layer User Dashboard]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 -->|BLE ZigBee LoRa| L2
    L2 -->|Wi-Fi Ethernet Cellular| L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    style L1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    style L5 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Power-State State Machine (Energy Perspective)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SleepState
    SleepState --> WakeUpState: Timer Interrupt
    WakeUpState --> SenseState: ADC Read
    SenseState --> ProcessState: Data Ready
    ProcessState --> TransmitState: Threshold Breached
    ProcessState --> SleepState: No Event
    TransmitState --> SleepState: ACK Received
    SenseState --> SleepState: Under Threshold
    SleepState --> [*]: Battery Dead
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include a **power/energy perspective** in any smart object design question. State machines that demonstrate duty-cycling score high marks.

## 4.5 Smart Object Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SO[Smart Objects]
    SO --> P[Physical Smart Objects]
    SO --> V[Virtual Smart Objects]
    P --> P1[Reactive Objects Sensor Only]
    P --> P2[Interactive Objects Sensor plus Actuator]
    P --> P3[Autonomous Objects Self Powered Decision]
    V --> V1[Digital Twin of Physical Object]
    V --> V2[Virtual Sensor Simulated Data Source]
    V --> V3[Software Agent Web Service Bot]
    style P3 fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
    style V1 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a smart object. List any four essential characteristics of a smart object used in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A *smart object* is a sensor- and actuator-equipped, microprocessor-based device that possesses a unique identifier, embedded processing, and communication capability, enabling it to perceive its environment, make decisions, and interact with other entities over a network.

**Four essential characteristics:**
1. **Identity** — possesses a unique address (e.g., IPv6, EPC).
2. **Sensing/Actuation** — interacts with physical environment.
3. **Embedded Processing** — runs local firmware for decision making.
4. **Communication** — transmits/receives data via wired or wireless protocols.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Physical → Electrical (input) | Electrical → Physical (output) |
| **Function** | Measures a physical quantity | Produces a physical effect |
| **Example** | LM35 temperature sensor | DC motor / Servo motor |
| **Quantity type** | Detects changes | Causes changes |
| **Power** | Generally passive | Requires drive power |

**Mnemonic:** *Sensors **SENSE** (input), Actuators **ACT** (output).*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned to Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a smart object. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A smart object's internal architecture consists of the following interconnected functional blocks:

1. **Sensor Subsystem** — Transducers (e.g., DHT22, MPU6050) that convert physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, acceleration) into analog electrical signals. A **signal conditioning** stage amplifies/filter the signal.

2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Converts the conditioned analog signal into digital code for the MCU. Resolution is typically **10–16 bits**.

3. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** — The brain of the smart object. Executes firmware that reads sensor values, applies calibration, makes threshold decisions, and orchestrates actuation/communication. Examples: ESP32, STM32, ATmega328P.

4. **Memory** — ROM/Flash stores firmware; RAM holds runtime data; EEPROM stores persistent state.

5. **Communication Module** — Radio transceiver (BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa) or wired interface (CAN, RS485) enabling data exchange with edge gateways or cloud servers.

6. **Actuator Subsystem** — Includes a driver circuit (e.g., H-bridge, MOSFET driver) and the actuator itself (motor, relay, valve, LED) producing a physical effect.

7. **Power Subsystem** — Battery, voltage regulator, and optionally an energy harvester (solar, vibration, thermoelectric).

8. **Clock & Reset Circuitry** — Crystal oscillator and watchdog timer for stable operation.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Closing Loop:** Sensor → ADC → MCU → Actuator → Physical Effect → Sensor (continuous sense-act loop).

---

**(b)** A smart object uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. It monitors temperature via a sensor whose output follows $V_{out} = 0.01 \cdot T + 0.5$ V (T in °C).
   (i) Compute the ADC voltage resolution (1 LSB). **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) Compute the temperature resolution of the entire system. **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) ADC Voltage Resolution:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095}$$

$$\Delta \approx 8.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 805.8 \text{ µV per LSB}$$

**(ii) Temperature Resolution:**

Sensor sensitivity (slope):
$$S = 0.01 \text{ V/°C} = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Overall temperature resolution:
$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta V_{ADC}}{S} = \frac{805.8 \text{ µV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = \frac{0.8058 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$\Delta T \approx 0.0806 \text{ °C per LSB} \approx 0.08 \text{ °C}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This system can resolve temperature changes of about **0.08 °C** — highly suitable for medical and laboratory-grade monitoring.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned to June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** What are the design considerations for building a smart object? Explain any five in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Design Considerations:**

1. **Power Budget & Energy Efficiency** — Smart objects are often battery-operated. Designers must minimize current draw via **duty cycling, low-power sleep modes, and energy harvesting**. Selection of MCU (e.g., MSP430 vs. ESP32) directly impacts battery life.

2. **Size and Form Factor** — For wearables, RFID tags, and biomedical implants, size is critical. Chip-on-board (COB) and System-on-Chip (SoC) solutions like ESP32 minimize footprint.

3. **Processing Capability vs. Cost** — More powerful MCUs cost more. Trade-off between edge-processing intelligence and BOM (Bill of Materials) cost must be optimized.

4. **Connectivity Range & Bandwidth** — Choose radio based on range (BLE ~10 m, Wi-Fi ~50 m, LoRa ~10 km) and bandwidth needs. For periodic low-rate telemetry, LoRa is ideal; for video, Wi-Fi is mandatory.

5. **Security** — Implement **AES-128 encryption**, secure boot, hardware root-of-trust. IoT devices are notoriously vulnerable — Mirai botnet (2016) infected 600,000+ smart cameras.

6. **Scalability** — Smart objects must operate in dense deployments. Address uniqueness (IPv6 over IPv4) and protocol overhead become design drivers.

7. **Environmental Robustness** — IP rating (IP67/IP68), operating temperature range, vibration tolerance — particularly for industrial and outdoor deployments.

8. **Interoperability** — Adherence to standards (oneM2M, Matter, OCF) ensures cross-vendor compatibility.

---

**(b)** A smart object consumes **$I_{active} = 25 \text{ mA}$** when active and **$I_{sleep} = 8 \text{ µA}$** when sleeping. The active duration per cycle is **$t_{on} = 50 \text{ ms}$** and the sleep duration is **$t_{off} = 2 \text{ s}$**. It uses a **$C = 1500 \text{ mAh}$** battery.
   (i) Calculate the duty cycle. **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the effective average current consumption. **(3 Marks)**
   (iii) Estimate the battery lifetime in days. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{0.05}{0.05 + 2} = \frac{0.05}{2.05} \approx 0.02439 = 2.439\%$$

**(ii) Effective Average Current:**

$$I_{eff} = I_{active} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$I_{eff} = 25 \text{ mA} \cdot 0.02439 + 0.008 \text{ mA} \cdot 0.9756$$

$$I_{eff} = 0.6098 + 0.0078 \approx 0.6176 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Battery Lifetime:**

$$L_{hours} = \frac{C}{I_{eff}} = \frac{1500}{0.6176} \approx 2428.5 \text{ hours}$$

$$L_{days} = \frac{2428.5}{24} \approx 101.2 \text{ days} \approx 3.4 \text{ months}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Penalty (–2 Marks):** If you compute $I_{eff}$ in mA but $I_{sleep}$ in µA without converting (e.g., write 0.008 instead of 8 µA converted), you lose 2 marks immediately. Always standardize units first.
> 2. **Duty Cycle Definition (–1 Mark):** Some students define $D = t_{on}/t_{off}$ instead of $t_{on}/(t_{on}+t_{off})$. The correct KTU board definition is the latter.
> 3. **Skipping the Sleep Contribution (–1 Mark):** Neglecting $I_{sleep}$ entirely loses one mark. Even though small, it is non-zero over long sleep durations.
> 4. **ADC Confusion:** When asked about "resolution," specify whether you mean ADC bit-resolution or system-level physical resolution. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart Objects**

- ✅ **Definition:** A *smart object* is a sensor + actuator + MCU + communication + unique ID + power — bridging physical and digital worlds.
- ✅ **Six Core Properties (Mnemonic "I-SAP-CP"):** **I**dentity, **S**ensing, **A**ctuation, **P**rocessing, **C**ommunication, **P**ower.
- ✅ **Architectural Layers:** Hardware → Firmware → Communication → Application.
- ✅ **Sensor Direction:** Physical → Electrical (input). **Actuator Direction:** Electrical → Physical (output).
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_m$ — mandatory for lossless digital sampling.
- ✅ **ADC Resolution Formula:** $\Delta = V_{ref} \div (2^N - 1)$ — memorize this exactly.
- ✅ **Sensitivity Formula:** $S = \Delta Y / \Delta X$ — slope of sensor's transfer characteristic.
- ✅ **Battery Life Formula:** $L = C / I_{avg}$ — convert units to mAh and mA.
- ✅ **Duty Cycling:** $D = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$ — the single most important technique for extending battery life.
- ✅ **Smart Object Categories:** Physical (Reactive, Interactive, Autonomous) vs. Virtual (Digital Twins, Virtual Sensors, Software Agents).
- ✅ **Common Wireless Protocols:** BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NFC — match protocol to range/data-rate/power.
- ✅ **Industrial Tag:** Always associate smart objects with **predictive maintenance, smart agriculture, healthcare wearables, smart cities, and logistics** for applied questions.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Words:** "Embedded intelligence," "Edge processing," "Sense-Think-Act loop," "Duty cycling," "Energy harvesting."
- ✅ **Numerical Defaults:** 12-bit ADC with 3.3 V → $\Delta \approx 806 \text{ µV}$; 10-bit ADC with 5 V → $\Delta \approx 4.89 \text{ mV}$.

> **End of Smart Objects Notes — Module 2 (KTU 2024 Scheme — PCADT602)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed autonomous network of spatially dispersed and dedicated sensors that monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, motion, or pollutants, and cooperatively pass their data through the network to a main location (sink/base station). In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a sensor network acts as the *perception layer* — the foundational data-acquisition substrate that bridges the physical world with the digital, networked world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Sensor Network is a collection of distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that **sense**, **process**, and **communicate** environmental data to a central sink via multi-hop wireless communication, forming the sensing backbone of any IoT deployment.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Sensor Node

Every sensor node (also called a *mote*) typically contains four fundamental sub-systems:

| # | Sub-system | Function |
|---|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Sensing Unit** | Contains the physical sensor + Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) |
| 2 | **Processing Unit** | Microcontroller/Processor + Tiny OS/RTOS for local computation |
| 3 | **Communication Unit** | Radio transceiver (typically IEEE 802.15.4 based) |
| 4 | **Power Unit** | Battery (often supplemented by energy harvesting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Units:** Modern motes also include a **mobilizer** (for moving nodes) and a **location-finding system** (GPS) for position-aware routing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Fire Brigade" Analogy:** Imagine a town of 100 houses spread across a large forest. Each house has a smoke detector. Instead of each detector screaming independently, they form a wireless chain — when **House 1** detects smoke, it whispers to its nearest neighbour, **House 2**, who whispers to **House 3**, and so on, until the alert reaches the **fire station (sink)**. The firefighters not only know there is a fire, but *exactly which house* triggered it and the *intensity* of the smoke. This is precisely what a **WSN does** in an IoT system — turning a forest of isolated sensors into a single intelligent nervous system.

The **critical insight** is that a sensor network is fundamentally a **resource-constrained**, **energy-limited**, **collaborative** system. A single sensor is "dumb"; a network of 1000 sensors, communicating and cooperating, is "intelligent." This is the **network effect** of IoT.

### 1.4 Why Sensor Networks Matter in IoT

- They provide **dense spatial sampling** that single sensors cannot.
- They enable **fault tolerance** through redundancy.
- They support **multi-hop communication** to overcome transmission range limitations.
- They form the **Cyber-Physical bridge** — converting physical phenomena into digital data packets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A sensor node's energy budget is dominated by **wireless communication**, not sensing or computation. Transmitting 1 bit over 100 m consumes as much energy as executing **~1000–3000 instructions** on a typical microcontroller.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network (Layered View)

A WSN protocol stack (compared to the OSI model) typically consists of:

1. **Application Layer** — User-defined sensing tasks (e.g., temperature monitoring, target tracking).
2. **Transport Layer** — Reliable data delivery, congestion control (if needed).
3. **Network Layer** — Routing of sensed data (e.g., RPL — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks).
4. **Data Link Layer** — MAC protocol (e.g., S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4) for medium access.
5. **Physical Layer** — Frequency band selection, modulation, encoding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Management Plane**, **Mobility Management Plane**, and **Task Management Plane** run *across* all layers — managing energy, node mobility, and task distribution respectively.

### 2.2 WSN Network Topologies

- **Star Topology** — All nodes communicate directly with a central base station. Energy efficient for short range, not scalable.
- **Mesh Topology** — Multi-hop, self-healing. Most common in IoT (e.g., ZigBee mesh).
- **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology** — Nodes cluster around cluster heads, which forward to the sink.
- **Hybrid Topology** — Combines star + mesh for performance optimization.

### 2.3 Communication Standards (The KTU Favourites)

| Standard | Frequency Band | Data Rate | Range | Typical IoT Use |
|----------|----------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| **IEEE 802.15.4** | 2.4 GHz (ISM) | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | PHY/MAC for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread |
| **ZigBee** | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Home automation, industrial control |
| **6LoWPAN** | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN |
| **Bluetooth LE (BLE)** | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | 10–50 m | Wearables, beacons |
| **LoRaWAN** | 868/915 MHz | 0.3–50 kbps | 2–10 km | Long-range, low-power outdoor IoT |
| **NB-IoT** | Cellular bands | <200 kbps | <15 km | Cellular LPWAN |

### 2.4 Energy Consumption Model (The Classic Radio Model)

A widely used first-order radio model (Heinzelman et al.) for energy consumed in transmitting a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & \text{if } d < d_0 \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & \text{if } d \geq d_0 \end{cases}$$

Energy consumed in **receiving** a $k$-bit packet:

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

The crossover distance $d_0$ is given by:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---------|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Transmission Energy (free space) | $E_{Tx} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^{2}$ | Energy to send k bits over short distance | Joules (J) |
| Transmission Energy (multi-path) | $E_{Tx} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^{4}$ | Energy to send k bits over long distance | Joules (J) |
| Receive Energy | $E_{Rx} = kE_{elec}$ | Energy to receive k bits | Joules (J) |
| Crossover Distance | $d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Boundary between free-space and multi-path regimes | metres (m) |
| Network Lifetime (worst-case) | $L = E_{initial}/E_{round}$ | Number of rounds until first node dies | rounds |
| Network Coverage (single node) | $A_{node} = \pi r_{s}^{2}$ | Sensing area of one node | $m^{2}$ |
| Minimum nodes for full coverage | $N \geq A_{region}/A_{node}$ | K-coverage requirement | nodes |
| Path Loss (Log-Distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_{0})$ | Signal attenuation with distance n | dB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use absolute value bars** `|x|` inside KTU formula tables. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Precision Agriculture** — Soil moisture and temperature sensors across a farm optimise irrigation.
- **Healthcare** — Wearable body sensor networks (BSN) monitor patients remotely.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Vibration and temperature sensors predict machine failure.
- **Smart Cities** — Air quality, traffic, and noise monitoring across urban areas.
- **Environmental Monitoring** — Forest fire detection, volcano monitoring, flood early warning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Consumption in Multi-Hop vs Direct Transmission

Consider a source node transmitting a $k$-bit packet to a sink located at distance $D$. We compare two strategies.

**Strategy A: Direct Transmission**

The node transmits directly over distance $D$ (assume $D > d_0$, so multi-path model applies):

$$E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4}$$

**Strategy B: Multi-Hop Transmission (n equal hops)**

The path is divided into $n$ equal hops, each of length $D/n$ (assume $D/n < d_0$, so free-space applies). Energy per hop:

$$E_{hop} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^{2}$$

Total energy across $n$ hops (note: receiver energy applies at each intermediate node):

$$E_{multi} = n \cdot \left[ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^{2} \right] + (n-1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Simplification (ignoring receive energy for the first comparison):**

$$E_{multi} \approx n \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n}$$

**Comparison Ratio:**

$$\frac{E_{multi}}{E_{direct}} = \frac{n \cdot E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot D^{2}/n}{E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4}}$$

**Finding the optimal number of hops** by differentiating $E_{multi}$ w.r.t. $n$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{multi}}{dn} = k \cdot E_{elec} - k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n^{2}} = 0$$

Solving:

$$n_{opt} = \frac{D}{d_0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n_{opt} = \frac{D}{d_{0}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The optimal number of hops equals the ratio of total distance to the crossover distance. For a typical sensor network with $D = 100$ m and $d_0 = 75$ m, $n_{opt} \approx 1$–$2$ hops — explaining why most WSNs use **clustered** or **short multi-hop** topologies.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Number of Sensors for k-Coverage

For a rectangular region of area $A_{region}$, and each sensor covers a circular sensing area of radius $r_s$:

$$A_{node} = \pi r_s^{2}$$

For **1-coverage** (every point is sensed by at least one node):

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{A_{region}}{\pi r_s^{2}} \right\rceil$$

For **k-coverage** (every point is sensed by at least k nodes — a common KTU question):

$$N_{min}^{(k)} = k \cdot \left\lceil \frac{A_{region}}{\pi r_s^{2}} \right\rceil = k \cdot N_{min}^{(1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Connectivity Constraint:** Coverage alone is insufficient. For a connected network, the communication radius $r_c$ must satisfy $r_c \geq 2 r_s$ (a classical result) to guarantee that k-covered networks are also k-connected.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison for KTU

| Protocol | Layer | Topology | Power Efficiency | Security | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-------|----------|------------------|----------|---------------|
| **IEEE 802.15.4** | PHY/MAC | Star/Mesh | High | AES-128 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ZigBee** | Network+ | Mesh | High | AES-128 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **6LoWPAN** | Adaptation | Mesh | High | App-layer | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **RPL** | Network | DAG | High | App-layer | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **CoAP** | Application | Client-Server | High | DTLS | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **MQTT** | Application | Pub/Sub | Very High | TLS | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **LoRaWAN** | MAC | Star | Ultra-Low | AES-128 | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes uniformly distributed over a $100 \text{ m} \times 100 \text{ m}$ field. Each node has $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, and a packet size $k = 4000$ bits. Compute the crossover distance $d_0$ and the energy to transmit to a sink at $D = 150$ m using the multi-path model.

**Step 1 — Crossover Distance:**

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Model Selection:** Since $D = 150$ m $> d_0 = 87.7$ m, we use the **multi-path** model.

**Step 3 — Transmit Energy:**

$$E_{Tx} = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{mp} D^{4} = (4000)(50 \times 10^{-9}) + (4000)(0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(150)^{4}$$

$$E_{Tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (4000)(0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(5.0625 \times 10^{8})$$

$$E_{Tx} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (4000)(0.000658) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 2.63 = 2.6322 \text{ J}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Energy spent on the **amplifier** ($\varepsilon_{mp} d^4$ term) is **~13,000 times larger** than the **electronics** term — confirming why long-distance direct transmission is energy-prohibitive in WSNs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of a Single Sensor Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSICAL WORLD - Temperature, Humidity, Pressure] --> B[SENSING UNIT]
    B --> B1[Physical Sensor]
    B1 --> B2[ADC - Analog to Digital Converter]
    B2 --> C[PROCESSING UNIT]
    C --> C1[Microcontroller - MSP430, ARM Cortex]
    C1 --> C2[Memory - RAM, Flash]
    C2 --> C3[Operating System - TinyOS, Contiki]
    C3 --> D[COMMUNICATION UNIT]
    D --> D1[Radio Transceiver - CC2420]
    D1 --> E[SINK - Base Station / Gateway]
    P[POWER UNIT - Battery + Energy Harvester] -.-> B
    P -.-> C
    P -.-> D
    L[LOCATION FINDING - GPS Module] -.-> C
    M[MOBILIZER - Optional] -.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Dotted arrows** represent *power supply* and *auxiliary* connections. Solid arrows represent *data flow*.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — WSN Network Architecture (Multi-Hop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph field1["SENSOR FIELD"]
        N1[Node 1] -->|Hop 1| N2[Node 2]
        N2 -->|Hop 2| N3[Node 3 - Cluster Head]
        N4[Node 4] --> N3
        N5[Node 5] --> N3
        N3 -->|Hop 3| N6[Node 6 - Cluster Head]
        N7[Node 7] --> N6
        N8[Node 8] --> N6
    end
    N6 -->|Final Hop| SINK[SINK - Base Station / Gateway]
    SINK --> CLOUD[INTERNET / CLOUD SERVER]
    CLOUD --> USER[End User - Mobile App / Dashboard]
    USER -->|Actuation| A[ACTUATORS - Optional Control Loop]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — WSN Protocol Stack vs OSI Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack["WSN PROTOCOL STACK"]
        L1[Application Layer - User Sensing Tasks]
        L2[Transport Layer - Reliable Delivery]
        L3[Network Layer - RPL Routing]
        L4[Data Link Layer - IEEE 802.15.4 MAC]
        L5[Physical Layer - Radio Frequency]
    end
    subgraph management["CROSS-LAYER MANAGEMENT PLANES"]
        PM[Power Management Plane]
        MM[Mobility Management Plane]
        TM[Task Management Plane]
    end
    PM -.-> L1
    PM -.-> L2
    PM -.-> L3
    PM -.-> L4
    PM -.-> L5
    MM -.-> L3
    MM -.-> L4
    TM -.-> L2
    TM -.-> L3
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Data Acquisition in WSN (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1. SENSE - Physical Phenomenon] --> B[2. SAMPLE - ADC Conversion]
    B --> C[3. PROCESS - Local Computation and Aggregation]
    C --> D{4. ROUTING DECISION - Multi-hop or Direct}
    D -->|Multi-hop| E[5a. TRANSMIT to Neighbour]
    D -->|Direct| F[5b. TRANSMIT to Sink]
    E --> G[6. RECEIVE at Intermediate Node]
    G --> C
    F --> H[7. SINK COLLECTS DATA]
    H --> I[8. FORWARD to Cloud / Internet]
    I --> J[9. APPLICATION LAYER ANALYSIS]
    J --> K[10. USER VISUALIZATION and ACTUATION]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Note:** The diagram above represents the **end-to-end information flow** in any IoT deployment. Always remember the chain: **Sense → Process → Transmit → Sink → Cloud → User → Actuate**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any four characteristics of WSNs that distinguish them from traditional wireless networks.

**Model Answer:**
A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and forward collected data to a central sink through multi-hop wireless communication.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**
1. **Dense deployment** with potentially thousands of nodes in a small area.
2. **Severe energy constraints** — battery-powered, often non-rechargeable.
3. **Self-organization** — nodes autonomously form the network without pre-existing infrastructure.
4. **Data-centric routing** — queries are addressed to data attributes, not to specific node IDs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** For 3-mark questions, define in **1.5 marks** and list with **brief explanation in 1.5 marks**. Avoid one-liners.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Star** and **Mesh** topologies in a WSN. Which is preferred for an industrial monitoring application and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Star Topology | Mesh Topology |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Connectivity | Direct node-to-sink | Multi-hop, node-to-node |
| Scalability | Poor (limited by hub range) | Excellent |
| Fault tolerance | Low (hub failure kills network) | High (alternate paths) |
| Energy efficiency | High (1-hop) | Lower (multi-hop overhead) |
| Latency | Low | Higher |

**Preferred for industrial monitoring: Mesh topology** — because industrial environments require **fault tolerance**, **reliable delivery**, and **scalability** in the face of node failures and RF interference.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a sensor node. List the functions of each unit. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the IEEE 802.15.4 standard and the ZigBee protocol stack. How do they enable IoT communication? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sensor Node Architecture:**

A sensor node consists of four primary units, supplemented by auxiliary units.

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises one or more physical sensors (temperature, humidity, accelerometer, etc.) and an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. The sensor produces an analog signal proportional to the measured physical quantity, which the ADC digitizes for processing.
2. **Processing Unit** — A low-power microcontroller (e.g., TI MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0) executes the sensing, signal processing, and protocol stack. It includes a small memory (RAM + Flash) and runs a lightweight OS like **TinyOS** or **Contiki**.
3. **Communication Unit** — A short-range radio transceiver (e.g., CC2420 chip operating at 2.4 GHz) handles packet transmission and reception. It is the **largest energy consumer** in the node.
4. **Power Unit** — A battery (often 2× AA) powers all subsystems. Energy harvesting modules (solar, vibration, thermal) may supplement it.

**Auxiliary units** include a **mobilizer** (motors for movement), **location finder** (GPS), and a **power generator** (harvesting circuit).

**(b) IEEE 802.15.4 and ZigBee:**

**IEEE 802.15.4** is a standard defining the **Physical (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** layers for low-rate wireless personal area networks (LR-WPANs). Key features:

- Operates in **ISM bands** (868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz).
- Maximum data rate: **250 kbps** (at 2.4 GHz).
- Uses **O-QPSK modulation** and **DSSS** spreading.
- Supports **16-bit short addresses** (up to 65,536 nodes per PAN) and **64-bit extended addresses**.
- Provides **CSMA/CA** based channel access and optional **AES-128** security.

**ZigBee** is built **on top** of IEEE 802.15.4 and adds the **Network (NWK)** and **Application (APL)** layers, forming the full **ZigBee protocol stack**:

- **ZigBee stack layers:** PHY → MAC (802.15.4) → NWK → APS → ZDO → Application.
- Supports **three device types**: Coordinator, Router, End Device.
- Enables **mesh networking** with up to 65,000 nodes per network.
- Provides **application profiles** for home automation, health care, and industrial control.

**Role in IoT:** Together, 802.15.4 and ZigBee form a **low-power, low-data-rate, mesh-capable** communication backbone for IoT — ideal for battery-operated devices transmitting small packets occasionally over short to medium ranges.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe the various network topologies used in WSNs. Compare their advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the energy consumption model for transmitting a k-bit packet over distance d. Show how the crossover distance d₀ is computed. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WSN Network Topologies:**

A WSN can be organized into several topologies, each with trade-offs between energy, latency, and reliability.

1. **Star Topology** — Every sensor node communicates *directly* with a central base station.
   - *Advantages:* Simple, low latency, easy synchronization.
   - *Limitations:* Poor scalability, single point of failure (the base station), high transmit energy for distant nodes.

2. **Mesh Topology** — Nodes relay data through neighbours; multiple paths exist between source and sink.
   - *Advantages:* Self-healing, high fault tolerance, scalable.
   - *Limitations:* Complex routing, higher protocol overhead, more energy due to multi-hop relaying.

3. **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology** — Nodes are grouped into clusters; each cluster has a **Cluster Head (CH)** that aggregates data and forwards it to the sink.
   - *Advantages:* Energy-efficient (LEACH protocol exploits this), scalable, reduced data redundancy.
   - *Limitations:* Cluster head becomes a bottleneck; CH selection overhead.

4. **Hybrid Topology** — Combines star clusters interconnected via a mesh backbone.
   - *Advantages:* Best of both worlds — efficiency of star within clusters, robustness of mesh between.
   - *Limitations:* Complex to design and maintain.

**Comparison Summary:** Star is preferred for small-scale deployments with high data rates; Mesh for large, fault-tolerant networks; Tree/Hierarchical for energy-constrained large-scale deployments; Hybrid for industrial-grade IoT.

**(b) Energy Consumption Model Derivation:**

Consider transmitting a $k$-bit message across a distance $d$. The total transmission energy has two components:

- **Electronic energy** $E_{elec}$ per bit (for digital processing, encoding, filtering).
- **Amplifier energy** — depends on distance and the propagation regime.

**Free-Space Model (short range, $d < d_0$):**
Signal decays as $d^2$ (proportional to the inverse-square law):

$$E_{Tx,fs} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

**Multi-Path Fading Model (long range, $d \geq d_0$):**
Signal decays as $d^4$ due to multi-path reflection/diffraction effects:

$$E_{Tx,mp} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

**Receive Energy:**

$$E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Crossover Distance** — Equating both models to find the boundary $d_0$:

$$E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}$$

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Practical significance:** For $d < d_0$, the free-space model underestimates energy (use $d^2$); for $d > d_0$, multi-path dominates and energy grows rapidly. This is why **multi-hop routing** is essential in WSNs — to keep each hop's distance below $d_0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse ZigBee with IEEE 802.15.4** — 802.15.4 is *only* PHY + MAC; ZigBee adds NWK + Application layers. Examiners *deduct 1–2 marks* for this confusion.
> 2. **Always include units** in energy calculations ($J$, $nJ$, $pJ$) — KTU answer scripts that omit units are penalized.
> 3. **Crossover distance** must be derived, not just stated. Write the *equating step* explicitly.
> 4. **Star vs Mesh vs Tree** — if asked to "compare", use a *table* with at least three parameters.
> 5. **Multi-hop derivation** — many students forget the **receive energy** at intermediate nodes. The total energy for $n$ hops is $n \cdot E_{Tx} + (n-1) \cdot E_{Rx}$ — not just $n \cdot E_{Tx}$.
> 6. **Avoid "OSI layer confusion"** — never equate the WSN "Transport Layer" with TCP. Most WSNs use **UDP-like** transport or skip it entirely.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensor Networks (Module 2)**

- [x] **Definition** — WSN = distributed autonomous sensor nodes + wireless multi-hop + sink.
- [x] **Four core units** of a sensor node — Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power.
- [x] **Three management planes** — Power, Mobility, Task — span across all layers.
- [x] **Four main topologies** — Star, Mesh, Tree (Clustered), Hybrid.
- [x] **IEEE 802.15.4** defines **only PHY + MAC**; ZigBee adds NWK + Application.
- [x] **Energy model** — free-space $d^2$ for short range, multi-path $d^4$ for long range.
- [x] **Crossover distance** — $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$.
- [x] **Communication is the dominant energy consumer**, not sensing or computation.
- [x] **Optimal hops** ≈ $D/d_0$ (rule of thumb for multi-hop design).
- [x] **k-coverage** requires **$N \geq k \cdot A_{region}/(\pi r_s^2)$** nodes.
- [x] **Connectivity requires** $r_c \geq 2 r_s$ for guaranteed k-connected k-covered networks.
- [x] **Standards to remember** — IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT.
- [x] **Applications** — Precision Agriculture, Healthcare (BSN), Industrial IoT, Smart Cities, Environmental Monitoring.
- [x] **Log-distance path loss** — $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting Smart Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connecting Smart Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Connecting Smart Objects** is the foundational process in the Internet of Things (IoT) architecture that enables physical "things" — equipped with sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, and transceivers — to participate in the digital world. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic covers the principles, criteria, models, and step-by-step mechanisms by which a *Smart Object* transitions from a passive, isolated device into a **node of the Internet**, capable of sensing, computing, communicating, and acting within a globally addressable network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Smart Object:** A *Smart Object* is a physical or virtual entity that possesses four essential capabilities — **(1) Sensing/Actuation, (2) Computation (local processing), (3) Communication (wired or wireless), and (4) a Unique Identifier (IP/MAC/URI)**. Connecting such objects is the prerequisite for the *Things* layer of IoT.

### 1.2 The 5 Characteristics of a Smart Object (Per KTU Reference Text)

| # | Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identity** | Has a unique address (e.g., IPv6, MAC, EPC) |
| 2 | **Sensing / Actuation** | Can measure physical phenomena or control hardware |
| 3 | **Computation** | Contains a microcontroller/processor for local logic |
| 4 | **Communication** | Can transmit/receive data over a network medium |
| 5 | **Power** | Has a power source (battery, mains, harvested) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postman" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a small village where each house has a sensor reporting temperature to a faraway city hall. To make the village "smart," we must (1) give every house a **unique postal address** (Identity), (2) install a **thermometer** (Sensor), (3) attach a **tiny computer** that decides when to send the data (Computation), (4) hire a **postman** who knows how to deliver mail over road/wireless paths (Communication), and (5) supply **electricity or batteries** to keep the postman working (Power). The act of *connecting* these houses to city hall — establishing addresses, mail routes, and rules of communication — is exactly what "Connecting Smart Objects" means in IoT.

### 1.4 Why "Connection" Matters in IoT

An isolated sensor is just a transducer. To become a "Thing" in the IoT, three conditions must be met:

$$\text{Object} + \text{Unique Address} + \text{Network Protocol} = \text{IoT Thing}$$

Without connection, there is no IoT — only electronics.

### 1.5 Metrics & Standards Snapshot

- **Bandwidth** is measured in **bits per second (bps / kbps / Mbps / Gbps)**.
- **Range** is typically expressed in **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**.
- **Power** is measured in **milliwatts (mW)** or **watts (W)**.
- **Data rate** follows the **Shannon–Hartley theorem**.
- **Identity** typically follows **IPv6 (128-bit)** or **EPC (Electronic Product Code, 96-bit)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Communication Criteria for Connecting Smart Objects

When a smart object is to be connected to the internet (or to a gateway), the designer must choose technologies that satisfy a *tuple* of constraints. The KTU syllabus lists these as the **Communication Criteria**:

**Step-by-step rationale for each criterion:**

1. **Range** — How far must data travel?
   - Personal Area (0–10 m): BLE, Zigbee, NFC
   - Local Area (10–100 m): Wi-Fi, BLE Mesh
   - Wide Area (>1 km): LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M
2. **Bandwidth (Data Rate)** — How much data per second?
3. **Power Consumption** — Is the device battery-operated? (Energy harvesting becomes critical.)
4. **Security** — Does the channel support encryption (AES-128, TLS, DTLS)?
5. **Cost & Complexity** — Are radios cheap enough for mass deployment?
6. **Topology** — Star, Mesh, Tree, or P2P?
7. **Interoperability** — IP-based (IPv6/6LoWPAN) vs proprietary (Zigbee, Z-Wave).

### 2.2 The Three-Step Process of "Connecting" a Smart Object

Every smart object that joins the IoT passes through a logical 3-stage connect process:

**Stage 1 — Provisioning (Configuration):**
The device is given an identity (IPv6 address), security credentials (keys, certificates), and a destination (URL of the IoT broker/server). This can be done at manufacturing, at deployment, or via a commissioning tool.

**Stage 2 — Connection Establishment (Handshake):**
The object performs a *link-layer handshake* (e.g., 802.11 association, BLE pairing) and a *network-layer handshake* (e.g., 6LoWPAN join, DHCPv6, RPL DAG construction) and finally a *transport-layer handshake* (TCP three-way, DTLS, CoAP).

**Stage 3 — Data Exchange (Service Registration):**
The object registers a resource (e.g., `/temperature`, `/actuator1`) on a service bus. Clients can then **read**, **subscribe**, or **invoke** these resources using application-layer protocols like **HTTP/REST, CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, or DDS**.

### 2.3 The IoT Communication Model (KTU High-Yield)

KTU specifies **four standard communication models** that govern how smart objects interact:

| Model | Direction | Example | KTU Shortcode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Request–Response** | Client → Server → Client | HTTP GET /temp from a browser | **RR** |
| **Publish–Subscribe** | Publishers ↔ Broker ↔ Subscribers | MQTT topic `home/room1/temp` | **PubSub** |
| **Push (Streaming)** | Source → Sink (one-way continuous) | Sensor streaming video/audio | **Push** |
| **Pull (Polling)** | Client repeatedly requests | CoAP periodic GET | **Pull** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, $S/N$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_{d} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{T_{tx}}$ | **Effective data rate** | $R_d$ in bps, $N_{bits}$ bits, $T_{tx}$ seconds |
| 3 | $N_{addr} = 2^{b}$ | **Number of unique addresses** | $b$ = address length in bits |
| 4 | IPv6 capacity: $2^{128}$ | **Total IoT addressable devices** | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| 5 | $P_{total} = P_{tx} + P_{rx} + P_{idle} + P_{sleep}$ | **Total node power budget** | Watts (W) or milliwatts (mW) |
| 6 | $E_{cycle} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{cycle}$ | **Energy per duty cycle** | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $L_{frame} = L_{hdr} + L_{payload} + L_{FCS}$ | **Frame length in bytes** | $L_{FCS}$ = Frame Check Sequence |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} \times 100\%$ | **Protocol efficiency (goodput ratio)** | Percentage (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Whenever a question asks *"Why IPv6 for IoT?"* — your answer **must** mention three things: (i) **Larger address space** ($2^{128}$), (ii) **Built-in security (IPsec)**, and (iii) **Stateless auto-configuration (SLAAC)**, which together solve the "identity crisis" of billions of smart objects.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors use **LoRa** (long range, low power) to connect to a farm gateway.
- **Smart Healthcare:** Wearable ECG patches use **BLE** to connect to a smartphone, which then relays via **MQTT over TLS** to a cloud broker.
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight controllers use **NB-IoT** (cellular, deep indoor) to connect directly to the operator's IP core network.

The choice of *how* you connect dictates the lifetime, cost, and scalability of the entire IoT deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why IPv6 Solves Smart Object Addressing

**Problem (KTU-style):** A company plans to deploy $N = 5 \times 10^9$ smart objects. Show whether **IPv4 (32-bit)** or **IPv6 (128-bit)** is sufficient. Each object must have a unique address.

**Step 1 — General formula for the number of unique addresses:**

$$N_{addr} = 2^{b}$$

where $b$ is the number of bits in the address field.

**Step 2 — Compute capacity of IPv4:**

$$N_{IPv4} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with required number of devices:**

$$N_{required} = 5 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Decision check:**

$$N_{IPv4} = 4.29 \times 10^{9} < N_{required} = 5 \times 10^{9}$$

Since IPv4 is *less than* the required count, **IPv4 is INSUFFICIENT**.

**Step 5 — Compute capacity of IPv6:**

$$N_{IPv6} = 2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$$

**Step 6 — Final decision:**

$$N_{IPv6} = 3.4 \times 10^{38} \gg N_{required} = 5 \times 10^{9}$$

Therefore, **IPv6 is sufficient** — in fact, IPv6 can address $\dfrac{3.4 \times 10^{38}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 6.8 \times 10^{28}$ times more devices than the company needs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Channel Capacity for a Smart Object Link

**Problem:** A temperature sensor uses a wireless channel of bandwidth $B = 250$ kHz with a signal-to-noise ratio $\dfrac{S}{N} = 1000$. Compute the maximum theoretical data rate (channel capacity).

**Step 1 — Recall Shannon's formula:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$C = 250 \times 10^3 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify inside the logarithm:**

$$1 + \frac{S}{N} = 1 + 1000 = 1001$$

**Step 4 — Compute the log base 2:**

$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{6.9088}{0.6931} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by bandwidth:**

$$C = 250 \times 10^3 \cdot 9.967 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 2.49 \text{ Mbps}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

This means that under a 250 kHz channel and SNR of 30 dB, the smart object can push roughly **2.49 Mbps** — enough to carry several temperature samples per second with very low payload, or to be a limiting factor when carrying high-throughput payloads like vibration spectra.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frame Efficiency of an IoT Protocol

**Problem:** A CoAP frame has $L_{hdr} = 4$ bytes, payload $L_{payload} = 20$ bytes, and $L_{FCS} = 2$ bytes. Find the protocol efficiency.

**Step 1 — Total frame length formula:**

$$L_{frame} = L_{hdr} + L_{payload} + L_{FCS}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$L_{frame} = 4 + 20 + 2 = 26 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$\eta = \frac{20}{26} \times 100\% \approx 76.92\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Approximately **76.92 %** of the transmitted bytes are useful payload — a *goodput ratio* typical of lightweight IoT protocols. (HTTP over TCP would yield only ~5–10 % efficiency due to large headers.)

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Budget of a Battery-Operated Smart Object

**Problem:** A soil sensor transmits at $P_{tx} = 50$ mW for 1 % of the time, receives at $P_{rx} = 30$ mW for 1 %, idles at $P_{idle} = 5$ mW for 8 %, and sleeps at $P_{sleep} = 0.01$ mW for 90 %. Compute average power and battery lifetime on a 2400 mAh, 3.7 V battery.

**Step 1 — Average power formula:**

$$P_{avg} = \sum_i (\alpha_i \cdot P_i)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= (0.01)(50) + (0.01)(30) + (0.08)(5) + (0.90)(0.01) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.009 \\
&= 1.209 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert battery energy:**

$$E_{batt} = V \cdot Q = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 2400 \text{ mAh} = 8880 \text{ mWh}$$

**Step 4 — Lifetime:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{8880}{1.209} \approx 7346 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to days:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{7346}{24} \approx 306 \text{ days} \approx 10 \text{ months}$$

This is the typical engineering target — a *battery lifetime of 1+ year* for unattended IoT nodes.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Connection Technology

| Criterion | Wi-Fi | BLE | Zigbee | LoRa | NB-IoT | LTE-M |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | ~50 m | ~10 m | ~100 m | ~10 km | ~15 km | ~10 km |
| Data Rate | High (~100 Mbps) | Low (~2 Mbps) | Med (~250 kbps) | Very low (~50 kbps) | Low (~200 kbps) | Med (~1 Mbps) |
| Power | High | Very Low | Low | Very Low | Medium | Medium |
| IP-native | Yes (IPv4/v6) | No (gateway) | No (gateway) | No (gateway) | Yes | Yes |
| Best Use | Video/Edge | Wearable | Home automation | Farm/Smart-city | Smart-meter | Asset tracking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The "**IP-native**" column is decisive. For *direct internet connection of every smart object*, prefer Wi-Fi, NB-IoT, or LTE-M. For battery-critical, low-data scenarios, use LoRa or BLE **with a gateway**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Smart Object Connection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Object with Sensor] --> B{Stage 1: Provisioning}
    B --> B1[Assign Unique IP Address]
    B --> B2[Inject Security Keys]
    B --> B3[Configure Server URL]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Link Establishment]
    C --> C1[Radio Association]
    C --> C2[Network Layer Join DHCPv6 / RPL]
    C --> C3[Transport Layer Handshake TCP / DTLS]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Data Exchange]
    D --> D1[Register Resource /topic]
    D --> D2[Publish Telemetry via MQTT or CoAP]
    D --> D3[Receive Commands / Subscriptions]
    D --> E[Connected IoT Thing on Internet]
    E --> F[Cloud Broker or Application Server]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Communication Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PubSub[Publish Subscribe Model]
        P1[Publisher Sensor] --> B[Broker MQTT]
        B --> S1[Subscriber App]
        B --> S2[Subscriber Mobile]
    end

    subgraph RR[Request Response Model]
        CL[Client App] -->|HTTP GET| SRV[Web Server]
        SRV -->|HTTP 200 + JSON| CL
    end

    subgraph Push[Push Streaming Model]
        CAM[IP Camera] -->|RTP stream| VR[Video Recorder]
    end

    subgraph Pull[Pull Polling Model]
        CO[CoAP Client] -->|GET temp every 10s| NS[Node Sensor]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Connected Smart Object

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Source Battery or Harvested] --> MCU
    SEN[Sensor Temperature Humidity Motion] --> ADC
    ADC[ADC Front End] --> MCU
    ACT[Actuator Relay Motor LED] --> DRV
    DRV[Driver Circuit] --> ACT
    MCU[Microcontroller MCU] --> RAD[Radio Transceiver]
    MCU --> MEM[Local Memory]
    RAD --> ANT[Antenna]
    ANT --> NW[Network Gateway or Base Station]
    NW --> CLD[Cloud Platform]
    SEC[Security Module Crypto Accelerator] --> MCU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the signal path from *Physical Phenomenon → Sensor → ADC → MCU → Radio → Antenna → Network → Cloud*. Every block is essential. If *any* block is missing, the object is *not* a connected smart object — it is either a sensor, an actuator, or an embedded system without internet access.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing a Connection Technology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Choose Connection Tech] --> Q1{Is device mains powered?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is range less than 100 m?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> W[Use Wi Fi 802.11]
    Q2 -- No --> C[Use Cellular LTE M or NB IoT]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is payload less than 100 bytes?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is range more than 1 km?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> L[Use LoRaWAN or Sigfox]
    Q4 -- No --> Z[Use Zigbee or BLE Mesh with gateway]
    Q3 -- No --> ST[Use Wi Fi or LTE M]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a *Smart Object* in IoT. List any **four** characteristics that qualify a physical object as a smart object.

**Model Answer:**

A **Smart Object** is a physical entity that has been augmented with sensing, processing, communication, and identification capabilities, allowing it to interact with other entities over a network (typically the Internet).

The four key characteristics are:

1. **Identity** — A unique address (e.g., IPv6, MAC, EPC, URI) to be unambiguously identified on the network.
2. **Sensing / Actuation** — The ability to measure a physical parameter (temperature, pressure, motion) or to act upon the environment (switching a relay, driving a motor).
3. **Computation** — A built-in microcontroller/processor that runs local logic, filters data, and makes decisions before transmission.
4. **Communication** — A wired or wireless transceiver (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT, Ethernet) to exchange data with peers, gateways, or the cloud.
