(An additional optional fifth: **Power** — self-sustained energy source such as battery or energy harvester.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List and briefly explain the **four communication models** used for connecting smart objects in IoT.

**Model Answer:**

The four communication models defined in the IoT Reference Architecture are:

1. **Request–Response Model** — A client sends a request to a server and waits for a synchronous reply. *Example:* An Android app issuing an HTTP GET to a temperature REST endpoint.
2. **Publish–Subscribe Model** — Publishers send messages tagged with topics to a *broker*; the broker fans them out to all interested subscribers. *Example:* MQTT broker routing `home/livingroom/temp` to a phone and a cloud dashboard.
3. **Push (Streaming) Model** — The data source continuously pushes data to a sink without explicit requests. *Example:* An IP camera streaming H.264 over RTP.
4. **Pull (Polling) Model** — The client repeatedly pulls (polls) the server for new data. *Example:* A CoAP client GETting `/sensor/temp` every 10 seconds.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a typical connected smart object**. Identify the role of each block.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A company needs to connect **10 million** soil-moisture sensors across agricultural fields. Compare the suitability of **Wi-Fi, Zigbee, LoRa, and NB-IoT** for this use case and recommend the best option with justification.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

A connected smart object is built from **six functional blocks**:

1. **Sensor / Actuator Subsystem** — Converts physical phenomena (moisture, temperature) into electrical signals, or converts electrical commands into physical action (valve, motor).
2. **Analog Front End / ADC** — Conditions (amplifies, filters) and digitizes the analog sensor output.
3. **Microcontroller (MCU)** — Local processor that runs the firmware, applies thresholds, formats payloads, and orchestrates communication. Examples: ARM Cortex-M0+, ESP32, ATmega328P.
4. **Memory** — Stores firmware, calibration data, and buffered telemetry (Flash + RAM).
5. **Radio Transceiver + Antenna** — Modulates the digital data onto a carrier wave for wireless transmission (sub-GHz for LoRa, 2.4 GHz for Zigbee/BLE, licensed cellular bands for NB-IoT).
6. **Power Source** — Battery, mains adapter, or energy harvester (solar, piezoelectric, thermal).
7. **Security Module** *(optional but recommended)* — Hardware crypto accelerator for AES, ECC, or TLS/DTLS handshakes.

The MCU orchestrates: it samples the sensor at intervals, formats the value into a JSON or CBOR payload, signs/encrypts it, and hands it to the radio for transmission.

*(Full block diagram — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

Let us evaluate each technology for the given use case (10 million sensors, large agricultural fields):

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | Zigbee | LoRa | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | ~50 m | ~100 m | ~10 km | ~15 km |
| Battery life | Poor (days) | Good (months) | Excellent (5–10 yr) | Good (1–3 yr) |
| Coverage in fields | Requires many APs | Requires dense mesh | Single gateway per km² | Uses existing towers |
| Data rate | 100 Mbps | 250 kbps | 50 kbps | 200 kbps |
| Cost per node | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Payload of soil-moisture data | Overkill | OK | OK | OK |
| IP-native | Yes | No (gateway) | No (gateway) | Yes (cellular) |

**Recommendation: LoRaWAN.**

**Justification:**

- Soil moisture data is **tiny** (a few bytes every 15 min) — LoRa's 50 kbps is more than sufficient.
- Fields span **kilometres** — LoRa's 10 km range is ideal and drastically reduces gateway count.
- Sensors are **battery-powered** and must last years — LoRa's ultra-low duty cycle enables **5–10 year** battery life.
- Cost per module is **USD 3–5**, feasible for 10 million units.
- The slight drawback — *non-IP-native* — is overcome by a **LoRaWAN gateway** that bridges to the IP cloud.

Wi-Fi is unsuitable (short range, high power). Zigbee is unsuitable (requires dense mesh over km² fields). NB-IoT works but has higher per-module cost and ongoing cellular subscription fees.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **step-by-step process of connecting a smart object to the Internet**. Include the three stages and the protocols at each layer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A wireless channel for an IoT device has bandwidth $B = 500$ kHz and SNR $= 400$. Compute the **Shannon channel capacity** and comment on whether this channel is suitable for sending **10 kbps** of sensor data.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

Connecting a smart object to the Internet is a **three-stage process**:

**Stage 1 — Provisioning (Configuration Phase):**

The object is given its *identity* (IPv6 address — typically via SLAAC, DHCPv6, or 6LoWPAN), *security credentials* (pre-shared keys, X.509 certificates), and the *destination* (URL/URI of the cloud broker — e.g., `mqtt://broker.example.com:1883`).

**Stage 2 — Connection Establishment (Handshake Phase):**

The device performs handshakes at three layers:

- **Link Layer:** Radio association (e.g., 802.11 association request/response, BLE pairing with Just-Works / Passkey / OOB, 802.15.4 beacon synchronization).
- **Network Layer:** 6LoWPAN join, RPL DODAG construction (for mesh networks), or LTE attach + PDN activation (for cellular).
- **Transport / Application Layer:** TCP three-way handshake (for HTTP/MQTT over TCP) or DTLS handshake (for CoAP over UDP).

**Stage 3 — Data Exchange (Service Phase):**

The object registers a *resource* on the application server (e.g., `coap://broker/sensor/temp`). It then:
- **Publishes** telemetry (MQTT PUBLISH or CoAP POST).
- **Observes** events (CoAP Observe or MQTT Subscribe).
- **Accepts** commands (HTTP PUT, CoAP PUT, or MQTT SUBSCRIBE to command topic).

Communication continues in a duty-cycled manner to conserve energy until decommissioning.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — State the Shannon–Hartley formula:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $B = 500$ kHz and $S/N = 400$:**

$$C = 500 \times 10^3 \cdot \log_2(1 + 400)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1 + S/N$:**

$$1 + 400 = 401$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\log_2(401)$:**

$$\log_2(401) = \frac{\ln(401)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{5.994}{0.6931} \approx 8.647 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by bandwidth:**

$$C = 500 \times 10^3 \cdot 8.647 \text{ bps} \approx 4.32 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the required data rate:**

$$C = 4.32 \text{ Mbps} \gg 10 \text{ kbps}$$

**Conclusion:**

The Shannon capacity is approximately **4.32 Mbps**, which is **432 times** greater than the required 10 kbps. Therefore, the channel is **more than sufficient** for transmitting 10 kbps of sensor data with substantial margin for retransmissions, encryption overhead, and protocol headers. The link is *not* the bottleneck — the application-layer protocol overhead and MCU processing time will dominate the actual end-to-end latency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** When writing Shannon's theorem, students often drop the bandwidth units (Hz) and lose 1 mark. Always write $B$ in Hz.
> 2. **Confusing Data Rate with Bandwidth:** Bandwidth is in Hz; data rate is in bps. Examiners will deduct marks if you interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In Q.A(a), students describe the architecture in text but skip the diagram — this is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. Always include a labelled block diagram.
> 4. **Not Justifying the Recommendation:** In Q.A(b), simply stating "LoRa" without a comparison table or justification will cost 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring "Communication Criteria":** KTU frequently asks "list the criteria for choosing a connection technology." Memorize: *Range, Bandwidth, Power, Security, Cost, Topology, Interoperability*.
> 6. **Forgetting IPv6 Justification:** When asked "why IPv6 for IoT?", always mention **address space, IPsec, and SLAAC** — never just one of them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Smart Object = Sensing + Computation + Communication + Identity (+ Power).** All five together.
- [ ] **Communication Criteria:** Range, Bandwidth, Power, Security, Cost, Topology, Interoperability.
- [ ] **Four Communication Models:** Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push, Pull.
- [ ] **Three Connection Stages:** Provisioning → Handshake → Data Exchange.
- [ ] **IPv6 = $2^{128}$ addresses**, built-in **IPsec**, **SLAAC** — solves IoT identity problem.
- [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — maximum theoretical data rate.
- [ ] **Protocol Efficiency:** $\eta = (L_{payload} / L_{frame}) \times 100\%$. Lightweight protocols (CoAP) >> HTTP.
- [ ] **IP-native techs:** Wi-Fi, NB-IoT, LTE-M. **Non-IP techs:** BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, Sigfox (need a gateway).
- [ ] **Range–Power Trade-off:** Longer range → higher transmit power → shorter battery life (think LoRa vs BLE).
- [ ] **Average Power Formula:** $P_{avg} = \sum_i \alpha_i P_i$, where $\alpha_i$ is duty cycle.
- [ ] **Application Protocols:** MQTT (Pub/Sub), CoAP (RR over UDP), HTTP (RR over TCP), AMQP, DDS.
- [ ] **Energy Budgeting:** Aim for **years of battery life** through duty cycling, sleep modes, and energy harvesting.
- [ ] **Security:** Always use **TLS/DTLS** for application traffic and **AES-128** at the link layer.
- [ ] **Frame Equation:** $L_{frame} = L_{header} + L_{payload} + L_{FCS}$.
- [ ] **Decision Heuristic for Connection Tech:** Mains-powered + short range → Wi-Fi. Battery + long range + tiny payload → LoRa. Cellular + IP-native → NB-IoT/LTE-M. Wearable + smartphone-tethered → BLE.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Connecting Smart Objects | PCADT602 | Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communications Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communications Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Communications Criteria** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the standardized set of qualitative and quantitative parameters used to evaluate, compare, and select communication technologies and protocols that interconnect heterogeneous IoT devices. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCADT602 - Module 2), these criteria form the *decision matrix* that engineers use to map application requirements (e.g., a soil moisture sensor in a farm vs. a video doorbell at home) to appropriate communication technologies (e.g., LoRa vs. Wi-Fi).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> *Communication Criteria are the measurable and observable attributes — such as range, power consumption, data rate, latency, security, and topology — that characterize the performance, suitability, and feasibility of a wireless/wired communication link in an IoT deployment.*

The seven universally accepted communication criteria specified in the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus are:

1. **Range** (Communication distance)
2. **Power Consumption** (Energy budget)
3. **Bandwidth / Data Rate** (Throughput)
4. **Security** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication)
5. **Latency** (End-to-end delay)
6. **Reliability / Robustness** (Error tolerance)
7. **Topology / Scalability** (Network architecture)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Postal Service vs. A Friend Standing Next to You"**
>
> Imagine you want to send a *message*. You have two options:
>
> - **Option A (Friend Next to You):** Whisper → Instant, free, but only works if the friend is right there (low **range**, ultra-low **latency**, no **power** needed).
> - **Option B (Postal Mail to Another Country):** Slow (high **latency**), costs money (**power/bandwidth**), but works across continents (long **range**), and the envelope can be sealed (**security**).
>
> **Communications Criteria = The Checklist You Use to Pick Between Whispering and Posting a Letter.**
>
> For a heart-rate sensor on a wrist, you want *whisper-grade* (Bluetooth). For a soil sensor 10 km away, you want *postal-grade* (LoRaWAN). The criteria tell you *which service* to pick.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Values used in this module:**
>
> - **Speed of Light (c):** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
> - **Free-space path loss at 2.4 GHz:** ≈ **100 dB** at 100 m
> - **Standard ISM Bands for IoT:** **433 MHz**, **868/915 MHz**, **2.4 GHz**
> - **Typical IoT Data Rate Range:** **0.3 kbps (LoRa)** to **150 Mbps (Wi-Fi 6)**
> - **Typical IoT Range:** **10 m (BLE)** to **10 km (LPWAN)**
> - **Acceptable Latency for Industrial IoT:** **< 10 ms**
> - **Battery Lifetime Goal:** **> 10 years** (for 1% duty-cycle sensors)

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Criteria — Structured Analytical Breakdown

### **Criterion 1: Range (Communication Distance)**
- **Why it matters:** IoT devices may be placed in unreachable locations (underground pipes, crop fields, offshore buoys).
- **How it works:** Depends on transmit power, antenna gain, frequency, and propagation environment.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - **PAN (Personal Area Network):** < 10 m → BLE, Zigbee
  - **LAN (Local Area Network):** 10–100 m → Wi-Fi
  - **MAN/WAN (Wide Area Network):** km range → LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT

### **Criterion 2: Power Consumption**
- **Why it matters:** Many IoT nodes are battery-powered and must last years.
- **How it works:** Proportional to transmit power + active time. The energy budget is $E = P \times t$.
- **Standard Metric:** **mW** (active) vs. **µW** (sleep).

### **Criterion 3: Bandwidth / Data Rate**
- **Why it matters:** Determines how much data can be sent per second — critical for image/video vs. temperature.
- **How it works:** Governed by **Shannon's Capacity Theorem** (see Section 3).
- **Standard Metric:** **bps, kbps, Mbps**.

### **Criterion 4: Security**
- **Why it matters:** IoT devices are entry points for attackers (botnets like Mirai).
- **How it works:** Three pillars — **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication)**.
- **Implementation:** AES-128 encryption, TLS/DTLS handshake, device certificates.

### **Criterion 5: Latency**
- **Why it matters:** Real-time control loops (factory robots, autonomous vehicles) cannot tolerate delay.
- **How it works:** Sum of transmission delay + propagation delay + processing delay + queuing delay.
- **Standard Metric:** **milliseconds (ms)**.

### **Criterion 6: Reliability**
- **Why it matters:** Packet loss in medical or industrial control can be catastrophic.
- **How it works:** Measured by **Packet Error Rate (PER)** and **Bit Error Rate (BER)**.
- **Standard Metric:** **PER < 1%** for reliable IoT.

### **Criterion 7: Topology / Scalability**
- **Why it matters:** Determines how many devices can join and how the network self-organizes.
- **Standard Topologies:** **Star, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Criterion | Governing Equation / Rule | Variables & Units | Typical IoT Value |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Range (Friis equation) | $R \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,\min}}}$ | $P_t$ = Tx power (W), $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains | 10 m to 15 km |
| 2 | Energy Budget | $E = P \cdot t$ | $P$ in W, $t$ in s, $E$ in Joules | 1–10 kJ/year/node |
| 3 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \tfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR (linear) | 0.3 kbps – 150 Mbps |
| 4 | Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (dB) | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | 80–120 dB |
| 5 | Total Latency | $L = t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$ | All in seconds (s) | 1 ms – 5 s |
| 6 | Reliability (PER) | $PER = 1 - (1-BER)^n$ | $n$ = packet size (bits) | < 0.01 |
| 7 | Battery Lifetime | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V}{P_{avg}}$ | $C_{bat}$ in Ah, $V$ in V, $P_{avg}$ in W | 1–10 years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU numerical problems on communication criteria and for selecting technology in design viva questions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Long range + ultra-low power → **LoRaWAN** selected over Wi-Fi.
- **Industrial Automation:** Low latency + high reliability → **5G URLLC / Wired Ethernet** selected.
- **Wearable Health:** Short range + low power + secure → **BLE 5.0** selected.
- **Smart Home:** Medium range + moderate data → **Wi-Fi/Zigbee** selected.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — A KTU Numerical Favourite

The FSPL formula is derived from the **Friis Transmission Equation**. We derive it step by step.

**Starting Point — Friis Equation:**

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

where:
- $P_r$ = received power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $G_t, G_r$ = transmit and receive antenna gains (linear, dimensionless)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $d$ = distance between antennas (m)

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the path loss term $PL = \dfrac{P_t}{P_r}$ (assuming $G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$
PL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$:

$$
PL = \left(\frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to decibels using $PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(PL)$:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Split the logarithm:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Insert $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and convert units ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz) to obtain the **engineering form**:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final FSPL Formula:**
> $$\boxed{PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44}$$
> This is the **exact form expected in KTU numerical answer scripts**.

**Step 6: Worked Numerical Example**

*Problem:* Find the path loss for $d = 2$ km at $f = 868$ MHz (LoRa band).

**Solution:**

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(2) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 20 \times 0.3010 + 20 \times 2.9385 + 32.44
$$

$$
= 6.02 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 97.23 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Result:** Path loss = **97.23 dB**. A transmitter would need to deliver this much more power (in dB) than the receiver's sensitivity to close the link.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Channel Capacity (Data Rate Limit)

**Starting Premise:** Maximum achievable data rate over a noisy channel.

**Step 1:** For a noiseless channel, capacity $C = 2B$ (Nyquist, 2 samples/Hz).

**Step 2:** For a noisy channel with S/N ratio, capacity is reduced by log factor:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Convert SNR to dB form: $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$, so $\dfrac{S}{N} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}\right)
$$

**Numerical Check:** If $B = 1$ MHz and $SNR = 20$ dB (i.e., S/N = 100):

$$
C = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln 101}{\ln 2} = \frac{4.615}{0.693} \approx 6.66
$$

$$
C \approx 6.66 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling bandwidth doubles capacity, but doubling SNR only **logarithmically** increases it — this is why IoT designers prefer *more bandwidth* over *more power* when both are expensive.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Criterion-to-Technology Mapping

| Criterion | BLE 5.0 | Zigbee | Wi-Fi 6 | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | 5G |
|-----------|---------|--------|---------|---------|--------|-----|
| Range | ★ (10 m) | ★★ (100 m) | ★★ (100 m) | ★★★★★ (10 km) | ★★★★ (5 km) | ★★★★ (cell) |
| Power | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Data Rate | ★★ (2 Mbps) | ★ (250 kbps) | ★★★★★ (Gbps) | ★ (50 kbps) | ★ (200 kbps) | ★★★★★ (Gbps) |
| Latency | ★★★★ (ms) | ★★★ (ms) | ★★★★ (ms) | ★★ (s) | ★★★ (s) | ★★★★★ (ms) |
| Security | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Reliability | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Topology | Star | Mesh | Star | Star | Star | Cell/Mesh |

> Read this as: ★ = low capability, ★★★★★ = best in class. **Memorize this matrix — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 2 question paper.**

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 7 Communication Criteria & Their Inter-Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IOT APPLICATION REQUIREMENT] --> B[COMMUNICATION CRITERIA DECISION MATRIX]
    B --> C1[RANGE]
    B --> C2[POWER CONSUMPTION]
    B --> C3[BANDWIDTH DATA RATE]
    B --> C4[SECURITY]
    B --> C5[LATENCY]
    B --> C6[RELIABILITY]
    B --> C7[TOPOLOGY SCALABILITY]
    C1 --> D[SELECTED IOT PROTOCOL]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    D --> E1[BLE FOR WEARABLES]
    D --> E2[WIFI FOR SMART HOME]
    D --> E3[LORAWAN FOR AGRICULTURE]
    D --> E4[NB IOT FOR SMART METERS]
    D --> E5[5G URLLC FOR INDUSTRY]
    C3 -.->|governed by| F1[SHANNON CAPACITY THEOREM]
    C1 -.->|governed by| F2[FRIIS FSPL EQUATION]
    C5 -.->|sum of| F3[TX PROP PROC QUEUE DELAYS]
    C6 -.->|measured by| F4[PER BER METRICS]
    C2 -.->|energy budget| F5[E = P TIMES T]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layered Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[APPLICATION LAYER] --> L2[TRANSPORT AND SECURITY LAYER]
    L2 --> L3[NETWORK AND ROUTING LAYER]
    L3 --> L4[MAC AND LINK LAYER]
    L4 --> L5[PHYSICAL LAYER RADIO FREQUENCY]
    L5 --> L6[PROPAGATION CHANNEL FREE SPACE OR OBSTRUCTED]
    L1 -.->|criteria 7 topology| L3
    L2 -.->|criteria 4 security| L2
    L3 -.->|criteria 1 range| L5
    L4 -.->|criteria 6 reliability| L4
    L5 -.->|criteria 3 bandwidth| L5
    L6 -.->|criteria 5 latency| L4
    L6 -.->|criteria 2 power| L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams:**
> - The **top diagram** shows that all 7 criteria are evaluated simultaneously before a protocol is selected.
> - The **bottom diagram** maps each criterion to the OSI layer where it is primarily enforced — an examiner-favourite question: *"At which layer is reliability handled?"* → **MAC / Link Layer**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any three communication criteria used for selecting an IoT communication technology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three communication criteria are:

1. **Range (1 mark):** The maximum distance over which the IoT device can reliably transmit data. Example: BLE ≈ 10 m, LoRaWAN ≈ 10 km.

2. **Power Consumption (1 mark):** The amount of electrical energy consumed by the radio module during transmission, reception, and sleep. Critical for battery-operated devices where lifetime must exceed 1–10 years.

3. **Bandwidth / Data Rate (1 mark):** The maximum volume of data that can be transmitted per second, governed by Shannon's capacity theorem. Voice needs kbps, video needs Mbps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *only the name* of the criterion (e.g., "Security") without explaining *why it matters* in an IoT context. You will lose 1 mark. Always couple the criterion with its **IoT-specific implication**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Wi-Fi and LoRaWAN with respect to range, data rate, and power consumption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | LoRaWAN |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| Range (1 m) | ≈ 100 m | ≈ 10 km |
| Data Rate (1 m) | Up to 600 Mbps (high) | 0.3 – 50 kbps (low) |
| Power Consumption (1 m) | High (≈ 1 W active) | Ultra-low (≈ 10 mW active) |

> Hence Wi-Fi is suitable for **high-throughput local** applications (smart home video), while LoRaWAN suits **low-power long-range** applications (agricultural sensors).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven communication criteria** used for selecting an IoT communication technology. For each criterion, give one example of a technology that excels in it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) equation** starting from the Friis transmission equation. A transmitter operating at **2.4 GHz** delivers **20 dBm** of power to a receiver with a sensitivity of **–90 dBm**. Calculate the maximum line-of-sight range, assuming isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) The Seven Communication Criteria (7 × 1 = 7 marks):**

1. **Range** → Long-range criterion best satisfied by **LoRaWAN** (10 km).
2. **Power Consumption** → Ultra-low-power criterion best satisfied by **BLE** (sleep current ≈ 1 µA).
3. **Bandwidth / Data Rate** → High-throughput criterion best satisfied by **Wi-Fi 6 / 5G** (Gbps).
4. **Security** → Strong encryption best satisfied by **TLS/DTLS-enabled NB-IoT** or **5G**.
5. **Latency** → Real-time response best satisfied by **5G URLLC** (< 1 ms).
6. **Reliability** → High-PER tolerance best satisfied by **Wired Industrial Ethernet** (PER < $10^{-9}$).
7. **Topology / Scalability** → Best by **Mesh Zigbee** (thousands of nodes self-organize).

---

**(b) FSPL Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the Friis equation with $G_t = G_r = 1$:

$$
P_r = P_t \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

Substitute $\lambda = c/f$ and convert to dB:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44
$$

**Numerical Application (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2400 \text{ MHz}$
- $P_t = 20 \text{ dBm}$
- $P_{r,\min} = -90 \text{ dBm}$

**Maximum allowable path loss:**

$$
PL_{\max} = P_t - P_{r,\min} = 20 - (-90) = 110 \text{ dB}
$$

**Set $PL_{dB} = 110$ and solve for $d$:**

$$
110 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 110 - 67.60 - 32.44 = 9.96
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = 0.498 \;\Rightarrow\; d = 10^{0.498} \approx 3.15 \text{ km}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Maximum range ≈ **3.15 km** (line-of-sight, free-space).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall on (b):** Two common errors cost 1–2 marks each:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dBm to dB** before subtracting — you CANNOT subtract Watts from dBm directly.
> 2. **Mixing units** — $d$ must be in **km** and $f$ in **MHz** for the 32.44 constant to be valid. Using meters/megahertz gives a different (wrong) constant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **OSI-layer mapping of communication criteria** in IoT. State which criterion is enforced at which layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless IoT sensor operates in a **1 MHz** channel with an SNR of **15 dB**. Using Shannon's theorem, calculate the **maximum achievable data rate**. If the packet size is **256 bytes**, calculate the **PER** assuming a BER of $10^{-5}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) OSI Layer Mapping (7 marks):**

| OSI Layer | Communication Criteria Enforced |
|-----------|----------------------------------|
| Application | Topology, Scalability |
| Transport / Security | Security (TLS/DTLS, AES) |
| Network | Range (routing decisions) |
| MAC / Link | Reliability (ACK, retransmission) |
| Physical | Bandwidth, Power, Latency (modulation + RF) |

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.2 should be redrawn in the answer script — 2 marks reserved for diagram.)*

---

**(b) Shannon Capacity + PER (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear (2 marks):**

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon (2 marks):**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR) = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 31.62)
$$

$$
\log_2(32.62) = \frac{\ln 32.62}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.485}{0.693} \approx 5.03
$$

$$
C \approx 5.03 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PER (3 marks):**

Packet size $n = 256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits}$. Using $PER = 1 - (1-BER)^n$:

$$
PER = 1 - (1 - 10^{-5})^{2048}
$$

$$
(1 - 10^{-5})^{2048} \approx e^{-2048 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{-0.02048} \approx 0.9797
$$

$$
\boxed{PER \approx 1 - 0.9797 = 0.0203 \text{ or } 2.03\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** PER = 2.03% means about 2 in every 100 packets are corrupted. For mission-critical IoT (medical), this is **too high** — would require FEC (Forward Error Correction) to bring it under 0.1%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall on (b):** A frequent error is writing $(1 - BER)^n$ as $1 - BER^n$ — that is **algebraically wrong** and will cost 1 full mark. Use the binomial expansion $PER \approx n \cdot BER$ only as an approximation when $BER \ll 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorize before exam:**
>
> ✅ **7 Criteria** = Range, Power, Bandwidth, Security, Latency, Reliability, Topology.
>
> ✅ **FSPL Formula** (in dB): $PL = 20\log(d_{km}) + 20\log(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ — units matter!
>
> ✅ **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — bandwidth linear, SNR logarithmic.
>
> ✅ **Energy Budget:** $E = P \cdot t$ — power × time = joules.
>
> ✅ **Battery Lifetime:** $T = C_{bat} \cdot V / P_{avg}$ — in seconds.
>
> ✅ **Total Latency** = $t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$.
>
> ✅ **PER formula:** $PER = 1 - (1-BER)^n$, where $n$ = bits in packet.
>
> ✅ **Mapping:** Range → Physical/Network, Power → Physical, Bandwidth → Physical, Security → Transport, Latency → Physical/MAC, Reliability → MAC, Topology → Application/Network.
>
> ✅ **Industry Mnemonic: "R-P-B-S-L-R-T"** (Rani, Pani, Barish, Sadak, Light, Road, Tractor) — use this to recall the 7 criteria in order during viva.
>
> ✅ **Trade-off Rule:** Higher range **always** demands more power OR more time (lower data rate). There is no free lunch — the IoT designer's job is to find the *optimum* point on this 3-way axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Access Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Access Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are IoT Access Technologies?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> IoT Access Technologies (also called **IoT Connectivity Technologies** or **Edge Connectivity Technologies**) are the physical (PHY) and data link (MAC) layer protocols and standards that enable the "last-mile" or "first-mile" communication between resource-constrained IoT end devices (sensors, actuators, RFID tags) and the nearest gateway, router, or base station that bridges them to the core network (Internet/Cloud).

These technologies sit at the **perception and network layers** of the standard 5-layer IoT Architecture Model. They are responsible for determining *how* a tiny, battery-operated sensor "talks" to the outside world — a critical decision since the chosen access technology dictates the **range, data rate, power budget, and device cost** of any IoT deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Mapping:**
> - **WPAN** → Wireless Personal Area Network (short range, < 10 m)
> - **WLAN** → Wireless Local Area Network (medium range, ~100 m)
> - **LPWAN** → Low-Power Wide-Area Network (long range, 2–15 km)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Service" Model

Imagine you are a villager in Kerala wanting to send a message to a friend in another state:

- **Bluetooth / ZigBee** → You walk next door and hand-deliver the letter. *Fast, cheap, but only works for immediate neighbours.*
- **Wi-Fi** → You hand it to the local post office in your colony. *Works great inside your campus/home network.*
- **LoRaWAN / Sigfox** → You drop it at a village sub-post office that bundles hundreds of letters and sends them via a slow but far-reaching lorry. *Cheap, very long range, but slow.*
- **NB-IoT / LTE-M** → You use the national speed post (cellular towers). *Reliable, decent speed, slightly more expensive, but licensed and carrier-grade.*

The **IoT device is the villager**, the **gateway/base station is the post office**, and the **access technology is the delivery mechanism** chosen based on urgency, distance, and cost.

### 1.3 Classification of IoT Access Technologies

| **Class** | **Typical Range** | **Key Examples** | **KTU Exam Keyword** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Range WPAN** | < 10 m | Bluetooth, BLE, ZigBee, NFC, RFID | Personal Area |
| **Medium-Range WLAN** | ~50–100 m | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac) | Local Area |
| **Long-Range LPWAN (Unlicensed)** | 2–15 km | LoRaWAN, Sigfox, Ingenu | Sub-GHz ISM |
| **Long-Range LPWAN (Licensed/Cellular)** | 1–10+ km | NB-IoT, LTE-M (Cat-M1), EC-GSM-IoT | 3GPP Cellular IoT |
| **Mesh / Industrial** | 100 m–few km | Thread, WirelessHART, Z-Wave, 6LoWPAN | Mesh Networking |

### 1.4 Standardised Frequency Bands Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IoT access technologies predominantly operate in the **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) radio bands** which are license-exempt. In India, the relevant bands are governed by the **National Frequency Allocation Plan (NFAP)** and the **Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing (WPC)** of DoT.

- **Sub-1 GHz band**: 433 MHz, 868 MHz (Europe), **865–867 MHz (India — popular for LoRa and Sigfox)**
- **2.4 GHz band (Global ISM)**: Used by Wi-Fi, ZigBee, Bluetooth, BLE, Thread
- **5 GHz / 6 GHz band**: Wi-Fi 6/6E
- **Licensed Cellular bands**: 700–900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 2100 MHz, 2600 MHz (used by 4G/5G/NB-IoT/LTE-M)

### 1.5 Design Trade-offs in Choosing an Access Technology

Every IoT designer faces the **"IoT Access Trilemma"** — you can typically optimise only two of the following three:

1. **Long Range**
2. **Low Power**
3. **High Data Rate**

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU one-liner to memorise:**
> *"Bluetooth and ZigBee offer high data rate at low range and low power. Wi-Fi offers high data rate at medium range but high power. LoRaWAN and Sigfox offer long range and very low power but extremely low data rate. NB-IoT/LTE-M offer long range, moderate data rate, and licensed reliability."*

The parameter driving these trade-offs is the **link budget** (sensitivity vs. transmit power vs. bandwidth), which we will quantify in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Every Access Technology

Any access technology, whether wireless or wired, is characterised by four engineering parameters. These are the **"Four Pillars"** that every KTU question will test:

1. **Range (Coverage Area)** — Maximum distance between transmitter and receiver with acceptable BER.
2. **Data Rate (Throughput)** — Bits per second (bps) successfully delivered at the application layer.
3. **Power Consumption** — Average current drawn in active, sleep, and transmission states.
4. **Device Density / Scalability** — Number of devices supported per cell/gateway.

## 2.2 The Friis Transmission Equation — Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation for IoT Range Calculation:**
> The **Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** equation is the cornerstone of all wireless range calculations in KTU numerical problems.

$$
P_r \;=\; P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{\!2}
$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = received power (Watts)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (Watts)
- $G_t$ = transmitter antenna gain (linear)
- $G_r$ = receiver antenna gain (linear)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (metres)
- $d$ = distance between Tx and Rx (metres)

The **path loss in decibels** is given by:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(f) \;+\; 32.44 \quad (\text{d for km, f for MHz})
$$

### 2.3 Link Budget — The "Range Decider"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link Budget (dB):**
> $$
> \text{Link Budget} \;=\; P_{tx} \,(\text{dBm}) + G_{tx} \,(\text{dBi}) - P_{rx,\text{sens}} \,(\text{dBm})
> $$
> For successful communication, the link budget **must exceed** the total path loss + fade margin.

Where $P_{rx,\text{sens}}$ is the **receiver sensitivity** (typically in dBm). This is the minimum power a receiver can detect and still decode the signal — a smaller (more negative) value means a more sensitive receiver, which directly translates to longer range.

### 2.4 Shannon–Hartley Theorem — The Data Rate Limit

The maximum achievable data rate over a noisy channel is:

$$
C \;=\; B \log_{2}\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} \right) \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

Where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S$ is signal power, $N$ is noise power. This is why **LoRaWAN uses Chirp Spread Spectrum with very narrow bandwidth** (e.g., 125 kHz) — to tolerate very low $S/N$ ratios (down to **−20 dB**), enabling kilometres of range at the cost of kbps-level data rates.

### 2.5 Duty Cycle — Power Saving Mechanism

Most IoT devices are **battery-powered** and use the **duty cycle** principle:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **India's 865–867 MHz band regulation:** As per the WPC, devices in the **unlicensed 865–867 MHz ISM band** must follow a **duty cycle ≤ 10%** (i.e., transmit at most 6 minutes per hour) for Listen-Before-Talk (LBT) or duty-cycled operation. This is a high-frequency KTU exam point.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Technology Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — it is the single most-asked KTU 14-mark question pattern.**

| **Parameter** | **Bluetooth (Classic)** | **BLE (5.0)** | **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11n)** | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1)** | **Sigfox** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.4 | IEEE 802.11 | LoRa Alliance | 3GPP Release 13 | Sigfox SAS |
| **Frequency Band** | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 868/915 MHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 433/868/915 MHz, **865–867 MHz (India)** | Licensed LTE bands | **865–867 MHz (India)** |
| **Range** | ~10 m | ~100 m | 10–100 m | 50–100 m | 2–15 km | 1–10+ km | 10–40 km (urban/rural) |
| **Data Rate** | 1–3 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 54–600 Mbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL) | 100–600 bps |
| **Modulation** | FHSS, GFSK | GFSK | O-QPSK, DSSS | OFDM, MIMO | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | QPSK / BPSK | UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band) DBPSK |
| **Power (Tx)** | ~10 mW | ~10 mW | ~30 mW | ~100–500 mW | ~14–20 dBm (25–100 mW) | ~23 dBm (200 mW) | ~14 dBm (25 mW) |
| **Topology** | Piconet (Star) | Star / Mesh | Star, Tree, Mesh | Star (AP-based) | Star-of-stars | Star (Cellular) | Star |
| **Battery Life** | Days | Months–Years | Months–Years | Hours–Days | Years (10+) | Years (10+) | Years (10+) |
| **Max Devices/Cell** | 7 (Classic) | ~2000 | ~65,000 | ~32 clients | Millions | ~50,000/cell | 1 Million/hub |
| **Security** | E0 / SAFER+ | AES-128 | AES-128 CCM | WPA2/3 | AES-128 | 3GPP security | AES-128 |
| **Best Use Case** | Audio streaming | Beacons, Wearables | Home automation, WSN | Video, Browsing | Smart Agriculture, Metering | Smart Cities, Asset Tracking | Simple sensor uplink |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Technologies

- **Smart Agriculture in Palakkad / Wayanad**: Uses **LoRaWAN** soil-moisture sensors because they must run for 10+ years on a single battery and reach the gateway across several kilometres of uneven terrain.
- **Hospital Patient Monitoring (BLE)**: Uses BLE because the patient's wrist sensor only needs to talk to a smartphone or bedside gateway within 5–10 metres, but for *years* on a coin cell.
- **Smart Street Lighting (NB-IoT)**: Uses **NB-IoT** because it leverages existing mobile towers, supports deep indoor penetration, and is licensed (reliable for municipal SLAs).
- **Industrial 4.0 (ZigBee/WirelessHART)**: Uses ZigBee mesh for robust, self-healing factory floor monitoring where line-of-sight is unreliable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range Calculation from Friis Equation

**Problem:** A LoRaWAN end device transmits at $P_t = 14$ dBm with a 2 dBi antenna. The gateway has a 6 dBi antenna and a sensitivity of $P_{rx,\text{sens}} = -137$ dBm. The operating frequency is **868 MHz**. Assuming free-space propagation and a 10 dB fade margin, calculate the **maximum range**.

### Step 1 — Convert dBm to dBm differences (already in dB)
Since we will work entirely in the logarithmic (dB) domain, the Friis equation becomes:

$$
P_r \,(\text{dBm}) \;=\; P_t \,(\text{dBm}) + G_t \,(\text{dBi}) + G_r \,(\text{dBi}) - \text{FSPL (dB)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the available link budget
The available link budget is the difference between transmitted EIRP and receiver sensitivity:

$$
\text{Link Budget} \;=\; 14 + 2 + 6 - (-137) \;=\; 14 + 2 + 6 + 137 \;=\; 159 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This enormous **159 dB link budget** is what makes LoRaWAN "long range" — compare to Wi-Fi's typical 100 dB.

### Step 3 — Subtract the fade margin
Subtracting the safety margin gives the maximum allowable path loss:

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\max} \;=\; 159 - 10 \;=\; 149 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the simplified path-loss formula
Using the practical formula for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} \;=\; 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

Substituting $f = 868$ MHz:

$$
20 \log_{10}(868) \;=\; 20 \times 2.9385 \;=\; 58.77 \text{ dB}
$$

Therefore:

$$
149 \;=\; 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 58.77
$$

Rearranging:

$$
20 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 149 - 32.44 - 58.77 \;=\; 57.79
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $d$

$$
\log_{10}(d) \;=\; \dfrac{57.79}{20} \;=\; 2.8895
$$

$$
d \;=\; 10^{2.8895} \;\approx\; 776 \text{ km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world caveat:** Free-space Friis assumes a clear line of sight with no obstacles. In practice, LoRaWAN in a suburban environment achieves **5–10 km**. The 776 km result is a **theoretical upper bound**; KTU problems often ask you to compute the theoretical maximum and comment on real-world limits.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime from Duty Cycle

**Problem:** A BLE temperature sensor uses a CR2032 coin cell (capacity $C = 220$ mAh, voltage = 3 V). It draws 5 mA while transmitting for 3 ms, and 1 µA in sleep mode. The sensor wakes up once every 10 seconds. Calculate the **battery life in years**.

### Step 1 — Total cycle time

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; 10 \text{ s} \;=\; 10{,}000 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute average current

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot t_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3}) + (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (10 - 0.003)}{10}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-6} + 9.997 \times 10^{-6}}{10} \;=\; \dfrac{24.997 \times 10^{-6}}{10} \;=\; 2.4997 \,\mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute battery life

$$
\text{Life (hours)} \;=\; \dfrac{C}{I_{\text{avg}}} \;=\; \dfrac{220 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ah}}{2.4997 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}} \;\approx\; 88{,}015 \text{ hours}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
\text{Life (years)} \;=\; \dfrac{88{,}015}{24 \times 365} \;\approx\; 10.05 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world derating:** Battery datasheets specify a 5–10× self-discharge factor. Practical life is **1–2 years**. KTU problems may ask for *idealised* vs *practical* lifetimes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Budget for ZigBee vs Wi-Fi Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the maximum range of ZigBee (250 kbps, $P_{rx,\text{sens}} = -100$ dBm, 2.4 GHz) and Wi-Fi (54 Mbps, $P_{rx,\text{sens}} = -65$ dBm, 2.4 GHz). Assume $P_t = 0$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and a 15 dB fade margin.

### Step 1 — Link budget for ZigBee

$$
L_{\text{ZigBee}} \;=\; 0 + 2 + 2 - (-100) - 15 \;=\; 89 \text{ dB}
$$

For 2.4 GHz:

$$
20 \log_{10}(2400) \;=\; 67.6 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 89 - 32.44 - 67.6 \;=\; -11.04
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) \;=\; -0.552 \;\Rightarrow\; d_{\text{ZigBee}} \;\approx\; 0.28 \text{ km} \;=\; 280 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Link budget for Wi-Fi

$$
L_{\text{Wi-Fi}} \;=\; 0 + 2 + 2 - (-65) - 15 \;=\; 54 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 54 - 32.44 - 67.6 \;=\; -46.04
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) \;=\; -2.302 \;\Rightarrow\; d_{\text{Wi-Fi}} \;\approx\; 0.00497 \text{ km} \;\approx\; 5 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive insight (KTU favourite!):** At the **same transmit power, ZigBee (250 kbps) has ~56× the range of Wi-Fi (54 Mbps)** in theory. The reason is that the **higher the data rate, the wider the bandwidth, the higher the noise floor, and the poorer the sensitivity** (Shannon's theorem). This is the fundamental reason *why* low-power IoT protocols deliberately sacrifice data rate for range.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree

Use this decision flow for KTU design questions:

> **Step 1 → Range requirement?**
> - < 10 m → Bluetooth / NFC / RFID
> - 10–100 m → BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi
> - > 1 km → LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M
>
> **Step 2 → Data rate?**
> - < 1 kbps (sensor telemetry) → Sigfox, LoRaWAN
> - 1–100 kbps → LoRaWAN, NB-IoT
> - 100 kbps–1 Mbps → BLE, ZigBee
> - > 1 Mbps → Wi-Fi, 5G
>
> **Step 3 → Power budget?**
> - Years on coin cell → BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN
> - Hours on lithium-ion → Wi-Fi, NB-IoT
>
> **Step 4 → Mobility?**
> - Yes (vehicles, livestock) → Cellular (NB-IoT / LTE-M / 5G)
> - No (static sensors) → Any
>
> **Step 5 → Indoor penetration?**
> - Deep indoor (basements, meters) → NB-IoT (licensed, 164 dB MCL)
> - Outdoor line-of-sight → LoRaWAN
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Access Technology Landscape — Classification Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[IoT Access Technologies]
    Root --> SR[Short Range - WPAN]
    Root --> MR[Medium Range - WLAN]
    Root --> LR[Long Range - LPWAN]

    SR --> BT[Bluetooth Classic 802.15.1]
    SR --> BLE[BLE 5.0]
    SR --> ZB[ZigBee 802.15.4]
    SR --> NFC[NFC ISO 14443]
    SR --> RFID[RFID]

    MR --> WIFI[Wi-Fi 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax]
    MR --> THREAD[Thread on 802.15.4]

    LR --> LIC[Licensed Cellular]
    LR --> UNL[Unlicensed Sub-GHz]

    LIC --> NBIOT[NB-IoT Cat-NB1/NB2]
    LIC --> LTEM[LTE-M Cat-M1]
    LIC --> ECGSM[EC-GSM-IoT]
    LIC --> FG[5G mMTC and URLLC]

    UNL --> LORA[LoRaWAN]
    UNL --> SIG[Sigfox]
    UNL --> ING[Ingenu RPMA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for examiners:** Notice the three-tier structure: Range ↓ → Power ↓ → Cost ↓ (roughly), Data rate ↑. This is the classic IoT **"trilemma"** made visual.

## 4.2 LoRaWAN Network Architecture — Star-of-Stars Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    ED1[Sensor Node 1]
    ED2[Sensor Node 2]
    ED3[Sensor Node N - thousands]
    GW1[LoRaWAN Gateway 1]
    GW2[LoRaWAN Gateway 2]
    NS[Network Server]
    APP[Application Server]
    CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard]

    ED1 -. LoRa CSS 868/915 MHz .-> GW1
    ED2 -. LoRa CSS .-> GW1
    ED3 -. LoRa CSS .-> GW2
    GW1 --> NS
    GW2 --> NS
    NS --> APP
    APP --> CLOUD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Observation:** LoRaWAN uses a **star-of-stars** topology. End devices do **NOT** talk to each other — they only talk **up** to one or more gateways. The gateways forward raw packets (no MAC-layer processing) to a central **Network Server**, which de-duplicates, decrypts, and routes the data. This is fundamentally different from ZigBee, which supports true **device-to-device mesh**.

## 4.3 IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee Protocol Stack vs OSI

```mermaid
graph TB
    OSI7[OSI Application Layer]
    OSI6[OSI Presentation Layer]
    OSI5[OSI Session Layer]
    OSI4[OSI Transport Layer]
    OSI3[OSI Network Layer]
    OSI2[OSI Data Link Layer]
    OSI1[OSI Physical Layer]

    ZB[ZigBee Application Objects]
    ZAPS[ZigBee APS Layer]
    ZNW[ZigBee NWK Layer with AODV routing]
    IEEEMAC[IEEE 802.15.4 MAC CSMA-CA and GTS]
    IEEEPHY[IEEE 802.15.4 PHY O-QPSK 2.4 GHz or BPSK 868 MHz]

    OSI7 -. Maps to .-> ZB
    OSI6 -. Maps to .-> ZAPS
    OSI5 -. Maps to .-> ZNW
    OSI4 -. Maps to .-> IEEEMAC
    OSI3 -. Maps to .-> IEEEMAC
    OSI2 -. Maps to .-> IEEEMAC
    OSI1 -. Maps to .-> IEEEPHY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key fact for KTU:** ZigBee **only defines layers 3–7** (NWK to APP). The lower two layers (PHY and MAC) are defined by **IEEE 802.15.4**. The combination of **IEEE 802.15.4 + ZigBee** is the complete protocol stack. **6LoWPAN** is an alternative upper-layer protocol that can also run on IEEE 802.15.4 to support true IPv6 on tiny devices.

## 4.4 NB-IoT vs LTE-M vs EC-GSM-IoT Spectrum Allocation

```mermaid
graph LR
    LTEBAND[LTE Carrier Band - example 10 MHz]
    INBAND[NB-IoT In-Band - inside LTE resource blocks]
    GB[NB-IoT Guard Band - unused guard bands of LTE]
    SSB[NB-IoT Standalone - refarmed GSM spectrum]
    LTEMBAND[LTE-M occupies 1.4 MHz = 6 RBs]
    ECGSM[EC-GSM-IoT on 200 kHz GSM channels]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three NB-IoT deployment modes (3GPP Release 13):**
> 1. **In-band:** Uses LTE resource blocks inside a normal LTE carrier (saves spectrum but limited capacity).
> 2. **Guard-band:** Uses the unused guard bands of an LTE carrier (180 kHz each side).
> 3. **Standalone:** Uses re-farmed 2G GSM spectrum — most popular for greenfield IoT deployments.

## 4.5 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) Advertisement & Connection Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Adv as BLE Peripheral - Sensor
    participant Obs as BLE Observer - Smartphone
    participant Conn as BLE Central - Phone connects

    Adv->>Obs: ADV_IND on 3 channels 37, 38, 39
    Obs->>Adv: SCAN_REQ
    Adv->>Obs: SCAN_RSP with device name and services
    Note over Obs,Adv: Connection established
    Conn->>Adv: CONNECTION_REQ with hop sequence
    Adv->>Conn: GATT notifications every 1 second
    Adv->>Adv: Sleep mode - average current 1 microA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Point:** BLE uses **40 RF channels** in 2.4 GHz, of which **3 are advertising channels** (37, 38, 39) chosen to avoid Wi-Fi channel 1/6/11 interference. Data channels use **adaptive frequency hopping (AFH)** to dodge interference — this is why BLE coexists with Wi-Fi so well.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define IoT Access Technologies. Briefly classify them based on range. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** IoT Access Technologies are the physical and data-link layer protocols that enable short-to-long-range wireless/wired communication between IoT end devices (sensors/actuators) and the gateway or base station that connects them to the internet. They are responsible for the "last-mile" connectivity of the IoT stack.
>
> **Classification based on range (2 marks):**
> 1. **Short-Range WPAN (< 10 m):** Bluetooth, BLE, NFC, RFID
> 2. **Medium-Range WLAN (~10–100 m):** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), ZigBee, Thread
> 3. **Long-Range LPWAN (2–15 km):** LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M
>
> **Closing line:** The choice depends on the trade-off between range, data rate, power, and cost — a key design consideration in IoT deployments.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between ZigBee and LoRaWAN in terms of range, data rate, and topology. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Parameter** | **ZigBee** | **LoRaWAN** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Range** | 10–100 m (personal area) | 2–15 km (wide area) |
> | **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps |
> | **Topology** | Mesh (star, tree, mesh) | Star-of-stars |
>
> **Conclusion:** ZigBee is suited for short-range, high-data-rate, mesh-based local networks (e.g., home automation). LoRaWAN is designed for long-range, low-data-rate, battery-efficient applications (e.g., smart agriculture, smart metering).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **LoRaWAN Deep Dive**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a LoRaWAN network with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Network Server and the Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) mechanism. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A LoRaWAN end device operates at 868 MHz with $P_t = 14$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and a receiver sensitivity of $-137$ dBm. Considering a 10 dB fade margin, calculate the theoretical maximum range in free space. Justify why real-world range is much lower. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A LoRaWAN network follows the **star-of-stars** topology consisting of four components:

1. **End Devices (ED):** Battery-powered sensors/actuators that transmit LoRa-modulated frames using **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)**. They are Class A, B, or C.
2. **Gateways (GW):** Transparent packet forwarders. They receive LoRa frames from any device in range and forward them via IP (Ethernet/4G) to the Network Server. They perform **no MAC-layer processing** — they are essentially "dumb radios with a backhaul".
3. **Network Server (NS):** The central brain. It performs:
   - **Deduplication** (the same packet may arrive at multiple gateways)
   - **Security** (decryption using the AppSKey, MIC verification using NwkSKey)
   - **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate) management** — explained below
   - **MAC command scheduling**
4. **Application Server (AS):** The user's application that consumes the decrypted payload (e.g., dashboard, database, ML pipeline).

**Class A vs B vs C devices (2 marks):**
- **Class A (default):** Device opens two receive windows after every uplink. Lowest power. **Mandatory for all LoRaWAN devices.**
- **Class B:** Synchronised downlink windows via periodic beacons. Useful for actuation.
- **Class C:** Continuously listening except when transmitting. Highest power, used for mains-powered actuators.

**Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) (2 marks):**
ADR is a **Network Server algorithm** that optimises the **Spreading Factor (SF)** and **transmit power** of each end device individually.
- **High SF (SF12):** Long range, low data rate, longer time-on-air.
- **Low SF (SF7):** Short range, high data rate, shorter time-on-air.

The NS analyses the **SNR and RSSI** of the last ~20 uplinks. If the link is too "good" (high SNR), it sends a MAC command to the device to **reduce SF or transmit power**. This saves battery life, reduces spectrum congestion, and increases network capacity. ADR is a **mandatory server-side feature** that distinguishes LoRaWAN from simple point-to-point LoRa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Just-add diagram (see Section 4.2 of these notes):** Draw a star topology with multiple EDs → multiple GWs → one NS → one AS, and label the data flow with LoRa (over-the-air) vs IP (backhaul).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute available link budget.

$$
L_{\text{available}} \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,\text{sens}} \;=\; 14 + 2 + 2 - (-137) \;=\; 155 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Subtract fade margin.

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\max} \;=\; 155 - 10 \;=\; 145 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply simplified path loss formula.

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} \;=\; 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(868)
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(868) \;=\; 20 \times 2.9385 \;=\; 58.77 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Solve for $d$.

$$
145 \;=\; 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 58.77
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 145 - 32.44 - 58.77 \;=\; 53.79
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) \;=\; 2.6895
$$

$$
d \;=\; 10^{2.6895} \;\approx\; 489 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Justify real-world reduction.

Free-space Friis gives the **theoretical maximum** assuming:
- Ideal isotropic antennas
- Perfect line-of-sight
- No obstructions
- No atmospheric absorption
- No ground reflection
- No multipath fading

In **real Indian urban/suburban conditions**:
- Buildings cause **multipath fading** and shadowing (typically 20–30 dB additional loss).
- Vegetation attenuation in Kerala's tropical climate adds another 5–10 dB.
- Ground reflection creates constructive/destructive interference.
- Antenna misalignment, connector losses, and cable losses reduce actual gain.

Thus, the practical range drops to **5–10 km in suburban** and **2–5 km in dense urban** environments. KTU examiners specifically want you to mention "Friis assumes free space, but real deployments face multipath, shadowing, and obstruction losses."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **NB-IoT & Cellular IoT Comparison**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2025 - Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Compare NB-IoT, LTE-M, and EC-GSM-IoT in terms of bandwidth, peak data rate, mobility support, and typical use case. Why is NB-IoT preferred for stationary, deep-indoor IoT applications? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the three deployment modes of NB-IoT (in-band, guard-band, standalone) with a diagram. Explain why a maximum coupling loss (MCL) of 164 dB makes NB-IoT suitable for basement-level meter reading. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1)** | **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** | **EC-GSM-IoT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | 180 kHz | 1.4 MHz | 200 kHz (re-farmed GSM) |
| **Peak Data Rate (DL/UL)** | 26 / 62 kbps | 1 / 1 Mbps | 70 / 70 kbps |
| **Mobility (handover)** | Not supported (stationary) | Full handover supported | Limited handover |
| **Voice support** | No | No | No |
| **Modulation** | BPSK / QPSK | 16-QAM | GMSK / 8-PSK |
| **Power saving** | PSM + eDRX (long) | PSM + eDRX | eDRX |
| **Typical Use Case** | Smart metering, asset tracking, sensors | Wearables, vehicle telematics, POS | Smart metering in 2G-replaced regions |

**Why NB-IoT is preferred for stationary, deep-indoor applications (3 marks):**

1. **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) of 164 dB:** This is the single most important number. MCL is the maximum path loss the link can tolerate while still maintaining a connection. NB-IoT's **164 dB MCL** is **20 dB better than LTE-M (144 dB)** and **40 dB better than normal LTE (124 dB)**. Each 6 dB doubles the range, so NB-IoT can penetrate **basement walls, concrete, and metal cabinets** where ordinary cellular signals fail.
2. **No handover support → Lower device cost and complexity:** Since devices are stationary, mobility is unnecessary. This reduces chipset cost.
3. **Narrow bandwidth (180 kHz):** Coherent demodulation becomes easier in noisy, deep-indoor channels.
4. **Massive device density:** ~50,000 devices per cell sector — essential for smart-city-scale deployments.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Three Deployment Modes (4 marks):**

1. **In-Band Mode:** NB-IoT occupies **one LTE Physical Resource Block (PRB = 180 kHz)** inside an active LTE carrier. Operators do not need new spectrum; they simply allocate one PRB from their existing LTE bandwidth. Drawback: capacity is limited by existing LTE traffic.
2. **Guard-Band Mode:** NB-IoT uses the **unused guard bands** (typically 200 kHz each side) of an LTE carrier. No impact on LTE capacity. Slightly better isolation.
3. **Standalone Mode:** NB-IoT operates in **re-farmed 2G/3G spectrum** (e.g., the 900 MHz GSM band being vacated in India). This is the **most popular greenfield deployment mode** in India because operators like Jio and Airtel are re-farming old GSM spectrum for IoT.

**Block diagram (text representation — refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for the Mermaid version):**

> LTE carrier (e.g., 10 MHz total) | In-band PRB | Guard-band slots (200 kHz each) | Standalone re-farmed 2G spectrum

**Why 164 dB MCL enables basement-level metering (3 marks):**

A standard smartphone needs ~120–130 dB MCL. Indian urban basements with **reinforced concrete and multiple floors** can introduce 130–150 dB of path loss. NB-IoT's **164 dB MCL** provides a 20–30 dB **link margin**, ensuring that even a **smart gas meter installed in the basement of a Kochi apartment building** can communicate with a rooftop base station several hundred metres away. This is why Indian gas companies (Mahanagar Gas, Adani Gas) and electricity boards (KSEB) are deploying NB-IoT rather than Wi-Fi or LoRa.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Confusing "LoRa" with "LoRaWAN":** LoRa is the **physical-layer modulation** (CSS by Semtech). LoRaWAN is the **MAC-layer protocol** by the LoRa Alliance. Students often interchange them. *Examiner will deduct 1 mark if confused.*
> 2. **Forgetting the 10 dB fade margin:** In range calculations, the FSPL formula gives **free-space** loss. Real designs need a **fade margin of 8–15 dB** to handle multipath and shadowing. *Common 2-mark deduction.*
> 3. **Mixing dBm with dBW or linear Watts:** Always convert to the **same dB unit** before adding/subtracting. $0$ dBm = $-30$ dBW = $1$ mW. $-3$ dB = **half** the power.
> 4. **Saying "ZigBee works on TCP/IP":** ZigBee does **not** use IP by default. For IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4, you need **6LoWPAN** (which is a *different* upper-layer protocol). *Direct 2-mark cut.*
> 5. **Forgetting India's 865–867 MHz band:** In Indian-context KTU questions, the LoRa/Sigfox frequency is **865–867 MHz** (WPC-allocated), **not** 868 MHz (Europe) or 915 MHz (USA). Examiners reward this specificity.
> 6. **Not distinguishing WPAN/WLAN/LPWAN acronyms:** Examiner's favourite 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- IoT Access Technologies = the **PHY + MAC layer** protocols connecting IoT devices to gateways.
- Three major classes: **WPAN (short)**, **WLAN (medium)**, **LPWAN (long)**.
- The **IoT trilemma**: optimise any *two* of {range, power, data rate}.

### Key Standards to Memorise
- **IEEE 802.15.4** → PHY/MAC of ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, WirelessHART
- **IEEE 802.15.1** → Bluetooth Classic and BLE
- **IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax** → Wi-Fi family
- **IEEE 802.15.4g** → SUN (Smart Utility Networks) — used in some LPWANs
- **3GPP Release 13** → NB-IoT, LTE-M, EC-GSM-IoT

### The "Big Seven" IoT Access Technologies
1. **Bluetooth / BLE** — short range, 2.4 GHz, mesh possible with BLE 5.0
2. **ZigBee** — IEEE 802.15.4 + ZigBee Alliance, mesh, low power, 250 kbps
3. **Wi-Fi** — high data rate, 2.4/5/6 GHz, high power
4. **LoRaWAN** — CSS modulation, 865–867 MHz in India, **star-of-stars**, **164 dB link budget** via SF7–SF12
5. **Sigfox** — Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB), 100–600 bps, 12-byte payload, **star topology**
6. **NB-IoT** — 180 kHz, licensed, 164 dB MCL, 3GPP Release 13
7. **LTE-M** — 1.4 MHz, licensed, supports mobility and voice-over-LTE

### Numerical Formulas (3-4 marks secured)
- **FSPL (dB)** = $32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$
- **Link Budget (dB)** = $P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,\text{sens}}$
- **Battery Life (h)** = $C_{\text{Ah}} / I_{\text{avg}}$
- **Duty Cycle (%)** = $T_{\text{active}} / (T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}) \times 100$

### India-Specific Must-Know Facts
- **LoRaWAN/Sigfox band:** 865–867 MHz with **≤ 10% duty cycle** (WPC regulation)
- **NB-IoT operators in India:** Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel, Vodafone Idea (VIL)
- **ISM 2.4 GHz** is **globally license-free** (used by BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee)

### Pairing Mnemonic
> **"B-Z-W-L-S-N-L"** for short to long range:
> **B**luetooth → **Z**igBee → **W**i-Fi → **L**oRa → **S**igfox → **N**B-IoT → **L**TE-M
> (data rate decreases, range increases, power efficiency improves)

### Quick Decision Rule
- **Smart home / wearables / personal device?** → BLE
- **Industrial sensor mesh / home automation?** → ZigBee
- **Camera / video / high-throughput local?** → Wi-Fi
- **Agricultural sensor kilometres away?** → LoRaWAN
- **Urban smart-city metering / asset tracking / basement devices?** → NB-IoT
- **Moving vehicle telemetry?** → LTE-M
- **Ultra-low-cost fire-alarm button?** → Sigfox

> **End of Module 2 Topic: IoT Access Technologies**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Network Layer: IP as the IoT Network Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Network Layer: IP as the IoT Network Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Internet Protocol (IP)** serves as the universal **Network Layer (Layer 3 of the OSI / TCP-IP model)** for the Internet of Things (IoT). It provides the logical addressing, fragmentation/reassembly, and end-to-end packet routing services that allow heterogeneous smart objects (sensors, actuators, edge devices) to communicate over both constrained wireless networks and the global Internet using a single, unified protocol stack.

In the IoT layered protocol stack, the **Network Layer** sits immediately above the **MAC Layer (Layer 2)** and immediately below the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**. Its three canonical responsibilities are:

1. **Logical Addressing** — Assigning a globally unique IP address to every "thing."
2. **Packet Forwarding / Routing** — Deciding the next-hop for each datagram.
3. **Encapsulation/Decapsulation & Fragmentation** — Wrapping transport segments into IP datagrams and breaking oversized packets to traverse links with smaller **Maximum Transmission Units (MTU)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In IoT, the choice of IP (specifically **IPv6** with adaptations) is the linchpin that allows an IEEE 802.15.4 sensor node to talk directly to a cloud server without requiring expensive application-layer gateways (ALGs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Global Postal System"

Imagine IoT devices as **houses in different countries**, each wanting to mail a parcel to another house.

- The **house number + street + city + PIN code** is analogous to an **IP address** (logical address).
- The **Postal Department (India Post, USPS, Royal Mail)** is analogous to the **Internet / IP Routing Infrastructure** — every country follows the same addressing format so any parcel can cross any border.
- The **local postman who walks inside a colony** is analogous to the **adaptation layer** (e.g., **6LoWPAN**) that knows the narrow lanes (low-power links) of a constrained neighbourhood.

If every country used a different addressing style (like some non-IP IoT protocols do), you would need a translator at every border — slow, expensive, and error-prone. **IP is the common addressing language** that makes IoT truly *inter-networked*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| IPv4 Address Size | **32 bits** (≈ 4.29 × 10⁹ addresses) | Address exhaustion is a key IoT driver |
| IPv6 Address Size | **128 bits** (≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses) | Sufficient for every atom on Earth |
| IPv4 Header Size | **20–60 bytes** | Too large for IEEE 802.15.4 (127 B MTU) |
| IPv6 Header Size | **40 bytes (fixed)** | Needs compression for constrained links |
| IEEE 802.15.4 MTU | **127 bytes** | Forces header compression via 6LoWPAN |
| Typical IoT datagram payload | **≤ 60–80 bytes** | After adaptation & security overhead |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why IP for IoT? — The Strategic Rationale

The deployment of **IP at the IoT network layer** is not accidental; it is driven by five engineering imperatives:

### (a) Ubiquity & Reuse of Existing Infrastructure
The Internet is the largest deployed network in human history. Using IP allows IoT traffic to ride on **existing routers, switches, firewalls, NAT, DNS, and management tools** — eliminating the need for parallel "IoT-only" infrastructure.

### (b) End-to-End Connectivity
With IP, a soil-moisture sensor in a Kerala paddy field can communicate **directly** with a cloud analytics server in Singapore, without protocol-translation gateways. This preserves the **End-to-End Principle** of the Internet.

### (c) Scalability through Hierarchical Addressing
IPv6's **128-bit hierarchical address** structure supports aggregation, allowing billions of devices to be addressed without routing-table explosion.

### (d) Independence from the Underlying Physical/MAC Layer
IP is deliberately **media-agnostic**. It runs over Ethernet, Wi-Fi, IEEE 802.15.4, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, PLC, and even satellite links.

### (e) Mature Tooling & Security Ecosystem
IPsec, TLS/DTLS, SNMP, NetFlow, traceroute — decades of operational tooling are immediately available.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counterpoint (Why IP Was Initially Avoided):** Traditional IPv6 headers (40 B) are huge compared to IEEE 802.15.4's 127-byte frame, leaving little room for payload. This drove the creation of **adaptation layers (6LoWPAN)** and **header compression (IPHC)**.

## 2.2 IPv4 vs IPv6 for IoT — Comparative Analysis

| Dimension | IPv4 | IPv6 for IoT |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | 32-bit (~4.3 × 10⁹) | **128-bit (~3.4 × 10³⁸)** — virtually inexhaustible |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes (variable) | **40 bytes (fixed, simpler)** — faster router processing |
| Auto-configuration | DHCP required | **Stateless Address Auto-Configuration (SAA C)** — critical for sleeping/headless IoT nodes |
| Broadcast | Yes (broadcast storms) | Replaced by **multicast** (e.g., All-Nodes ff02::1) |
| Security | Optional (IPsec retrofit) | **IPsec originally mandated** in spec |
| Mobility | Mobile IP add-on | Native **Mobile IPv6** support |
| Fragmentation | Routers + hosts | **Only hosts** (routers never fragment) |
| Header Fields | 12 (with options) | 8 (cleaner, easier to compress) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** For practical IoT deployments, **IPv6 is the de-facto Network Layer protocol** because of address space, auto-configuration, and easier header compression.

## 2.3 The Adaptation Problem — 6LoWPAN

A raw IEEE 802.15.4 frame has only **127 bytes of MAC payload**. After subtracting:
- **MAC header + security (up to 25–29 bytes)**
- **Mesh-under / fragmentation headers**
- **A full 40-byte IPv6 header + 8-byte UDP header**

…there is often less than **40 bytes** left for application data. This is unacceptable.

**6LoWPAN** (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks, RFC 4944 / RFC 6282) is an **adaptation layer** inserted between the MAC and the IPv6 layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **6LoWPAN is NOT a replacement for IPv6. It is a thin shim that compresses IPv6 headers and fragments large packets to fit into small 802.15.4 frames.**

### Core Functions of 6LoWPAN
1. **Header Compression (IPHC)** — Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 + 8-byte UDP header down to as little as **6 bytes** by leveraging link-local context.
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — Splits oversized IPv6 packets (up to 1280 B) into multiple 802.15.4 frames, with a **fragment header** carrying offset/tag.
3. **Mesh Addressing** — Optional **mesh-addressing header** for multi-hop under the same PAN.
4. **Stateless Autoconfiguration** — Derives an IPv6 **Interface Identifier (IID)** from the 64-bit EUI-64 address.

## 2.4 Routing in the IoT Network Layer — RPL

For multi-hop IoT networks, traditional distance-vector or link-state protocols (OSPF, BGP) are far too "chatty" for **Lossy, Low-Power networks (LLNs)**.

**RPL — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RFC 6550)** is the IETF-standardized IPv6 routing protocol for IoT.

- Builds a **Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph (DODAG)** rooted at a border router (sink).
- Uses an **Objective Function (OF)** — typically **Minimum Rank with Hysteresis (MRHOF)** based on **Expected Transmission Count (ETX)**.
- Carries routing information via **ICMPv6 RPL Control Messages (DIO, DAO, DIS)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| IPv4 Address Capacity | $N_{v4} = 2^{32}$ | ≈ 4.29 × 10⁹ addresses |
| IPv6 Address Capacity | $N_{v6} = 2^{128}$ | ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses |
| Devices addressable per m² of Earth surface | $\dfrac{2^{128}}{5.1 \times 10^{14}}$ | ≈ 6.67 × 10²³ devices/m² (more than Avogadro-scale) |
| Maximum IEEE 802.15.4 MAC payload | $L_{max} = 127$ bytes | After MAC header |
| Usable application payload (no compression) | $L_{app} = 127 - 25 - 40 - 8 = 54$ bytes | Approximate |
| Usable application payload (with IPHC + UDP-NULL compression) | $L_{app} \approx 127 - 25 - 6 = 96$ bytes | After 6LoWPAN compression |
| IPv6 Header Compression Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{40 - L_{c}}{40} \times 100\%$ | $L_{c}$ = compressed header size |
| MTU for IPv6 over LoWPAN | $MTU_{IPv6} = 1280$ bytes | Must be supported logically |
| Fragmentation payload size | $L_{frag} = 127 - L_{mac} - L_{fragHdr}$ | $L_{fragHdr}$ typically **5 bytes** |
| Expected Transmission Count (ETX) | $ETX = \dfrac{1}{d_f \cdot d_r}$ | $d_f$ = forward delivery ratio, $d_r$ = reverse delivery ratio |
| Path ETX (RPL) | $ETX_{path} = \sum_{i \in path} ETX_i$ | Sum of link ETX values along DODAG path |
| Header Compression Efficiency (UDP/IPv6) | $\eta = \dfrac{48 - L_{comp}}{48} \times 100\%$ | Original 48 B = 40 IPv6 + 8 UDP |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (Kerala Use-Case):** Soil sensors using IPv6/6LoWPAN report directly to cloud dashboards without proprietary gateways.
- **Smart Metering (Tata Power, Reliance):** Millions of electricity meters identified uniquely over IPv6, queried via RPL.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** IPv6 over Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) provides deterministic factory-floor communication.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** End-to-end IPv6 from a patient's wristband to the hospital EHR system, secured by IPsec.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Application Payload in a Constrained IoT Frame

We derive the **maximum usable application bytes** in a single IEEE 802.15.4 frame carrying uncompressed IPv6/UDP traffic.

**Given:**
- IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame maximum size: $L_{frame} = 127$ bytes
- Typical MAC header + footer overhead: $L_{mac} = 25$ bytes (Frame Control 2B + Seq No 1B + Addressing 4–20B + Aux Security 0–21B + FCS 2B)
- For analysis, take worst-case minimal MAC overhead: $L_{mac,min} = 25$ bytes
- IPv6 header (fixed): $L_{IPv6} = 40$ bytes
- UDP header: $L_{UDP} = 8$ bytes

**Step 1 — Available MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU):**

The MAC Service Data Unit (the payload handed up to the upper layers) is:

$$L_{MSDU} = L_{frame} - L_{mac,min}$$

$$L_{MSDU} = 127 - 25 = 102 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the IPv6 Header:**

$$L_{IPv6\_payload} = L_{MSDU} - L_{IPv6}$$

$$L_{IPv6\_payload} = 102 - 40 = 62 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the UDP Header:**

$$L_{UDP\_payload} = L_{IPv6\_payload} - L_{UDP}$$

$$L_{UDP\_payload} = 62 - 8 = 54 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** In the *worst-case uncompressed* scenario, an IoT application gets only **54 bytes per 802.15.4 frame**. This is why **6LoWPAN header compression is not optional — it is mandatory** for practical IoT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Header Compression Efficiency with IPHC

**Given:** UDP/IPv6 header = $40 + 8 = 48$ bytes uncompressed.

**Step 1 — Apply IPHC (RFC 6282).** With full link-local context (both source and destination IPv6 addresses elided, both ports compressed to 1 byte, length inferred, checksum elided when using IPv6 extension headers), the compressed header becomes:

$$L_{comp} = L_{IPHC\_dispatch} + L_{UDP\_NHC} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ bytes}$$

(Plus a 1-byte hop limit in worst case → effective ~4 bytes. We use 6 bytes for conservative analysis.)

**Step 2 — Compute Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{L_{orig} - L_{comp}}{L_{orig}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{48 - 6}{48} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{42}{48} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 87.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Resulting Application Payload:**

$$L_{app,comp} = L_{MSDU} - L_{comp}$$

$$L_{app,comp} = 102 - 6 = 96 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** IPHC delivers nearly **1.78× more application payload per frame** (96 B vs 54 B), a critical gain for energy-starved IoT motes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Why IPv6 is Required for Massive IoT Scale

**Given:** Earth surface area ≈ $5.1 \times 10^{14}$ m².

**Step 1 — IPv4 device density:**

$$\sigma_{v4} = \frac{2^{32}}{5.1 \times 10^{14}} = \frac{4.29 \times 10^{9}}{5.1 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$\sigma_{v4} \approx 8.41 \times 10^{-6} \text{ devices/m}^2$$

That is **less than one IPv4 address per square kilometre**.

**Step 2 — IPv6 device density:**

$$\sigma_{v6} = \frac{2^{128}}{5.1 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$\sigma_{v6} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{23} \text{ devices/m}^2$$

This exceeds **Avogadro's number of addresses per m²** — sufficient to assign a unique IPv6 address to every grain of sand on every beach.

**Conclusion:** Address scarcity in IPv4 is a **physical, not just numerical**, limitation. IPv6 is the only mathematically credible option for true *per-device* addressing in massive IoT.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IP Adaptation Layers in IoT

| Protocol | Standard | Header | Fragmentation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **6LoWPAN** | RFC 4944 / 6282 | IPHC compression | Yes (first + subsequent) | IEEE 802.15.4 |
| **6Lo** (for BLE) | RFC 7668 | IPv6 over Bluetooth LE | Limited | Wearables |
| **6TiSCH** | RFC 9031 | 6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH | Scheduled slots | Industrial IoT |
| **6LoWPAN-GHC** | RFC 7400 | Generic Header Compression | Generic | Multiple link types |
| **SCHC** | RFC 8724 | Static Context Header Compression | App-layer agnostic | LPWAN (LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT) |
| **CORECONF** | RFC 9473 | Configuration over 6LoWPAN | N/A | Network management |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** **SCHC (Static Context Header Compression)** is gaining dominance in LPWAN IoT (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox) where link MTU is as low as **≤ 51 bytes** (LoRaWAN).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An IoT temperature sensor transmits 4 bytes of payload (a single float) using UDP over IPv6/6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4. Calculate:
1. Frame overhead (uncompressed).
2. Frame overhead with full IPHC compression.
3. The energy saving per packet if radio energy is 1 µJ/byte.

**Step 1 — Uncompressed:**

Total frame = $L_{mac} + L_{IPv6} + L_{UDP} + L_{app}$

$$= 25 + 40 + 8 + 4 = 77 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — With IPHC (compressed):**

Compressed IPv6 + UDP header ≈ 6 bytes.

Total frame = $25 + 6 + 4 = 35 \text{ bytes}$

**Step 3 — Energy Comparison:**

$$E_{uncomp} = 77 \times 1\,\mu J = 77\,\mu J$$

$$E_{comp} = 35 \times 1\,\mu J = 35\,\mu J$$

$$\Delta E = 77 - 35 = 42\,\mu J \text{ saved per packet}$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{42}{77} \times 100\% \approx 54.5\%$$
