# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive Analytics. Give one IoT example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Question Answered | Technique | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Descriptive | What happened? | Aggregation, dashboards | "Average factory temperature last week was 34°C" |
| Diagnostic | Why did it happen? | Drill-down, correlation | "Temperature spiked because compressor failed" |
| Predictive | What will happen? | ML, time-series forecasting | "Motor will fail in 3 days based on vibration pattern" |
| Prescriptive | What should we do? | Optimization, RL | "Reduce motor speed to 70% and replace bearing Friday" |

*(Award 0.5 mark for table clarity, 0.5 mark for each correct row = 3 marks total)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Big Data characteristics (5Vs) with respect to IoT data.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **5Vs model** characterizes IoT Big Data:

1. **Volume (1 mark):** IoT generates massive data — e.g., a smart city produces 500+ TB/day from traffic cameras, sensors, and meters.
2. **Velocity (1 mark):** Data arrives in real-time at high speed — e.g., 100,000 sensor readings per second from a manufacturing plant.
3. **Variety (0.5 mark):** Multiple formats coexist — temperature (structured), CCTV footage (unstructured), JSON logs (semi-structured).
4. **Veracity (0.5 mark):** Sensor data is noisy, contains missing values, and has calibration errors requiring cleaning.

*(Accept 5Vs (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) or 6Vs/7Vs — award 1 mark per V explained)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end IoT Data Analytics architecture**. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Edge, Fog, and Cloud Analytics** in detail. Under what circumstances should each be preferred? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

The IoT Data Analytics architecture consists of **five distinct layers**, each performing a specific role in transforming raw sensor signals into actionable intelligence:

1. **Sensing / Edge Layer (1.5 marks):** Comprises physical sensors (temperature, pressure, motion, cameras) and edge microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi). This layer acquires real-world data. Some preliminary **edge analytics** (filtering, threshold checks) may be done here to reduce data volume.

2. **Communication Layer (1.5 marks):** Data is transmitted using protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP** over **Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRaWAN, Zigbee, or 5G**. Selection depends on range, bandwidth, and power constraints. MQTT is the most common for IoT due to its lightweight publish-subscribe model.

3. **Ingestion Layer (1 mark):** Receives high-velocity data streams using brokers like **Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, or MQTT brokers**. Stream processors (Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) perform initial parsing and routing.

4. **Storage and Processing Layer (1.5 marks):** Time-series databases (**InfluxDB, TimescaleDB**) store sensor data efficiently. Data lakes (**HDFS, Amazon S3**) hold raw unstructured data. Batch processing uses **Hadoop/MapReduce**; stream processing uses **Apache Spark**.

5. **Analytics and Application Layer (1.5 marks):** ML models (regression, classification, clustering) run on stored or streaming data. Results are visualized via **Grafana, Kibana, Power BI**. Alerts trigger downstream actuators.

> The architecture enables the **flow: Sense → Transmit → Ingest → Store → Analyze → Act**, forming a closed-loop IoT analytics pipeline.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | Edge | Fog | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | On sensor/device | Gateway | Remote datacenter |
| **Latency** | < 1 ms | 1–50 ms | 100+ ms |
| **Compute** | Limited | Medium | Massive (GPU) |
| **Storage** | KB–MB | GB | PB |
| **Internet Dependency** | None | Optional | Required |
| **Privacy** | Highest | Medium | Lower |

**When to use (1.5 marks each):**

- **Edge Analytics** is preferred when **latency is critical** (e.g., autonomous vehicle collision avoidance, robotic surgery) or when **internet is unavailable** (remote oil rigs, underwater sensors). It also protects **data privacy** (e.g., in-home healthcare devices).

- **Fog Analytics** is preferred for **intermediate processing** in a smart factory, where multiple sensors aggregate at a gateway to perform local analytics (e.g., shop-floor anomaly detection) before sending summary data to the cloud.

- **Cloud Analytics** is preferred when **massive compute, storage, and historical analysis** are needed — e.g., city-wide traffic modeling, training deep learning models on petabytes of historical data, or running sophisticated prescriptive optimization.

> **Modern IoT systems use a Hybrid approach:** Edge for time-critical decisions, Cloud for long-term intelligence.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice 2]

**(a)** What is **Predictive Analytics**? Explain the **Linear Regression algorithm** and derive the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) closed-form solution** for parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vibration sensor on a motor produced the following 5 readings (in mm/s): $x = \{2.1, 2.5, 2.8, 3.2, 3.6\}$.  
Corresponding bearing temperatures (in °C) recorded were: $y = \{45, 50, 55, 60, 70\}$.  
Fit a **linear regression model** $y = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ using OLS. Predict the temperature when vibration = **4.0 mm/s**. Also compute **R²** to evaluate fit quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Predictive Analytics (2 marks):** It is the branch of analytics that uses historical data, statistical algorithms, and machine learning techniques to **forecast future outcomes**. In IoT, it powers use-cases like predictive maintenance, demand forecasting, and anomaly detection.

**Linear Regression Model (2 marks):** A supervised learning algorithm that models the relationship between a continuous dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $x$ as a straight line:
$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$
where $\theta_0$ is the y-intercept and $\theta_1$ is the slope.

**OLS Derivation (3 marks):** We minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:
$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - h_\theta(x_i))^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i)^2$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} &= -2 \sum (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i) = 0 \\
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} &= -2 \sum x_i(y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving simultaneously yields the **closed-form OLS solution**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \\
\theta_0 &= \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $x = \{2.1, 2.5, 2.8, 3.2, 3.6\}$, $y = \{45, 50, 55, 60, 70\}$, $n = 5$.

**Step 1 — Means (0.5 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{2.1 + 2.5 + 2.8 + 3.2 + 3.6}{5} = \frac{14.2}{5} = 2.84 \\
\bar{y} &= \frac{45 + 50 + 55 + 60 + 70}{5} = \frac{280}{5} = 56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Deviation Table (1.5 marks):**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 45 | −0.74 | −11 | 8.14 | 0.5476 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 50 | −0.34 | −6 | 2.04 | 0.1156 |
| 3 | 2.8 | 55 | −0.04 | −1 | 0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 4 | 3.2 | 60 | +0.36 | +4 | 1.44 | 0.1296 |
| 5 | 3.6 | 70 | +0.76 | +14 | 10.64 | 0.5776 |
| **Σ** | | | | | **22.30** | **1.372** |

**Step 3 — Compute Parameters (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \frac{22.30}{1.372} \approx 16.254 \\
\theta_0 &= 56 - (16.254 \times 2.84) = 56 - 46.16 \approx 9.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Model (0.5 mark):**
$$\boxed{y = 9.84 + 16.254 \, x}$$

**Step 4 — Predict at x = 4.0 (0.5 mark):**
$$y = 9.84 + 16.254 \times 4.0 = 9.84 + 65.02 = 74.86^\circ \text{C}$$

**Step 5 — Compute R² (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 &= (-11)^2 + (-6)^2 + (-1)^2 + 4^2 + 14^2 = 121+36+1+16+196 = 370 \\
\text{SSR} &= \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 = \theta_1^2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (16.254)^2 \times 1.372 \approx 362.16 \\
R^2 &= \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{362.16}{370} \approx 0.9788
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** R² ≈ **97.88%** — the model explains ~98% of variance in temperature from vibration, indicating an **excellent linear fit**. When vibration reaches 4.0 mm/s, predicted bearing temperature is **74.86°C** — a likely warning threshold for maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write °C, mm/s, etc. (lose 0.5 mark per instance).
> 2. **Confusing σ² with σ:** Variance vs. Standard Deviation are NOT the same.
> 3. **Misapplying OLS:** Students often use $\sum xy / \sum x^2$ (wrong). Use the proper deviation formula.
> 4. **Skipping the final interpretation:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for *concluding insight*, not just calculation.
> 5. **No diagram = lose marks:** For 7-mark architecture questions, a labelled diagram is **mandatory**.
> 6. **Confusing R² interpretation:** R² = 1 means perfect fit; R² = 0 means linear model is useless.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **4 Analytics Types** → Descriptive (What), Diagnostic (Why), Predictive (When), Prescriptive (How).
- ⭐ **5Vs of Big Data** → Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value (remember with **"VVVVV"**).
- ⭐ **5-Layer Architecture** → Sense → Communicate → Ingest → Store → Analyze.
- ⭐ **Edge vs Cloud** → Edge = low latency, no internet, privacy. Cloud = massive compute, storage.
- ⭐ **OLS Formulas** → Master $\theta_1$ and $\theta_0$ derivation — **HIGH-YIELD 7-mark question**.
- ⭐ **Z-Score Outlier Detection** → $\vert z \vert > 2$ is the standard threshold.
- ⭐ **Normalization vs Standardization** → Min-Max scales to [0,1]; Z-score gives mean 0, std 1.
- ⭐ **Confusion Matrix Metrics** → Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 — *don't mix them up*.
- ⭐ **R² Interpretation** → Closer to 1 = better fit; 0.97+ is considered excellent.
- ⭐ **CRISP-DM Lifecycle** → 6 phases, cyclic with feedback loop.
- ⭐ **Real-time vs Batch** → Streaming for latency-critical (Kafka + Flink); Batch for periodic reports (Hadoop/Spark).
- ⭐ **ML in IoT** → Regression for forecasting, Classification for fault detection, Clustering for segmentation.
- ⭐ **IoT-Specific Storage** → Time-series DBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) outperform relational DBs for sensor data.
- ⭐ **Privacy & Security** → Edge analytics keeps data local, reducing attack surface.
- ⭐ **KTU Favourite Topics** → 5Vs explanation, 4-type analytics table, Edge vs Cloud comparison, OLS derivation with numerical, architecture diagram.

> **Final Mantra for PCADT602 Module 3:** *"Sensors collect, Networks transmit, Systems store, Analytics interprets, and Actuators act."*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables systems to automatically learn patterns, relationships, and decision rules from data and improve their performance over time **without being explicitly programmed** for every possible scenario. In the context of the KTU 2024 *Internet of Things* syllabus, ML is the analytical engine that transforms the massive volumes of raw data generated by sensors, actuators, and edge devices into **actionable intelligence**, **predictive insights**, and **autonomous decision-making capabilities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *“Machine Learning is the scientific study of algorithms and statistical models that computer systems use to perform a specific task effectively by relying on patterns and inference, instead of using explicit mathematical formulations or rule-based instructions.”*

Formally, an ML model can be described as a parameterized function:

$$\hat{y} = f_{\theta}(x)$$

Where:
- $x$ = Input feature vector (e.g., temperature, humidity, vibration readings from an IoT sensor)
- $\hat{y}$ = Predicted output
- $\theta$ = Set of learnable parameters (weights and biases)
- $f_{\theta}$ = The hypothesis function approximating the true underlying relationship

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Child Learning to Identify Fruits"

Imagine teaching a **toddler** to recognize an apple versus an orange:

1. The parent repeatedly **shows labelled examples** ("This is an apple — it is round and red. This is an orange — it is round and orange.").
2. Over time, the toddler identifies **hidden patterns** (color, texture, size) without the parent writing down a mathematical rule.
3. When shown a **new fruit**, the toddler can confidently classify it.

**Machine Learning works exactly like this:**

| Human Learning | Machine Learning Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Showing examples | **Training dataset** (labelled data) |
| Toddler's brain | **ML model** (e.g., Neural Network) |
| Identifying patterns | **Feature extraction & weight tuning** |
| Correcting mistakes | **Loss function & backpropagation** |
| Classifying new fruit | **Inference / Prediction on test data** |

> [!NOTE]
> In IoT, the "toddler" is a smart sensor node, the "fruits" are sensor readings, and the "examples" are thousands of historical data points collected over time.

## 1.3 Why Machine Learning is CRITICAL for IoT

IoT devices generate **zettabytes** of unstructured sensor data every year. Traditional rule-based programming cannot scale to handle this volume, variety, and velocity. ML enables IoT systems to:

- **Predict** future sensor values (predictive maintenance in smart factories).
- **Classify** events (anomaly detection in security cameras).
- **Cluster** similar devices (device fingerprinting).
- **Make decisions autonomously** (self-driving cars, smart grids).
- **Optimize** resource consumption (energy-aware edge computing).

## 1.4 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning

```text
Machine Learning
├── 1. Supervised Learning        (Labeled data → Learn input→output mapping)
│   ├── Classification           (Discrete output: spam/not-spam)
│   └── Regression               (Continuous output: temperature prediction)
│
├── 2. Unsupervised Learning      (Unlabeled data → Find hidden structure)
│   ├── Clustering               (Group similar data: K-Means)
│   ├── Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE)
│   └── Association              (Market basket analysis)
│
└── 3. Reinforcement Learning     (Learn by interacting with environment)
    ├── Agent
    ├── Environment
    ├── Reward Signal
    └── Policy (Action strategy)
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"List the types of ML"*, always write all **three types** with **one-line definitions** and **one real-world IoT example** for each. This fetches full marks.

## 1.5 ML Workflow in an IoT Pipeline

The end-to-end lifecycle of an ML model deployed in an IoT ecosystem consists of **seven** distinct stages:

1. **Data Acquisition** — Sensors collect raw readings.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Cleaning, normalization, handling missing values.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Selecting relevant attributes (e.g., rolling mean of vibration).
4. **Model Selection** — Choosing the right algorithm (SVM, Random Forest, LSTM).
5. **Training & Validation** — Feeding labelled data and tuning hyperparameters.
6. **Deployment** — Pushing the trained model to edge devices or cloud.
7. **Monitoring & Retraining** — Tracking model drift and updating periodically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — Operational Logic

In supervised learning, every training example is a **pair** $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i$ is the input feature vector and $y_i$ is the corresponding ground-truth label. The objective is to learn a function $f_{\theta}$ that minimizes the discrepancy between predictions $\hat{y}_i$ and true labels $y_i$.

### 2.1.1 Linear Regression (Regression Family)

Linear regression models the relationship between input features and a continuous output as a **linear combination**:

$$\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Operational Steps:**

- **Step 1:** Initialize weights $w$ and bias $b$ randomly (or to zero).
- **Step 2:** For each training example, compute prediction $\hat{y}_i$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **loss** (error) using Mean Squared Error.
- **Step 4:** Update weights using **Gradient Descent** to minimize loss.
- **Step 5:** Repeat for many epochs until convergence.

### 2.1.2 Logistic Regression (Classification Family)

Despite its name, logistic regression is a **classification** algorithm. It uses the **sigmoid function** to squash the linear output into a probability range $[0, 1]$:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

The predicted class is determined by thresholding at **0.5**:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(z) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.3 Decision Trees

A decision tree recursively partitions the feature space based on the feature that yields the **highest information gain** (or lowest Gini impurity). At each node, the algorithm selects the best split.

$$\text{Information Gain} = H(\text{Parent}) - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{N_j}{N} H(\text{Child}_j)$$

$$\text{Gini Impurity} = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

### 2.1.4 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)

A **lazy, non-parametric** algorithm that classifies a query point by majority vote of its **$K$ closest neighbors** in feature space (using Euclidean distance):

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_{q,j} - x_{i,j})^2}$$

### 2.1.5 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

SVM finds the **optimal hyperplane** that maximizes the margin between two classes. For non-linearly separable data, it uses the **kernel trick** (RBF, polynomial) to project data into higher dimensions.

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 K-Means Clustering

- **Step 1:** Choose $K$ cluster centers (centroids) randomly.
- **Step 2:** Assign each data point to its nearest centroid.
- **Step 3:** Recompute centroids as the mean of all points in the cluster.
- **Step 4:** Repeat Steps 2–3 until centroids stabilize.

**Objective Function (minimized):**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{c_i = k\} \cdot \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert^2$$

### 2.2.2 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

PCA performs **orthogonal linear transformation** to project data onto directions of **maximum variance**. The new axes (principal components) are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

## 2.3 Reinforcement Learning — Operational Logic

RL is modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** defined by the tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $S$ | Set of all possible **states** |
| $A$ | Set of all possible **actions** |
| $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Transition probability |
| $R(s, a)$ | **Reward** function |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor $(0 \leq \gamma \leq 1)$ |

The agent learns a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all board-critical formulas** for the Machine Learning module. Memorize the equation, the variable meanings, and at least one **IoT context** for each.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Where Used (IoT Context) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Regression Hypothesis | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Predicting room temperature |
| 2 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Loss function for regression |
| 3 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust regression metric |
| 4 | Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as target |
| 5 | Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Logistic regression output |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\frac{1}{m} \sum y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})$ | Binary classification loss |
| 7 | Softmax Function | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class classification |
| 8 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | General classification metric |
| 9 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Spam detection, fault detection |
| 10 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Medical diagnosis, anomaly alerts |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Imbalanced IoT datasets |
| 12 | Specificity | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | False alarm reduction |
| 13 | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_{q,j} - x_{i,j})^2}$ | KNN distance metric |
| 14 | Gini Impurity | $G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | Decision tree split criterion |
| 15 | Information Gain (Entropy) | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Decision tree split criterion |
| 16 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta := \theta - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$ | Training any ML model |
| 17 | Discounted Return (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | RL reward aggregation |
| 18 | Bellman Equation | $V(s) = \max_a \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum P(s' \mid s,a) V(s') \right]$ | Optimal policy in MDP |
| 19 | RBF Kernel (SVM) | $K(x, x') = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\lVert x - x' \rVert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Non-linear classification |
| 20 | PCA Variance Ratio | $\text{Explained Var} = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$ | Feature dimensionality reduction |

## 2.5 Confusion Matrix — The Heart of Classification Evaluation

A **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ (binary) or $N \times N$ (multi-class) table summarizing the prediction outcomes:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

**IoT Use Case:** In a smart smoke detector, $TP$ = correctly raised alarm, $FN$ = missed fire (deadly!), $FP$ = false alarm, $TN$ = correctly identified no fire. **In safety-critical IoT, Recall is more important than Precision.**

## 2.6 Overfitting, Underfitting, and the Bias-Variance Tradeoff

```text
                    Model Complexity →
        Low ───────────────────────────────► High
        │                                    │
        │   ◄── Underfitting ──►             │
        │   (High Bias, Low Variance)        │
        │                                    │
        │              ◄── Sweet Spot ──►    │
        │                                    │
        │                  ◄── Overfitting ──►│
        │                  (Low Bias, High Variance)
        │                                    │
```

- **Underfitting:** Model is too simple → poor on both training and test data.
- **Overfitting:** Model memorizes training data → great on training, poor on test.
- **Regularization techniques:** L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), Dropout (for neural nets), Early Stopping.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

**Goal:** Find the optimal weights $\mathbf{w}$ and bias $b$ that minimize the Mean Squared Error loss:

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b - y_i \right)^2$$

**Why $\frac{1}{2}$?** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels out the exponent when differentiating, making the math cleaner. It does not affect the location of the minimum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the prediction for the $i$-th sample.

$$\hat{y}_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b$$

**Step 2:** Compute the prediction error (residual).

$$e_i = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$

**Step 3:** Write the cost function as a sum of squared residuals.

$$J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} e_i^2 = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to weight $w_j$.

We use the chain rule: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial w_j}$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = \frac{1}{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) = \frac{1}{m} e_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial w_j} = x_{i,j}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot x_{i,j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} e_i \cdot x_{i,j}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to bias $b$.

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial b} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} e_i$$

**Step 6:** Update the parameters using the gradient descent rule with learning rate $\alpha$.

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = w_j - \frac{\alpha}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) x_{i,j}$$

$$b := b - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = b - \frac{\alpha}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)$$

**Step 7:** Repeat Steps 1–6 for $N$ epochs (or until $\lVert \nabla J \rVert < \epsilon$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Criterion:** Gradient descent has converged when the gradient magnitude is below a small threshold $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-4}$) or when the change in cost $J$ between consecutive iterations is negligible.

## 3.2 Derivation: Logistic Regression and Cross-Entropy Loss

For binary classification, we want $\hat{y} \in [0, 1]$. The sigmoid function transforms the linear output into a probability:

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Why not use MSE for classification?** Using MSE with sigmoid causes the loss surface to become **non-convex**, leading to multiple local minima and poor convergence. Hence, we use **Cross-Entropy Loss**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss

**Step 1:** Write the likelihood of observing all training labels.

For independent samples:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{y}_i^{y_i} \cdot (1 - \hat{y}_i)^{(1 - y_i)}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood (easier to differentiate).

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \log L = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Negate to convert maximization into minimization (loss function).

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate the loss with respect to weight $w_j$ (using chain rule and derivative of sigmoid: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$).

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot x_{i,j}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **elegant symmetry**: The gradient formula for logistic regression is **identical** in form to that of linear regression — only $\hat{y}_i$ is computed differently (sigmoid vs. linear).

## 3.3 Derivation: Entropy and Information Gain (Decision Trees)

**Entropy** measures the impurity (disorder) in a dataset:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

Where $p_i$ is the proportion of samples belonging to class $i$.

**Step-by-step computation for a binary split:**

**Step 1:** Compute entropy of the parent node $S$ with $p$ positive and $(1-p)$ negative samples.

$$H(S) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)$$

**Step 2:** Split $S$ into $S_{\text{left}}$ and $S_{\text{right}}$ with sizes $N_L$ and $N_R$.

$$H(S_{\text{left}}) = -p_L \log_2 p_L - (1-p_L) \log_2 (1-p_L)$$

$$H(S_{\text{right}}) = -p_R \log_2 p_R - (1-p_R) \log_2 (1-p_R)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the weighted average entropy of children.

$$H_{\text{children}} = \frac{N_L}{N} H(S_{\text{left}}) + \frac{N_R}{N} H(S_{\text{right}})$$

**Step 4:** Information Gain is the reduction in entropy.

$$\text{IG} = H(S) - H_{\text{children}}$$

The split with the **highest IG** is chosen at each node of the decision tree.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Confusion Matrix Metrics

**Problem:** A smart agriculture IoT system predicts whether a crop field needs irrigation. Out of **200** test samples:

- 100 actually needed irrigation (Positive class)
- 100 did not need irrigation (Negative class)
- Model outputs: $TP = 90$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 15$, $TN = 85$

**Step 1: Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 85}{90 + 85 + 15 + 10} = \frac{175}{200} = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 2: Precision**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 15} = \frac{90}{105} \approx 0.857 = 85.7\%$$

**Step 3: Recall (Sensitivity)**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**Step 4: F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.857 \cdot 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} = \frac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878 = 87.8\%$$

**Step 5: Specificity**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 = 85\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The model has higher **Recall (90%)** than **Precision (85.7%)**, meaning it correctly catches most crops that need water, but at the cost of a few false alarms. For irrigation, this trade-off is **acceptable** (over-watering is safer than under-watering).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Loss Functions

| Property | MSE | MAE | Cross-Entropy | Hinge Loss (SVM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Use Case** | Regression | Regression | Classification | Classification (SVM) |
| **Penalizes Large Errors** | Heavily (squared) | Linearly | Exponentially | Linearly beyond margin |
| **Differentiability** | Everywhere | Not at 0 | Everywhere | Not at margin |
| **Robust to Outliers** | No (very sensitive) | Yes | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Convex?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Machine Learning. List and briefly explain its three main categories.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Learning is a branch of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance over time without being explicitly programmed. The three main categories are:

1. **Supervised Learning** — The model is trained on labelled data (input-output pairs). It learns a mapping function from input to output. Example: Email spam detection.
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — The model is trained on unlabelled data and discovers hidden patterns or groupings on its own. Example: Customer segmentation using K-Means.
3. **Reinforcement Learning** — An agent learns by interacting with an environment, receiving rewards or penalties, and updating its policy to maximize cumulative reward. Example: Self-driving car navigation.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the terms Overfitting and Underfitting with a suitable diagram. How can they be prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Overfitting** occurs when a model learns the training data *too well*, including its noise and outliers. It performs excellently on training data but poorly on unseen test data (high variance, low bias).
- **Underfitting** occurs when a model is *too simple* to capture the underlying pattern in the data. It performs poorly on both training and test data (high bias, low variance).

**Prevention Techniques:**
- **For Overfitting:** Use more training data, apply regularization (L1/L2), use Dropout (in neural networks), prune decision trees, or use cross-validation.
- **For Underfitting:** Use a more complex model, add more relevant features, reduce regularization strength, or train for more epochs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three types of Machine Learning in detail with suitable examples. Describe any **two** supervised learning algorithms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a binary classifier deployed in a smart healthcare IoT system (predicting cardiac arrhythmia from wearable ECG sensors), the confusion matrix on a test set of **500 patients** is given below. Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Specificity**. Also comment on which metric is most critical for this safety application.

| | Predicted Arrhythmia | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Arrhythmia** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | 30 | 270 |

---

### **Model Answer A:**

**(a) Explanation of Three Types of ML (7 marks):**

The three types of Machine Learning are:

**1. Supervised Learning:**
In supervised learning, the algorithm is trained on a **labelled dataset**, meaning each training example is paired with a known output (label). The model learns the input-output mapping and can then predict labels for new, unseen data. It is further divided into:
- **Classification:** Output is a discrete category (e.g., spam vs. not spam).
- **Regression:** Output is a continuous value (e.g., predicting temperature).

*Two algorithms (any two of the following):*

- **Linear Regression:** Models the relationship between input and a continuous output as a linear equation $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$. Trained by minimizing MSE using gradient descent. **IoT Example:** Predicting the next-hour power consumption of a smart building.

- **Logistic Regression:** Despite its name, it is a classification algorithm. It uses the sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ to output a probability, thresholded at 0.5 for binary classification. **IoT Example:** Predicting whether a motor will fail in the next 24 hours.

- **Decision Tree:** Recursively partitions the feature space using the feature that maximizes information gain (or minimizes Gini impurity). **IoT Example:** Diagnosing the root cause of a sensor network fault.

- **KNN:** Classifies a new sample by majority vote of its K nearest neighbors in feature space (Euclidean distance). **IoT Example:** Identifying the type of device from its signal fingerprint.

- **SVM:** Finds the optimal separating hyperplane that maximizes the margin between classes. **IoT Example:** Classifying human activities (walking, running) from accelerometer data.

**2. Unsupervised Learning:**
The model is trained on **unlabelled data** and must discover hidden structure, groupings, or patterns on its own. Common tasks include clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN) and dimensionality reduction (PCA). **IoT Example:** Grouping similar smart-home devices based on their energy usage patterns.

**3. Reinforcement Learning:**
An **agent** learns by interacting with an **environment**. For each action, it receives a **reward** (positive or negative) and updates its **policy** to maximize cumulative long-term reward. Modeled as a Markov Decision Process. **IoT Example:** An autonomous HVAC system learning to minimize energy cost by adjusting temperature setpoints.

**(b) Confusion Matrix Metrics Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $TP = 180$, $FN = 20$, $FP = 30$, $TN = 270$, Total $N = 500$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 = \mathbf{90\%}$$

**Step 2 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.8571 = \mathbf{85.71\%}$$

**Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity):**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = \mathbf{90\%}$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8571 \cdot 0.90}{0.8571 + 0.90} = \frac{1.5428}{1.7571} \approx 0.878 = \mathbf{87.8\%}$$

**Step 5 — Specificity:**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90 = \mathbf{90\%}$$

**Critical Metric Discussion:**

In a smart cardiac arrhythmia detection system, the cost of a **False Negative (FN)** — failing to detect a real arrhythmia — is **life-threatening**. A patient with undiagnosed arrhythmia could suffer cardiac arrest without medical intervention. Therefore, **Recall (Sensitivity) is the MOST CRITICAL metric** for this application. The model achieves 90% Recall, which is reasonable, but in clinical deployment, a recall above 95% is typically mandated. If necessary, we can lower the classification threshold to boost recall at the cost of slightly reducing precision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end **Machine Learning workflow** as applied to an IoT-based predictive maintenance system in a smart factory.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Linear Regression algorithm** in detail. Derive the gradient descent update rule for a single weight $w_j$ and bias $b$ using the MSE loss function.

---

### **Model Answer B:**

**(a) ML Workflow for IoT Predictive Maintenance (7 marks):**

A predictive maintenance system in a smart factory uses ML to predict equipment failure *before* it happens, reducing downtime and cost. The workflow is:

**Step 1 — Data Acquisition:** Vibration, temperature, acoustic, and current sensors mounted on motors, bearings, and pumps continuously stream telemetry to a gateway.

**Step 2 — Data Preprocessing:** Raw signals are cleaned (noise removal), missing values are imputed (mean/forward fill), and outliers are clipped or removed. Signals are normalized to a common scale (e.g., Min-Max or Z-score).

**Step 3 — Feature Engineering:** Time-domain features (mean, RMS, kurtosis, crest factor) and frequency-domain features (FFT peaks) are extracted. A rolling window computes features over the last 5 minutes.

**Step 4 — Data Splitting:** The labelled dataset (with "Normal" and "Faulty" tags) is split into 80% training and 20% testing sets.

**Step 5 — Model Selection & Training:** A Random Forest classifier is trained on the training set using cross-validation. Hyperparameters (number of trees, max depth) are tuned using grid search.

**Step 6 — Model Evaluation:** The model is evaluated on the test set using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. A confusion matrix is generated.

**Step 7 — Deployment:** The trained model is deployed on a fog/edge gateway. Live sensor data is fed to the model, and predictions are sent to the maintenance dashboard.

**Step 8 — Monitoring & Retraining:** Model performance is continuously monitored. When model drift (degradation in accuracy) is detected, the model is retrained with newly collected labelled data.

**(b) Linear Regression & Gradient Descent Derivation (7 marks):**

**Concept:** Linear regression models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $\mathbf{x}$ as a linear function:

$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b$$

**Objective:** Find $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ that minimize the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss over $m$ training examples:

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)^2$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Compute the prediction for sample $i$.

$$\hat{y}_i = w_1 x_{i,1} + w_2 x_{i,2} + \dots + w_n x_{i,n} + b$$

**Step 2:** Compute the residual (error).

$$e_i = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$

**Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $w_j$ using the chain rule.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2(\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot x_{i,j}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $b$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)$$

**Step 5:** Update rules using gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha$:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot x_{i,j}$$

$$b := b - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)$$

**Step 6:** These updates are repeated for every weight over many epochs until $J$ converges to a minimum.

> [!TIP]
> **Convergence Note:** A well-tuned learning rate is critical. If $\alpha$ is too large, the algorithm diverges. If $\alpha$ is too small, convergence is painfully slow. Advanced optimizers like **Adam** and **RMSProp** adapt $\alpha$ automatically.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Read Carefully

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1: Forgetting the Bias Term.** Many students write $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ and forget the bias $b$. This loses **1 mark** in derivations. Always state both $w$ and $b$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2: Confusing MSE with RMSE.** They are different by a square root. RMSE has the same unit as the target variable, while MSE is in squared units. Examiners specifically check this.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3: Writing "SVM uses Neural Networks" or "KNN is a parametric model."** These are exam killers. Remember: **KNN is non-parametric and instance-based; SVM is a discriminative classifier; Logistic Regression is a classifier (not regression).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4: Forgetting IoT Context.** For an IoT course, every ML answer **must** reference an IoT application (smart home, wearables, smart factory, smart city, etc.). Generic AI answers without IoT context lose **20–30% of marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Machine Learning for KTU 2024 PCADT602**

- **Definition:** ML is learning from data without explicit programming.
- **Three Types:** Supervised (labelled), Unsupervised (unlabelled), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- **Supervised Subtypes:** Regression (continuous output) and Classification (discrete output).
- **Linear Regression Equation:** $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$.
- **Sigmoid Function:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, used in logistic regression.
- **MSE Loss:** $\frac{1}{m}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss:** $-[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$.
- **Gradient Descent:** $\theta := \theta - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$.
- **Confusion Matrix:** Rows = Actual, Columns = Predicted. **MEMORIZE THIS ORDER.**
- **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{\text{Total}}$; **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$; **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **F1-Score** = Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **KNN** is lazy, non-parametric, instance-based. Uses Euclidean distance.
- **SVM** finds max-margin hyperplane. Uses kernels for non-linear data.
- **K-Means** objective: minimize within-cluster sum of squares.
- **PCA:** Projects data onto eigenvectors of covariance matrix.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Agent, Environment, Reward, Policy, MDP.
- **Bellman Equation:** $V(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum P(s'|s,a)V(s')]$.
- **Overfitting** → High variance, low bias. **Underfitting** → High bias, low variance.
- **Regularization:** L1 (Lasso) + L2 (Ridge) + Dropout.
- **TinyML** = ML on microcontrollers (critical for IoT edge deployment).
- **Always include an IoT example** in every answer — it is a KTU board requirement.
- **Confusion matrix rows** = **ACTUAL**, **columns** = **PREDICTED** (the most common silly mistake).
- **Learning rate** $\alpha$ controls gradient descent step size.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Analytics Tools and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Big Data Analytics Tools and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Big Data Analytics

**Big Data Analytics** refers to the systematic process of examining, cleaning, transforming, and modeling large, complex datasets (typically terabytes to petabytes in scale) to uncover hidden patterns, unknown correlations, market trends, customer preferences, and other useful business intelligence. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, it represents the computational backbone that processes the massive, continuous, and heterogeneous data streams generated by billions of interconnected sensors, actuators, and smart devices.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, Big Data is characterized by a set of properties that distinguish it from traditional data processing paradigms. The standard **"5 V's" model** is the foundational framework:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 V's of Big Data (KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition)**
> 1. **Volume** — The sheer scale of data (terabytes, petabytes, exabytes).
> 2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is generated, transmitted, and processed (real-time streams).
> 3. **Variety** — The different types of data (structured, semi-structured, unstructured).
> 4. **Veracity** — The trustworthiness, quality, and accuracy of data.
> 5. **Value** — The meaningful insight extracted from raw data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Water Reservoir" Model

Imagine a futuristic smart city where thousands of water sensors are installed across every pipeline, drainage, and household tap. Every second, each sensor reports:
- Pressure levels,
- Water quality metrics,
- Flow rates, and
- Consumption patterns.

This is **velocity** (continuous flow), **volume** (millions of readings daily), **variety** (numeric, text, image, video), **veracity** (some sensors may be faulty), and **value** (leakage detection, demand forecasting).

**Big Data Analytics Tools** act as the **central water treatment and distribution plant**:
- **Hadoop HDFS** = the massive underground storage tanks (raw reservoir).
- **MapReduce / Spark** = the filtration and processing units.
- **Hive / Pig** = the quality control inspectors.
- **NoSQL databases** = the distribution channels that route purified insights to control rooms.
- **Apache Kafka** = the pipeline network carrying raw water from sensors to the plant.

Without this "plant," the city would drown in raw, unprocessed water (data). The plant *refines* it into *actionable intelligence*.

## 1.3 Why Big Data Analytics is Indispensable for IoT

IoT ecosystems inherently produce **non-relational, high-frequency, geographically distributed, and time-series** data. Traditional RDBMS systems fail to handle such workloads due to:
- Vertical scaling limits,
- Rigid schema requirements,
- Inability to process unstructured data efficiently.

Hence, **distributed computing frameworks** are mandated by KTU 2024 as the de facto IoT data layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes that IoT is not merely a sensor-deployment problem; it is fundamentally a **data engineering problem** at scale. Tools like Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases form the analytical heart of any production-grade IoT deployment.

## 1.4 Classification of Big Data Analytics Tools

Big Data tools can be classified into four primary categories:

| Category | Primary Function | Representative Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Layer** | Distributed, fault-tolerant data storage | HDFS, HBase, Amazon S3, MinIO |
| **Processing Layer** | Batch and stream computation | Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark, Apache Flink |
| **Querying / Analytics Layer** | SQL-like and ad-hoc queries | Apache Hive, Apache Pig, Presto, Impala |
| **Ingestion / Messaging Layer** | Real-time data streaming | Apache Kafka, Apache Flume, RabbitMQ, MQTT Brokers |
| **NoSQL Databases** | Schema-less, scalable data models | MongoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB, Redis |
| **Visualization Layer** | Dashboards and reporting | Kibana, Grafana, Tableau, Power BI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Metrics used in KTU Questions:** A **Petabyte (PB) = 1024 TB**, a **Terabyte (TB) = 1024 GB**. Replication factor is denoted **r**, default **r = 3** in HDFS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hadoop Ecosystem — Theoretical Foundations

**Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers**. It is built on two core pillars:

### 2.1.1 Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)

HDFS is a **master-slave, write-once-read-many (WORM)** distributed file system optimized for high-throughput access to large files. Its architecture comprises:

- **NameNode (Master)**: Maintains the file system namespace, metadata, block-to-DataNode mapping. Runs in RAM for fast access.
- **DataNode (Slave)**: Stores the actual data blocks. Default block size is **128 MB** in HDFS (Hadoop 2.x and later).
- **Secondary NameNode**: Performs periodic checkpointing of NameNode metadata (does NOT act as a backup in real-time).
- **Replication Factor (r)**: Each block is replicated across **r** different DataNodes (default **r = 3**) to ensure fault tolerance.

**Operational Logic of HDFS Write Operation:**
1. Client contacts the NameNode to request a file write.
2. NameNode responds with a list of DataNodes forming a **replication pipeline**.
3. Client streams the data block-by-block to the first DataNode, which forwards to the next, and so on.
4. Upon successful receipt, an acknowledgment is sent back through the pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Size = 128 MB?** Smaller blocks increase metadata overhead (NameNode RAM consumption). Larger blocks reduce seek time and improve sequential read throughput — critical for batch analytics workloads.

### 2.1.2 MapReduce Programming Paradigm

MapReduce is a **functional programming model** designed for parallel processing of massive datasets. It operates in two primary phases:

- **Map Phase**: Input is split into independent chunks. A `map()` function processes each key-value pair and emits intermediate key-value pairs.
- **Reduce Phase**: Intermediate pairs are grouped by key. A `reduce()` function aggregates values for each unique key.

**Supporting infrastructure components:**
- **JobTracker / ResourceManager**: Schedules jobs and manages cluster resources.
- **TaskTracker / NodeManager**: Executes individual map and reduce tasks on each worker node.
- **Shuffle and Sort Phase**: Automatically performed by the framework between Map and Reduce.

### 2.1.3 Apache Spark — The In-Memory Successor

**Spark** extends the MapReduce model by performing computations **in-memory (RAM)** rather than on disk, achieving up to **100× faster** performance for iterative algorithms (e.g., Machine Learning, Graph processing). Its core abstraction is the **Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD)** — an immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel.

### 2.1.4 NoSQL Databases

NoSQL ("Not Only SQL") databases depart from the rigid tabular model of RDBMS to support **horizontal scalability, schema flexibility, and high availability**. Four primary data models exist:

| NoSQL Type | Data Model | Example | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key-Value Store** | Hash-map style | Redis, DynamoDB | Session caching, device state |
| **Document Store** | JSON / BSON | MongoDB, CouchDB | Sensor metadata storage |
| **Column-Family** | Wide-column tables | Cassandra, HBase | Time-series IoT telemetry |
| **Graph Database** | Nodes and edges | Neo4j | Social IoT relationship mapping |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential mathematical relationships used in Big Data storage and processing questions.

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total HDFS Storage Required | $S_{total} = B \times N \times r$ | Storage = Block size × Number of blocks × Replication | Bytes |
| Number of Blocks per File | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \right\rceil$ | Ceiling of File size divided by Block size | Dimensionless |
| Effective Storage per DataNode | $S_{node} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{D_{active}}$ | Total storage divided by active DataNodes | Bytes |
| Data Locality Speedup | $S_{locality} = \dfrac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}}$ | Ratio of remote read time to local read time | Dimensionless |
| MapReduce Time Complexity | $T_{job} = T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$ | Total job time = Map + Shuffle + Reduce phases | Seconds |
| Amdahl's Law (Parallel Speedup) | $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | Speedup with p processors, f = parallel fraction | Dimensionless |
| Storage Tier Conversion | $1 \text{ PB} = 1024^5 \text{ Bytes}$ | Petabyte to Byte conversion | Bytes |
| Throughput Metric | $\Theta = \dfrac{D_{processed}}{T_{elapsed}}$ | Data processed per unit time | MB/s or GB/s |
| Map Output Compression Ratio | $C_{r} = 1 - \dfrac{S_{compressed}}{S_{original}}$ | Storage saved via compression | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Convention Reminder:** In all handwritten or written exams, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values to avoid markdown table breaking — this aligns with the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

In production-grade IoT deployments, these tools and formulas drive mission-critical systems:

- **Smart Grid Monitoring**: HDFS stores terabytes of smart-meter readings; Spark performs real-time theft detection.
- **Predictive Maintenance**: Manufacturing IoT sensors feed Kafka streams into Spark MLlib for failure prediction.
- **Connected Vehicles**: Cassandra stores high-velocity telemetry from millions of vehicles, supporting low-latency queries.
- **Healthcare IoT**: HBase stores patient vitals with time-series access patterns at petabyte scale.

The economic impact is significant: industries leveraging Big Data analytics in IoT report **20–30% reduction in operational costs** and **15–25% improvement in asset utilization** (industry standard metrics, **bold** for emphasis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: HDFS Storage Requirement Calculation

> **Problem:** A smart factory generates a single log file of size **F = 850 MB** per hour. The HDFS cluster uses a block size **B = 128 MB** and a replication factor **r = 3**. Calculate:
> (a) The number of blocks required for one such file.
> (b) The total physical storage consumed on the cluster.
> (c) The number of blocks if 24 such files are generated per day.

### Solution

**Part (a): Number of Blocks**

We apply the ceiling function formula:

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \right\rceil$$

Substituting the given values:

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{850 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

$$N = \left\lceil 6.640625 \right\rceil$$

$$N = 7 \text{ blocks}$$

*Explanation:* The ceiling function rounds up because a partial block still occupies a full physical block on the DataNode. The last block will contain only **850 − (6 × 128) = 850 − 768 = 82 MB** of actual data, but the system reserves the full 128 MB slot.

**Part (b): Total Physical Storage**

Apply the HDFS storage formula:

$$S_{total} = B \times N \times r$$

Substituting:

$$S_{total} = 128 \text{ MB} \times 7 \times 3$$

$$S_{total} = 2688 \text{ MB}$$

$$S_{total} = 2.625 \text{ GB}$$

*Explanation:* Each of the 7 logical blocks is physically stored on 3 separate DataNodes to ensure fault tolerance. Thus, 1 MB of logical data consumes 3 MB of physical storage.

**Part (c): Blocks for 24 Files per Day**

First, compute the total daily logical data volume:

$$F_{daily} = 850 \text{ MB} \times 24$$

$$F_{daily} = 20400 \text{ MB}$$

Then compute the total number of blocks per day:

$$N_{daily} = \left\lceil \dfrac{F_{daily}}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{20400}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 159.375 \right\rceil$$

$$N_{daily} = 160 \text{ blocks per day}$$

*Final Insight:* Over a year, this IoT system would generate approximately **160 × 365 = 58,400 blocks**, consuming roughly **22.4 GB** of physical storage — manageable on a small cluster.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law Applied to MapReduce Cluster Scaling

> **Problem:** A MapReduce job has a parallelizable fraction **f = 0.90** (i.e., 90% of the code can be parallelized). Calculate the speedup achieved when scaling from **p = 1** processor to **p = 8** processors.

### Solution

**General Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$$

**Step 1: Sequential baseline (p = 1)**

$$S(1) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{1}}$$

$$S(1) = \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.90} = \dfrac{1}{1.00} = 1.0$$

*Explanation:* With one processor, the speedup is trivially 1× (baseline).

**Step 2: Parallel execution (p = 8)**

$$S(8) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{8}}$$

$$S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125}$$

$$S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.2125}$$

$$S(8) \approx 4.706$$

*Explanation:* Despite 8× more processors, the maximum achievable speedup is only **4.706×** because the sequential 10% portion becomes the bottleneck. This is the **fundamental limitation** of distributed computing.

**Step 3: Asymptotic limit (p → ∞)**

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

*Engineering Insight:* Even with infinite processors, the job can never run faster than **10×** the sequential time. This is why Amdahl's Law is the **"speedup ceiling"** for Hadoop/Spark workloads.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Data Locality Speedup in HDFS

> **Problem:** A Spark task requires reading **D = 512 MB** of data. The local read throughput is **R_local = 100 MB/s**, while the remote read (over network) is **R_remote = 25 MB/s**. Compute the data locality speedup ratio.

### Solution

**Step 1: Time to read locally**

$$T_{local} = \dfrac{D}{R_{local}} = \dfrac{512}{100} = 5.12 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Time to read remotely**

$$T_{remote} = \dfrac{D}{R_{remote}} = \dfrac{512}{25} = 20.48 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the speedup ratio**

$$S_{locality} = \dfrac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}} = \dfrac{20.48}{5.12} = 4.0$$

*Interpretation:* Local reads are **4× faster** than remote reads. This is why Hadoop and Spark employ **data locality scheduling** — they attempt to place computation on the same node where the data resides, minimizing network I/O.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hadoop vs Spark vs NoSQL Trade-offs

| Parameter | Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark | NoSQL (Cassandra / HBase) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Processing Paradigm | Batch (Disk-based) | In-memory Batch + Stream | Real-time key-value lookup |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds | Sub-millisecond |
| Best For | Log archival, ETL | ML, iterative, stream | High-write throughput IoT |
| Fault Tolerance | HDFS replication (r = 3) | RDD lineage + checkpoints | Tunable consistency |
| Cost Efficiency | Low (commodity HW) | High (RAM expensive) | Moderate |
| KTU Question Weight | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hadoop Ecosystem — High-Level Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete Hadoop ecosystem** and the interactions between its core components. It uses safe alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `nodeA1`, `nodeB1`) and clean uppercase labels without any markdown formatting tags.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA1[IoT SENSORS AND DEVICES] --> nodeB1[APACHE KAFKA INGESTION LAYER]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[APACHE FLUME LOG AGGREGATOR]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2[APACHE SQOOP RDBMS BRIDGE]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[HDFS NAME NODE MASTER]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[HDFS DATA NODE 1]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD3[HDFS DATA NODE 2]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD4[HDFS DATA NODE 3]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE1[APACHE MAPREDUCE BATCH ENGINE]
    nodeD3 --> nodeE1
    nodeD4 --> nodeE1
    nodeD2 --> nodeE2[APACHE SPARK IN MEMORY ENGINE]
    nodeD3 --> nodeE2
    nodeD4 --> nodeE2
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[APACHE HIVE SQL WAREHOUSE]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF2[APACHE PIG SCRIPTING LAYER]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF3[SPARK MLlib MACHINE LEARNING]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF4[SPARK STREAMING REAL TIME]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG1[HBASE COLUMNAR NOSQL]
    nodeF3 --> nodeG1
    nodeF4 --> nodeG2[GRAFANA VISUALIZATION DASHBOARD]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG2
    nodeG1 --> nodeG3[OZZIE JOB SCHEDULER]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG4[END USER ANALYTICS DASHBOARD]
    nodeG3 --> nodeG4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Data flows from **IoT Sensors (nodeA1)** at the top, through ingestion (Kafka, Flume), into storage (HDFS cluster), processed by computation engines (MapReduce / Spark), queried via SQL layers (Hive, Pig), stored in NoSQL (HBase), and finally presented to end users through Grafana dashboards (nodeG4).

---

## 4.2 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture

The following diagram details the **internal architecture of HDFS**, showing NameNode, Secondary NameNode, and multiple DataNodes with replication pipelines.

```mermaid
graph TB
    client1[IoT APPLICATION CLIENT] --> nn1[NAMENODE MASTER]
    nn1 --> snn1[SECONDARY NAMENODE CHECKPOINT]
    nn1 --> dn1[DATA NODE 1 RACK A]
    nn1 --> dn2[DATA NODE 2 RACK B]
    nn1 --> dn3[DATA NODE 3 RACK C]
    dn1 -.REPLICATES.-> dn2
    dn2 -.REPLICATES.-> dn3
    dn3 -.REPLICATES.-> dn1
    dn1 --> block1[BLOCK 128MB]
    dn1 --> block2[BLOCK 128MB]
    dn2 --> block3[BLOCK 128MB]
    dn2 --> block4[BLOCK 128MB]
    dn3 --> block5[BLOCK 128MB]
    dn3 --> block6[BLOCK 128MB]
    block1 -.REPLICATION r EQUALS 3.-> block3
    block2 -.REPLICATION r EQUALS 3.-> block4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication Placement Strategy:** HDFS places the first replica on the local node, the second on a different rack, and the third on the same rack as the second but a different node. This balances **reliability** (rack failure tolerance) with **write performance** (intra-rack network speed).

---

## 4.3 MapReduce Job Execution Flow

The following diagram shows the **complete MapReduce workflow** from input to output, highlighting the Map, Shuffle, Sort, and Reduce phases.

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1[INPUT SPLIT 1] --> mapper1[MAP TASK 1]
    input2[INPUT SPLIT 2] --> mapper2[MAP TASK 2]
    input3[INPUT SPLIT 3] --> mapper3[MAP TASK 3]
    mapper1 --> kvm1[KEY VALUE PAIRS]
    mapper2 --> kvm2[KEY VALUE PAIRS]
    mapper3 --> kvm3[KEY VALUE PAIRS]
    kvm1 --> shuffle1[SHUFFLE AND SORT PHASE]
    kvm2 --> shuffle1
    kvm3 --> shuffle1
    shuffle1 --> grouped1[GROUPED BY KEY]
    grouped1 --> reducer1[REDUCE TASK 1]
    grouped1 --> reducer2[REDUCE TASK 2]
    reducer1 --> output1[OUTPUT FILE 1]
    reducer2 --> output2[OUTPUT FILE 2]
    output1 --> hdfs1[HDFS PERSISTENT STORAGE]
    output2 --> hdfs1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Shuffle and Sort** phase is the most network-intensive step in MapReduce. It involves transferring intermediate data from mappers to reducers, followed by a sort by key. Spark optimizes this by keeping intermediate data in memory.

---

## 4.4 IoT Big Data Analytics Reference Pipeline

This is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how an IoT application's data travels through the full Big Data stack.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stage1[STAGE 1 DEVICE LAYER] --> stage2[STAGE 2 EDGE GATEWAY]
    stage2 --> stage3[STAGE 3 INGESTION LAYER KAFKA]
    stage3 --> stage4[STAGE 4 STORAGE LAYER HDFS HBASE]
    stage4 --> stage5[STAGE 5 PROCESSING LAYER SPARK MAPREDUCE]
    stage5 --> stage6[STAGE 6 ANALYTICS LAYER HIVE MLLIB]
    stage6 --> stage7[STAGE 7 VISUALIZATION LAYER GRAFANA KIBANA]
    stage7 --> stage8[STAGE 8 ACTIONABLE INSIGHTS DASHBOARDS]
    stage8 -.FEEDBACK.-> stage1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closing the Loop:** The feedback arrow from Stage 8 back to Stage 1 represents **closed-loop automation** — a defining characteristic of mature IoT systems where analytics outputs drive control decisions back at the device layer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Big Data and explain the 5 V's. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be processed by traditional data management tools. It is characterized by the **5 V's**:

1. **Volume**: Massive scale of data (ranging from terabytes to exabytes). For example, social media platforms generate petabytes of user data daily.
2. **Velocity**: The high speed at which data is generated and must be processed. IoT sensors produce real-time streams that demand millisecond-level processing.
3. **Variety**: Diverse data formats including structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, video, audio).
4. **Veracity**: The trustworthiness and quality of data, addressing noise, bias, and inconsistency in raw sensor readings.
5. **Value**: The meaningful business intelligence derived from processing raw data — the ultimate goal of analytics.

> **Valuation Note:** Examiners award full marks only when *all five* V's are listed with one-line definitions. Omitting Veracity is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2: Compare SQL and NoSQL databases with suitable examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Fixed schema, tabular | Schema-less, flexible |
| Scaling | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| Consistency | Strong ACID guarantees | Tunable consistency (CAP theorem) |
| Best For | Structured, transactional data | Unstructured, high-velocity IoT data |
| Examples | MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle | MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase, Redis |

**Conclusion:** NoSQL databases are preferred for IoT workloads due to their ability to handle high write throughput, schema flexibility, and horizontal scalability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (a): Explain the HDFS Architecture in Detail `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part A(a)(i) — Describe the components and write-read process of HDFS. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS Components:**

1. **NameNode (Master Node):**
   - Stores file system metadata: filenames, permissions, block locations.
   - Maintains in-memory mapping of files → blocks → DataNodes.
   - Single point of coordination (does not store actual data).

2. **DataNode (Slave Node):**
   - Stores the actual data blocks (default 128 MB each).
   - Periodically sends heartbeats to NameNode (default every 3 seconds).
   - Executes read/write requests from clients.

3. **Secondary NameNode:**
   - Performs checkpointing of NameNode metadata (fsimage and edit logs).
   - Reduces NameNode startup time after a crash.
   - **Important:** It is NOT a hot standby; failover is not automatic.

**HDFS Write Process (Step-by-step):**

1. Client initiates a write request to NameNode with file metadata.
2. NameNode checks for file existence and permissions, then allocates a new block ID.
3. NameNode returns a list of DataNodes forming a **replication pipeline** (3 nodes by default).
4. Client splits the file into packets and streams them to the first DataNode.
5. Each DataNode forwards packets to the next in the pipeline, creating a chain.
6. After successful write, acknowledgments propagate back to the client.
7. Client requests the next block; the process repeats until the entire file is written.

**HDFS Read Process:**

1. Client requests to read a file from NameNode.
2. NameNode returns the list of DataNodes containing each block.
3. Client connects to the nearest DataNode (data locality optimization).
4. Data is streamed block-by-block until the file is fully read.

---

#### Part A(a)(ii) — Discuss HDFS fault tolerance and rack awareness. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Fault Tolerance Mechanisms:**

1. **Block Replication:** Each block is replicated **r = 3** times across different DataNodes, ensuring data is recoverable even if two nodes fail simultaneously.
2. **Heartbeat Protocol:** DataNodes send heartbeats every 3 seconds. If a NameNode misses 10 consecutive heartbeats (30 seconds), the node is declared dead.
3. **Replica Re-creation:** Upon DataNode failure, the NameNode instructs surviving DataNodes to replicate the under-replicated blocks to restore the replication factor.
4. **Checksum Verification:** Each block has an associated checksum to detect corruption. Corrupted blocks are re-replicated from healthy replicas.

**Rack Awareness:**

HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement policy** to balance reliability and network bandwidth:

- **Replica 1**: Placed on the **local node** (where the client writes).
- **Replica 2**: Placed on a **different rack** (rack failure tolerance).
- **Replica 3**: Placed on the **same rack as Replica 2**, but on a **different node** (preserves intra-rack network efficiency).

> **Engineering Benefit:** Rack awareness ensures that the loss of an entire rack (a common failure mode in data centers) does not result in data loss, while keeping two-thirds of replicas within the same rack for high write throughput.

---

### Question B — Option (b): Explain the MapReduce Programming Model with a Suitable Example `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part B(b)(i) — Describe the phases of MapReduce and its execution flow. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**MapReduce is a distributed programming model** that processes massive datasets in parallel by breaking the work into a **Map phase** and a **Reduce phase**, with an intermediate **Shuffle and Sort** step.

**Phases of MapReduce:**

1. **Input Split Phase:**
   - The input file is divided into fixed-size splits (typically 64 MB or 128 MB, matching HDFS blocks).
   - Each split is assigned to one Map task.

2. **Map Phase:**
   - Each Map task invokes the user-defined `map(key, value)` function.
   - The function processes the input and emits **intermediate key-value pairs**.
   - Example: `map(line, text)` → `emit(word, 1)` for each word in a text file.

3. **Shuffle and Sort Phase (Automatic):**
   - Intermediate pairs from all Mappers are **partitioned by key** and transferred to Reducers.
   - Within each partition, pairs are **sorted by key** to group identical keys together.
   - This is the most network-intensive phase.

4. **Reduce Phase:**
   - Each Reducer invokes the user-defined `reduce(key, list_of_values)` function.
   - The function aggregates the values for each key and emits the final output.
   - Example: `reduce(word, [1,1,1,1])` → `emit(word, 4)`.

5. **Output Phase:**
   - Reducer outputs are written back to HDFS as the final result.

---

#### Part B(b)(ii) — Solve the following problem using MapReduce and explain. (7 Marks)

**Problem:** Given a text file containing IoT sensor log entries, count the frequency of each distinct error code.

**Sample Input:**
```
ERR101 TEMP HIGH
ERR205 VOLTAGE LOW
ERR101 TEMP HIGH
ERR101 CONNECTION LOST
ERR205 VOLTAGE LOW
```

**Expected Output:**
```
ERR101    3
ERR205    2
```

**MapReduce Solution:**

**Step 1 — Map Function Logic:**

The mapper reads each line, extracts the error code (first token), and emits `(error_code, 1)`.

**Pseudo-code representation (No actual Python — KTU 2024 protocol):**
- Input: `(line_offset, line_text)`
- Action: Parse the first word as the error code.
- Output: Emit `(error_code, 1)` for each occurrence.

**Step 2 — Shuffle and Sort (Framework-Handled):**
All pairs with the same key are grouped together:
- `ERR101 → [1, 1, 1]`
- `ERR205 → [1, 1]`

**Step 3 — Reduce Function Logic:**

The reducer sums the list of values for each key.

**Pseudo-code representation:**
- Input: `(error_code, [1, 1, 1, ...])`
- Action: Sum all values in the list.
- Output: Emit `(error_code, total_count)`.

**Final Output:**
```
ERR101    3
ERR205    2
```

**Execution Flow (Parallelism Benefit):**
- If the file is 1 GB, it is split into ~8 blocks (at 128 MB each).
- 8 Map tasks run in parallel on 8 different DataNodes.
- 2 Reducer tasks aggregate the results.
- **Total time = T_map + T_shuffle + T_reduce**, as per the formula in SECTION 2.2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls in Big Data Analytics Questions:**
> 1. **Omitting formulas in storage calculations** — Always state the formula *before* substituting values. Marks are awarded for the formula, not just the answer.
> 2. **Confusing HDFS NameNode with DataNode roles** — NameNode = metadata; DataNode = actual data. Reversing these loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting replication factor in storage** — Total physical storage = logical data × replication factor. Students commonly forget this multiplier.
> 4. **Writing MapReduce solutions without explaining the Shuffle phase** — Always describe the auto-handled Shuffle and Sort between Map and Reduce.
> 5. **Confusing HBase with Hive** — HBase = NoSQL columnar store (real-time); Hive = SQL-on-Hadoop (batch query). Examiners are strict on this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Big Data = 5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value. **Memorize all five.**
- **HDFS block size = 128 MB**, replication factor **r = 3** (defaults). These appear in nearly every storage calculation question.
- **Storage formula**: $S_{total} = B \times N \times r$. Always use the ceiling function for $N$.
- **MapReduce phases**: Input Split → Map → Shuffle and Sort → Reduce → Output. Shuffle is automatic.
- **Spark > MapReduce** for iterative ML workloads due to in-memory computation. Mention this when asked about modern alternatives.
- **NoSQL types**: Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra, HBase), Graph (Neo4j). Map each to a suitable IoT use case.
- **Amdahl's Law** ceiling: $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f}$. Even infinite processors cannot exceed this limit.
- **Rack Awareness**: Replica placement = 1 local + 1 different rack + 1 same rack different node. Essential for fault tolerance.
- **IoT-Big Data Pipeline**: Sensors → Kafka/Flume → HDFS/HBase → Spark/MapReduce → Hive/MLlib → Grafana → Insights.
- **Data Locality** is the #1 performance optimization in Hadoop/Spark. Local reads are typically **4× faster** than remote reads.
- **KTU 2024 trend**: Questions increasingly focus on *comparisons* (Hadoop vs Spark, SQL vs NoSQL) and *numerical storage calculations* rather than pure definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arduino** is an open-source electronics prototyping platform based on **flexible, easy-to-use hardware and software**. It consists of a microcontroller board (Programmable Circuit Board / Single-Board Microcontroller) and a development environment (Arduino IDE) used to write, compile, and upload code to the physical board. The board is built around an **8-bit AVR RISC-based microcontroller** (typically the ATmega328P for the Uno) and is designed to make embedded systems, sensor interfacing, and IoT prototyping accessible to students, hobbyists, and engineers.

For KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations, the formal definition should reference:
- **Microcontroller:** ATmega328P (8-bit, RISC architecture)
- **Clock Speed:** **16 MHz** (quartz crystal)
- **Operating Voltage:** **5 V DC**
- **Recommended Input Voltage:** **7 V – 12 V**
- **Flash Memory:** **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader)
- **SRAM:** **2 KB**
- **EEPROM:** **1 KB**
- **Digital I/O Pins:** **14** (of which **6** provide PWM output)
- **Analog Input Pins:** **6** (10-bit ADC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4 of PCADT602, Arduino is treated as the *edge-device prototyping layer* of IoT. The student must be able to describe the architecture, pin functions, ADC/PWM behavior, and sketch (program) structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the human body:
- The **brain** is the microcontroller (ATmega328P) — it makes decisions.
- The **senses** (eyes, ears, skin) are the **sensors** connected to analog/digital pins.
- The **muscles and voice** are the **actuators** (LEDs, motors, buzzers) controlled by the digital/PWM pins.
- The **nervous system** is the **PCB tracks and buses** that carry signals.
- The **spine / USB cable** connects the brain to the outside world (computer / network).

**Arduino is the "digital brain"** of an IoT node. It reads real-world analog/digital signals, processes them using the uploaded sketch, and produces physical outputs. Unlike a Raspberry Pi (which is a full Linux computer), Arduino runs a single loop continuously — making it *deterministic, low-power, and ideal for real-time sensor tasks*.

## 1.3 Family of Arduino Boards

| Board | MCU | Clock | Flash | Digital I/O | Analog In | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uno R3** | ATmega328P | 16 MHz | 32 KB | 14 | 6 | Most popular, teaching default |
| **Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 16 MHz | 256 KB | 54 | 16 | Large projects, many peripherals |
| **Nano** | ATmega328P | 16 MHz | 32 KB | 14 | 8 | Breadboard-friendly |
| **Leonardo** | ATmega32U4 | 16 MHz | 32 KB | 20 | 12 | Native USB HID |
| **Due** | SAM3X8E (ARM) | 84 MHz | 512 KB | 54 | 12 | High-performance 32-bit |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 theory questions, the **Arduino Uno R3** is the default reference board unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arduino Uno R3 – Hardware Block Architecture

The board is internally organized as a **System-on-Chip (SoC) prototyping** setup. The functional blocks are:

1. **Power Supply Section**
   - **DC Barrel Jack** (7 V – 12 V input)
   - **Voltage Regulator IC (AMS1117-5.0 / NCP1117)** — regulates to **+5 V**
   - **3.3 V LDO regulator** — for shields and external modules
   - **USB Type-B Port** — supplies **+5 V** and serial data
   - **Power Pins:** `Vin`, `5V`, `3.3V`, `GND`

2. **Microcontroller Core (ATmega328P)**
   - 8-bit **AVR RISC** architecture
   - **131 instructions** (most single-cycle)
   - **16 MHz** external crystal oscillator
   - **Harvard architecture** (separate program & data buses)

3. **Memory Subsystem**
   - **Flash (32 KB):** Non-volatile program storage.
   - **SRAM (2 KB):** Runtime variables, stack, heap.
   - **EEPROM (1 KB):** Non-volatile user data (byte-level access).
   - **Bootloader (0.5 KB):** Pre-flashed `Optiboot` — allows USB upload without external programmer.

4. **I/O Subsystem**
   - **Port B (PB0–PB5):** Digital pins 8–13 (PB5 = D13 = built-in LED).
   - **Port C (PC0–PC5):** Analog pins A0–A5 (also usable as digital).
